title: Hubert Strang vom Palästinakorps auf seinem Weg nach Jerusalem und zurück nach Wilhelmshaven | Hubert Strang of Palestinecorps on his way to Jerusalem and back to Wilhelmshaven
text:
TITLE
Hubert Strang vom Palästinakorps auf seinem Weg nach Jerusalem und zurück nach Wilhelmshaven | Hubert Strang of Palestinecorps on his way to Jerusalem and back to Wilhelmshaven

METADATA
Title: Hubert Strang vom Palästinakorps auf seinem Weg nach Jerusalem und zurück nach Wilhelmshaven | Hubert Strang of Palestinecorps on his way to Jerusalem and back to Wilhelmshaven
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Jerusalem
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Hubert Strang was drafted into Berlin in 1914 and trained as a flamethrower. In 1917 he found himself in trench warfare in France. At this time he regularly wrote field postcards to his sister Christine Strang in Euskirchen-Billig. A little later he was transferred to the Balkans and later took part in the Palestine campaign. Together with the Palestine Corps he moved overland via Poland, the Balkan states (Serbia), Lebanon (Baalbek) and Syria (Aleppo, Damascus) to Palestine. There he marched through Nazareth, Bethlehem and Tiberias to Jerusalem, where the corps was received by English soldiers and informed that the war was over. Strang was transported to Constantinople with his comrades and interned in the port on a ship confiscated by the English. Later, as a British prisoner of war, he crossed the Mediterranean to Wilhelmshaven, where he was released in 1918. From this ship called "Asgard" there is a board newspaper and a nautical chart that mark the exact course from Turkey back to Germany.

RELATED MATERIALS
Extensive collection of postcards from the Palestine campaign, containing black-and-white and colored prints from Constantinople, Jerusalem and Damascus, among other places, documenting the corps' route; associated sales catalogue, the photographic art publisher Schiller & Janssen, Gütersloh i. Westf., contains the complete index of all 625 trading cards about the Palestine campaign.

title: FRAD033-090 Histoire d'Alphonse et Paul Gueytat | Documents from Alphonse et Paul Gueytat
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-090 Histoire d'Alphonse et Paul Gueytat | Documents from Alphonse et Paul Gueytat

METADATA
Title: FRAD033-090 Histoire d'Alphonse et Paul Gueytat | Documents from Alphonse et Paul Gueytat
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Alphonse Gueytat was born in Lyon in 1866. Knight of the Legion of Honor, he was battalion commander in the 63rd infantry regiment when he was killed in action at Saint-Léonard near Reims on September 26, 1914. He wrote his daily diary. Paul Gueytat, born in Orléans in 1878, was also an officer during the Great War: captain in the 13th regiment of foot hunters, he belonged to the cycling group of the 6th cavalry division when he died on the field of honor in Sarrebourg, the August 18, 1914, at age 36.

RELATED MATERIALS
Postcards and correspondence. Plan of the military cemetery. Regimental orders and sketches, notes, notebook, press article. Photographs. Double death notice.

title: FRAD017 - Histoire de la famille Lebrun | Famille Lebrun
text:
TITLE
FRAD017 - Histoire de la famille Lebrun | Famille Lebrun

Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Maurice Lebrun (grandfather of Michel Lebrun, applicant) was born on 5/03/1890 in Saulzoir, dairy employee, originally from the North, near Cambrai. He was mobilized on August 3, 1914 in the 41st Infantry Regiment, 2nd Battery. He came to Chepniers at the military hospital after being gassed on 07/26/1917 (Battle of the Yser) and again on March 18, 1918 (Battle of Fontavert) and there he met his future wife Lucette Chenoury de Polignac. He died in 1941 in Montendre. One of his brothers, Emile, was a prisoner and died in 1918 of typhus while still in captivity.

RELATED MATERIALS
A military booklet, a notebook of songs written during the war by another soldier, 1 quote after injuries (gas), a free care booklet, photographs

title: FRAD087_052-Histoire d'Antoine Malaty | Story of Antoine Malaty
text:
TITLE
FRAD087_052-Histoire d'Antoine Malaty | Story of Antoine Malaty

METADATA
Title: FRAD087_052-Histoire d'Antoine Malaty | Story of Antoine Malaty
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1919
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story told by Mr. Pierre Dudognon, grandson of Antoine Malaty. Antoine Malaty, born in Sauvagnas (Lot-et-Garonne), was a career soldier (policeman on horseback). He wrote to his wife and children during the Great War several cards that partially retrace his journey. Several of these maps represent Champagne and the North of France. Intoxicated by a mustard gas attack, he was repatriated to the rear: he was at the beginning of 1918 in Saint-Denis, in the barracks of the 110th Gendarmerie section. From December 1918, he was in Rouen, from where he sent new cards to his family. Antoine Malaty's correspondence bears witness to his concern for his wife, living with her children on her parents' farm. Little said about his living conditions, he evokes his prospects for leave, and some political events, such as the attack perpetrated against Georges Clémenceau in 1919. Antoine Malaty continued his career in the gendarmerie after the war. He was awarded the 1914-1918 Interallied Medal and the 1914-1918 War Commemorative Medal. Ernest Durand and Pierre Dudognon, respectively maternal and paternal uncles of Mr. Pierre Dudognon, also participated in the First World War.

RELATED MATERIALS
- photograph of Antoine Malaty dressed as a mounted policeman (1910) - correspondence exchanged by Antoine Malaty with his family (1915, 1918-1919) - inter-allied victory medal (with diploma) and commemorative medal of the 1914 war- 1918

title: Hermann Staratzkys Kriegstagebuch "Der Große Krieg" | War diary of Hermann Staratzky "The Great War"
text:
TITLE
Hermann Staratzkys Kriegstagebuch "Der Große Krieg" | War diary of Hermann Staratzky "The Great War"

METADATA
Title: Hermann Staratzkys Kriegstagebuch "Der Große Krieg" | War diary of Hermann Staratzky "The Great War"
Record type: TEXT
Places: Hamburg, Deutschland
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The author, then 17 years old, reports in detail in his diary how he experienced the time immediately before and after the outbreak of war in 1914. He writes about the mood at the time of the impending entry into the war and in the first days of the war, the mood among travelers, in the city, among peers, among his teachers, at the barracks gate, with his mother and also with himself the author has spent a Wandervogel holiday in the Eifel and is now alone on a hike along the Lahn. But soon he drives back to his hometown of Hamburg and reports from there. The records cover the period from July 26 to August 24, 1914. Hermann does not survive the war. He is wounded and dies on October 22, 1916.

RELATED MATERIALS
PDF document with the transcript of Hermann Staratzky's diary from July 26 to August 24, 1914.

title: ACA4640172_43_BOUDET_01 - Félix Boudet, officier et artiste | Félix Boudet, Officer and Artist
text:
TITLE
ACA4640172_43_BOUDET_01 - Félix Boudet, officier et artiste | Félix Boudet, Officer and Artist

METADATA
Title: ACA4640172_43_BOUDET_01 - Félix Boudet, officier et artiste | Félix Boudet, Officer and Artist
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Saint-Cyrien
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Félix BOUDET was my paternal grandfather, born in 1881 and from a family of officers. Saint-Cyrien, he left in 1914 as a cavalry lieutenant, in the 25th then in the 3rd Dragons) and fought brilliantly. He received 3 citations and was injured in Craonne in 1917: I still have the helmet pierced by the bullet that hit him in the head. He then returned to battle. Passionate about music and drawing, he sketched during the war years, in pencil or watercolor, the characters he met and the events he lived. He had a real talent as a portraitist and caricaturist. His brother Joseph, also from Saint-Cyr, served in the war as an artilleryman.

RELATED MATERIALS
Picture of Boudet and Colleagues and three other hand drawn pictures

title: FRBNBU-103 Georges Kohn photographe des " chevaliers du ciel " | Documents from Beilin family
text:
TITLE
FRBNBU-103 Georges Kohn photographe des " chevaliers du ciel " | Documents from Beilin family

METADATA
Title: FRBNBU-103 Georges Kohn photographe des " chevaliers du ciel " | Documents from Beilin family
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1919
Places: France
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Georges Kohn (great-great-uncle of contributor Jacques Beilin) from Central France, an architect after the war, was hired as an aviation photographer for reconnaissance missions during the conflict. This collection of 184 photographs is a reflection of the war as Georges Kohn was able to see it during his reconnaissance missions. We can see in particular combat zones, battlefields riddled with shells, Nieuport planes, Farman planes, squadron insignia, flying aces, destroyed monuments, Amiens, Craonne, the coast 108, attacks seen from the sky, the Italian zone, ground planes, military airfields, a German armored tank, etc.

RELATED MATERIALS
184 aerial and terrestrial photographs, large and small format.

title: FRAD017_110_Georges Pissard | Georges Pissard
text:
TITLE
FRAD017_110_Georges Pissard | Georges Pissard

METADATA
Title: FRAD017_110_Georges Pissard | Georges Pissard

Places: Esnes
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Georges Pissard was born on July 13, 1881 in Poursay-Garnaud (Charente-Maritime, France). He is a farmer. He was mobilized on August 1, 1914 in the 6th Infantry Regiment of Saintes. Then he was appointed adjutant on March 15, 1916 in the 206th infantry regiment, promoted to lieutenant on October 30, 1918, assigned on November 16 to the 13th native skirmishers. He received a citation on August 24, 1918 "excellent non-commissioned officer active and courageous at the front since the beginning of the campaign has always in all circumstances ensured with energy and cold blood the missions entrusted to him particularly during the attacks of July 29 and August 1 by ensuring in difficult circumstances the supply of ammunition to units in line". He received the Croix de Guerre and the Military Medal.

RELATED MATERIALS
Madame Marie-Madeline Crespeau, the great-niece of Georges Pissard, came to bring photos of the Front taken by her great-uncle, particularly in the towns of Esnes (Nord) and Auvaucourt (Verdun).

STORY / CONTEXT
Constant Brun, maternal grandfather of André Rivaud (contributor), born January 14, 1989, class 1909 went to war in August 1914 on the Belgian front. He was taken prisoner by the Germans at the very beginning of the war and was placed as a prisoner as a laborer on a farm. He was not mistreated. He returned to France at the end of the war but his exact date of return remains unknown, probably in 1919.

RELATED MATERIALS
- a double-sided photograph during his captivity in Germany (scanned in two separate documents, the front = 01, the back = 02)

title: FRAD087_007-Correspondance et carnet d'Alphonse Faure | Story of Alphonse Faure
text:
TITLE
FRAD087_007-Correspondance et carnet d'Alphonse Faure | Story of Alphonse Faure

METADATA
Title: FRAD087_007-Correspondance et carnet d'Alphonse Faure | Story of Alphonse Faure
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1919
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
History of Alphonse Faure told by his granddaughter. Born in Limoges in 1878, Alphonse Faure did not fight the whole of the war, which he started in the North of France. He was incorporated into the 89th Territorial Regiment, 4th Battalion, 14th Company. It passed in particular during the conflict in the Vosges, in Lorraine and in the Somme. During his presence at the front, Alphonse Faure sent many postcards to his wife Louise and his two daughters. The first are views of the towns and regions he passed through; as the conflict progressed, he sent more and more views depicting the destruction caused by war. He evokes in his correspondence his daily life, the cold, or the parcels sent to him by his family. It was probably his wife who put together all of these maps after the war in an album. This also brings together cards addressed to the Faure family by friends, as well as cards sent by the daughters of Alphonse Faure to their father during the conflict. Alphonse Faure also kept, from August 16, 1914 to November 26, 1915, a war diary in which he described very precisely his journeys and his daily life at the front.

RELATED MATERIALS
Album of postcards exchanged between Alphonse Faure, his family and his friends; Alphonse Faure's war diary; fighter card

title: Henri-Louis Cadot, Mémoire transmise | Henri-Louis Cadot, memory transmitted
text:
TITLE
Henri-Louis Cadot, Mémoire transmise | Henri-Louis Cadot, memory transmitted

METADATA
Title: Henri-Louis Cadot, Mémoire transmise | Henri-Louis Cadot, memory transmitted
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
It's a simple notebook, black, as big as a hand. From hand to hand, from father to son, from son to nephew, it remains preserved in our family. Henri-Louis Cadot kept a Road Book throughout his military service, from 1915 to 1919. First, we imagine this book hidden in the bottom of a pocket in violent hours, in mud, gas, fury. Its excellent state of preservation, the regular and neat writing that we discover there however suggest that it is rather a transcription of original notes taken in the field. Transcribed notes, simplified notes? What is striking is the laconic character of the story, its sobriety. We always stay in the factual. Even on the occasion of the death of his close comrades, Henri-Louis Cadot leaves nothing to guess about his state of mind and his morale. "On the 25th, Saulnier was killed", "Bergère de Quelaines was killed and buried in Fromeréville", "Fouassier was killed by a shell on the night of 3 to 4 and buried on the side of the road" is hollow, leaving it to us to imagine the rest and the unsaid. “Calm”, “Dirty corner”, “Good life”, “Very quiet area”, “We drink water from the canal”, “It is freezing very hard and nothing to eat”. Fear of disciplinary measures in the event that overly compromising remarks have been entered? Deliberate stoicism to overcome trials? Censorship after the time of transcription because the horror and the psychological trauma were such that we give up transmitting them? Several consecutive days are frequently omitted. We would deduce too hastily that nothing happened. The parallel consultation of the Diary of Marches and Operations (JMO) of the 143rd RI shows that these periods absent from the story could have been devoted to routine training. Often, however, they were those of violent clashes, in Champagne, Alsace, Flanders, Lorraine, Aisne and the Ardennes where the exhausted combatant had much more to do than take notes. Without the revelations of this JMO, one could almost read Henri-Louis Cadot's Road Book as a simple reminder of the places crossed. A route. That's where we went. What happened there is too hard to tell, it can't be said, I didn't have the time, I didn't want to, you wouldn't be able to understand. Going through these pages of the family memory, we can only feel an immense respect and as much tenderness for this little liaison agent of twenty years tossed about in the Great War by choices that are beyond him and of which he is probably unaware of the stakes. Between two fights, in Bray-Dunes, my grandfather sees the sea and "picks shells". Jean-Francois Cadot

RELATED MATERIALS
Full digitized version of the Road Book left by my paternal grandfather Henri-Louis Cadot of the 143rd RI. Photos of my grandfather and military documents.

title: Sanitätsmaterial, Auszeichnungen | Der Sanitätsfeldwebel Johannes Kühn an der Westfront

Sanitätsmaterial, Auszeichnungen | Der Sanitätsfeldwebel Johannes Kühn an der Westfront

Places: Westfront Amiens, Frankreich
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My father volunteered for the war in 1914 before he graduated from high school. It was first used on the Eastern Front and later on the Western Front, including Verdun, Flanders and the Amiens area. His last rank was medical sergeant. In 1918 he was released. My father said that, among other things, he was staying with a doctor near Amiens, for whom he also made house calls in emergencies. His private syringe set with its contents dates from this period.

RELATED MATERIALS
Syringe set of a medical sergeant and medal bar

title: FRAD067-123/2 Second album de Nicolas Hiessler (1884-1952), infirmier en Flandre et en Artois (armée allemande) | Album from Nicolas Hiessler, with photographs from the Western Front
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-123/2 Second album de Nicolas Hiessler (1884-1952), infirmier en Flandre et en Artois (armée allemande) | Album from Nicolas Hiessler, with photographs from the Western Front

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-123/2 Second album de Nicolas Hiessler (1884-1952), infirmier en Flandre et en Artois (armée allemande) | Album from Nicolas Hiessler, with photographs from the Western Front
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
It is in memory of Miss Valentine Hiessler (born in Oberbronn on May 31, 1914, deceased), that she was still going to visit in 2006 at the retirement home of Haguenau, which Mrs. Teresa Weller entrusted to the Departmental Archives of Bas- Rhine two photographic albums from the father of this acquaintance, Constable Nicolas Hiessler (31/10/1884-13/05/1952). Consultation of this man's individual Veteran's file (Arch. dep. Bas-Rhin, call number 844 D 165) makes it possible to retrace his career and corroborate dates and places captioning his war photographs. Private, non-commissioned officer (03/28/1915), then nurse-sergeant (01/20/1918), Nicolas Hiessler served from August 3, 1914 to November 22, 1918, essentially (from October 31, 1914) in the Bayerische Sanitäts Kraftwagen Abteilung 6. As such he took part in position combat in Flanders and Artois from October 14, 1915 to June 23, 1916 and from January 1, 1917 to April 8, 1918 - services which he still in 1946, difficulties in asserting as justifying his rights to retirement as a combatant. He also took part in the fighting to defend the border in Lorraine (08-19/08/1914, within the 42. inf. Div. (XXI AK)), in the battles near La Bassée, Arras (09/05 -23/07 and 25/09-13/10/1915) and Armentières (09-18/04/1918); he ended the war on the western front, in Flanders and still in Artois (19/04-06/09/1918). Holder of the “Mil. Fuhrerschein 3 b”, Nicolas Hiessler was decorated twice: Bay. mil. Green. Kreuz 3. Kl. md Kr. u. Schw. (27/11/1916); Prof. Eis. Kreuz 2. Klasse (07/10/1918). The two photographic albums he kept from the war attest to his passage, between 1916 and 1918, in Brussels, Valenciennes, Anzin, Arras, Hénin-Liétard (today Hénin-Beaumont), Croizilles, Hunes, Winkles, Loos-en -Gohelle, Lens, Loison-sous-Lens, Pont-à-Vendin, Vimy, Douchy-les-Mines, Mouchy, Roubaix, Ostend, Tournai, Bailleul, Herlies, Raismes… See also contribution FRAD067-123/1.

RELATED MATERIALS
2 - Photographic album, approx. 28 x 21cm, 1916-1918

title: FRAD071-002 Claude BERNARD, du front à l'hôpital | Documents from Claude Bernard
text:
TITLE
FRAD071-002 Claude BERNARD, du front à l'hôpital | Documents from Claude Bernard

METADATA
Title: FRAD071-002 Claude BERNARD, du front à l'hôpital | Documents from Claude Bernard
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story by Jacqueline GONZALES about Claude BERNARD, her paternal grandfather, who died before she was born. It was on August 2, 1914, the first day of general mobilization in France, that Claude BERNARD celebrated his 28th birthday in his commune of Pierrefitte-sur-Loire (Allier). He went to the front at the beginning of the war, he was wounded there for the first time at the beginning of 1917; he was then treated at the Compiègne hospital and then at Le Mans (Sarthe). When he left the hospital, he joined the 86th infantry regiment at Le Puy. Wounded again in August 1918 at Tronquoy in the Somme, he was evacuated to Parthenay (Sarthe) where he remained until after the armistice. From this hospital, he wrote to his family. He is eager to join his wife and his baby born on April 8, 1918, who will be the father of Jacqueline Gonzales.

RELATED MATERIALS
Photographs, medals, diploma of honor of the war of 1914-1918.

title: FRAD067-120 Regrets d'un membre du peloton d'exécution, Villers-en-Haye (Meurthe-et-Moselle), 7 février 1915 | Postcard about an execution

FRAD067-120 Regrets d'un membre du peloton d'exécution, Villers-en-Haye (Meurthe-et-Moselle), 7 février 1915 | Postcard about an execution

Title: FRAD067-120 Regrets d'un membre du peloton d'exécution, Villers-en-Haye (Meurthe-et-Moselle), 7 février 1915 | Postcard about an execution
Record type: TEXT

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Taken from a rich collection of patriotic postcards, an anonymous card attracts attention by its content more than by its motif. It bears the text, unsigned: "Villers-en-Haye, Meurthe-et-Moselle, February 7, 1915. Today I have a moment to let you know some news from the country, which is not the most brilliant; but, what do you want, we have hope, we are pushing back the Boches day by day; yesterday we just made a deal that did not suit me – when you are ordered, you have to do it – we shot a married man from the 100th who had deliberately injured himself on October 27; he passed the court martial on the 5th, condemned to death, shot by the 50th, the poor devil did not suffer; that the same thing does not happen again, I will do my best not to find myself there.

title: FRAD067-122 Auguste Gresset annonce à ses proches la mort à son poste de combat du Franc-Comtois Alexis Antoine Billot, le 4 juillet 1915 | About the death of Alexis Antoine Billot
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-122 Auguste Gresset annonce à ses proches la mort à son poste de combat du Franc-Comtois Alexis Antoine Billot, le 4 juillet 1915 | About the death of Alexis Antoine Billot

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-122 Auguste Gresset annonce à ses proches la mort à son poste de combat du Franc-Comtois Alexis Antoine Billot, le 4 juillet 1915 | About the death of Alexis Antoine Billot
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
From father to son, the first name of Alexis was transmitted, in homage to Alexis Antoine Billot (1871-1915), gunner serving and/or stretcher bearer at the 52nd battery of the 2nd Field Artillery Regiment, mortally wounded, dead for France at Bois-le-Prêtre (Meurthe-et-Moselle) on July 4, 1915. A few pieces found while emptying the family home in Conlièges (Jura) show, in a particularly moving way, how his comrade Auguste Gresset is discharged of his duty with regard to the deceased, by taking care of his burial, having the family informed and recording the exact circumstances of the disastrous event.

RELATED MATERIALS
3 letters from Auguste Gresset to Pierre Dubost, relation of Mme Billot to Lons-le-Saunier (Jura), 1915; 2 photographs of the tomb of Alexis Antoine Billot in Montauville (Meurthe-et-Moselle), sd, 1915; 1 postcard from the Montauville military cemetery (Meurthe-et-Moselle), sd, 1915; 2 military citations, 1919 and 1920

title: FRAD033-080 Histoire de René, Laurent Hueber | Documents from René, Laurent Hueber
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-080 Histoire de René, Laurent Hueber | Documents from René, Laurent Hueber

METADATA
Title: FRAD033-080 Histoire de René, Laurent Hueber | Documents from René, Laurent Hueber
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Military booklet. Photographs: photograph representing a family meal ["souvenir of the baptism of Nénette on August 15, 1913"; René Hueber recognizable by his cap, left, in the foreground]; 7 pictures representing René Hueber; 6 pictures representing Louis Vincenot, brother-in-law of René Hueber, husband of Eugénie Hueber; photo showing the crash of a German plane shot down at Braisne near Soissons (May 25, 1915); 4 pictures representing friends. Miscellaneous documents: two cards from the "Serrez-les-Rangs" gymnastics society in Saint-Maur (1914); letter from Louis Vincenot, brother-in-law of René Hueber, auto mechanic of the 1st group, sector 22 (March 18, 1917); letter from his parents (April 14, 1917); handwritten song entitled "A Robinson" (Robinson is undoubtedly a party place, a guinguette).

RELATED MATERIALS
René, Laurent Hueber was born on May 11, 1896 in Saint-Maur-des-Fossés. Mobilized against Germany on April 13, 1915, he belonged to the 4th section of the 9th company of the 59th infantry regiment. He died on April 18, 1917 in Sept-Saulx (Marne).

title: FRAD033-084 Histoire de Joseph Piquemal | Documents from Joseph Piquemal
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-084 Histoire de Joseph Piquemal | Documents from Joseph Piquemal

METADATA
Title: FRAD033-084 Histoire de Joseph Piquemal | Documents from Joseph Piquemal
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Joseph Piquemal was born in Foix on April 2, 1891. Son of Jean Piquemal and Françoise Blazy, Joseph was a farmer in Foix when he joined the 3rd battalion of the 83rd infantry regiment on August 2, 1914. On August 8, he arrived at Valmy where he was placed under the orders of Commander Chevassu who was killed the following December 9 while giving his orders to the 12th company to repel the attackers. Shot in the arm at the end of August-beginning of September 1914, Joseph Piquemal was hospitalized for six months in Pont-L'Abbé (Finistère). After multiple complications, Joseph Piquemal requests, on November 5, 1926, a summons before the reform commission of Toulouse to have his disability noted.

RELATED MATERIALS
Military booklet. Correspondence: army correspondence card addressed to Joseph Piquemal (November 11, 1914); letter from Commandant Chevassu's wife addressed to Joseph Piquemal (October 9, 1914). Miscellaneous documents: card of the Ariège Association of disabled, reformed, widows, orphans, ascendants and veterans of the Great War (1923); diploma of the so-called “victory” inter-allied medal; medical certificate explaining and attesting to the injury received during the war and its consequences (November 5, 1926).

title: FRAD077-141B, Charles-Philippe Françon du 133e Régiment d'Infanterie | Charles-Philippe Françon, of 133e Infantery Regiment.
text:
TITLE
FRAD077-141B, Charles-Philippe Françon du 133e Régiment d'Infanterie | Charles-Philippe Françon, of 133e Infantery Regiment.

METADATA
Title: FRAD077-141B, Charles-Philippe Françon du 133e Régiment d'Infanterie | Charles-Philippe Françon, of 133e Infantery Regiment.
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1917
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Charles-Philippe Françon, maternal grandfather of the contributor, was from the class of 1902, registration number 1082. He served in the 133rd Infantry Regiment stationed in Belley (France, Ain). In working life, he was a hand weaver converted into a weaver of special very fine metal fabrics (head of production in metal fabrics). During World War I, he served in the campaign against Germany, Austria-Hungary and Bulgaria from August 2, 1914 to June 11, 1917. He went into auxiliary service on January 18, 1917, after being gassed. He then suffered from “emphysema attributable to the forehead”. In April 1917, he was transferred to the 53rd artilleryman, 68th battery, as 2nd gunner, he was a gunner-driver there. In 1917, he was assigned to the Mulatier son Dupont factory in Melun (establishment working for National Defense). Charles-Philippe Françon recounted having participated in revolt movements in 1917 in Bulgaria, protesting against the harshness of the war, the lack of food. He recounted having taken part in the capture of the food reserves of the Bulgarian army and in particular of a sampote (barrel) of wine (at Demir-Kapu, in present-day Macedonia). After the war, he settled in Dammarie-lès-Lys (France, Seine-et-Marne) then in Melun as a weaver, a trade he practiced until the end of his life. He was also a foreman for the Festa Company which made party favors, masks and articles for parties and which operated a workshop at the central prison of Melun (France, Seine-et-Marne).

RELATED MATERIALS
1- Military booklet, 2- Savings book.

STORY / CONTEXT
Story of Jean Collet told by his grandson. Jean Collet was a sharecropper in Saint-Auvent (Haute-Vienne), a profession he continued to exercise after the Great War. Little is known of his career during the war, except that he belonged to the 89th territorial infantry regiment and that he fought throughout the war, especially around Verdun. He received the 1914-1918 War Medal and the Inter-Allied Victory Medal.

RELATED MATERIALS
Commemorative diploma of the First World War to which are attached the 1914-1918 war medal and the inter-allied victory medal awarded to Jean Collet.

title: FRAN-PA-206 Deux morts dans le Bois de la Gruerie | Photographs from Leleu and Marousez
text:
TITLE
FRAN-PA-206 Deux morts dans le Bois de la Gruerie | Photographs from Leleu and Marousez

METADATA
Title: FRAN-PA-206 Deux morts dans le Bois de la Gruerie | Photographs from Leleu and Marousez
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1915
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The Leleu-Marousez family was hard hit by the war, since two of its members died in quick succession on the forehead. In October 1914, Paul Leleu, a cook from the North, died in combat in the Bois de la Gruerie (Marne): he belonged to the 147th infantry regiment. Terrible coincidence, his brother-in-law, Auguste Marousez, who had married Clara Leleu (Paul's sister), died a year later in 1915 in the same Bois de la Gruerie. He was a brewer in Verchain-Maugré (59): his wife succumbed to grief shortly after and their two daughters were therefore raised by their grandmother, a nurse in Artres (59). Auguste Marousez, the day before his death, had found his own brother on the front, Ferdinand (who was to be one of the few in the family to return alive from the fighting): he spent his last night with him.

RELATED MATERIALS
Black and white photographs of Paul Leleu, Clara Leleu and her two daughters and Auguste Marousez, sd

title: FRAD087_056-Histoire d'Ernest Rolle-Milaguet | Story of Ernest Rolle-Milaguet
text:
TITLE
FRAD087_056-Histoire d'Ernest Rolle-Milaguet | Story of Ernest Rolle-Milaguet

METADATA
Title: FRAD087_056-Histoire d'Ernest Rolle-Milaguet | Story of Ernest Rolle-Milaguet
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story of Ernest Rolle-Milaguet told by his grandson. The son of a carpenter and a cook, Ernest Rolle-Milaguet learned the trade of carpenter-joiner. He had several brothers. He was mobilized on February 24, probably in the year 1916. He was part of a regiment of sappers. In his travel diary, he noted all his movements until September 10, 1919, the date on which he was sent to Sarrelouis, in the Rhineland. During the conflict, he passed successively through Picardy, Champagne, the Paris region and the Verdun region. He was not injured during the war. He obtained the Croix de Guerre on April 14, 1918 and received it on May 4 of the same year. He was also mentioned in the orders of the regiment on June 1, 1918 for his "courage and coolness in the execution of a supply duty under the bombardment". On his return from the war, he married and settled in Bussière-Poitevine (Haute-Vienne), where his wife had opened a restaurant. Because of his experience as a sapper, he also acted, for many years, as the municipal artificer for the July 14 ceremonies.

RELATED MATERIALS
Ernest Rolle-Milaguet's travel diary (February 1916-September 1919); copy of a photograph by Ernest Rolle-Milaguet; citation at the orders of the regiment (1918)

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra

STORY / CONTEXT
Maternal grandfather of Jocelyne Waserscztajn (contributor), Camille Victor Belmon born October 2 or 10, 1888, class 1908, in Domballe in the Meuse had just married in 1913 when he went to war. He was a corporal. He attended a mass in the middle of the Argonne forest at La Hazarée in January 1917 and the photo of this ceremony has been kept in the family. Left alone during the war, his wife going to see her parents in the Marne was very close to the front lines and distributed, with her parents, hot drinks to the soldiers on the way. If they didn't have a quarter to drink, they gave them the drink in their helmet. Camille Victor Belmon emerged unscathed from the war and died long after, Jocelyne Waserscztajn (contributor) having known her until the age of 24.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photo of a mass in the Argonne forest in 1917 - photos with his regiment - cardboard with pasted photos - cardboard with pasted photo

title: FRBNBU-110 François Vendel "ma guerre", une blessure inguérissable | Francois Vendel's war, an incurable injury and wound
text:
TITLE
FRBNBU-110 François Vendel "ma guerre", une blessure inguérissable | Francois Vendel's war, an incurable injury and wound

METADATA
Title: FRBNBU-110 François Vendel "ma guerre", une blessure inguérissable | Francois Vendel's war, an incurable injury and wound
Record type: TEXT
Places: Belfort Meuse Marne Verdun
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Born on October 10, 1888, class 1908, François Léon Vendel, paternal grandfather of Claude Vendel (contributor) was mobilized at Bosmont in the 133rd RI, equipped on August 2, 1914 at 2:00 p.m. He lived in Paris, which he had left on Saturday August 1, 1914 at 1:00 p.m. to go to Belfort to his family. He then leaves for Aillevillers at 9:00 in the evening. On August 10 he was sent to Cravanche near Belfort in the 26th company. On September 4, 1914, he was on guard at Belfort station when 2 bombs were dropped by a German airman, without result. On October 13, 1914 he was sent to Commercy then to Pont-sur-Meuse and Marlotte (line of fire) and Croix-Saint-Jean in the Meuse. He was appointed sergeant on November 7, 1914. He joined the 172nd Infantry Regiment, 13th Company, in which he was in May 1915 (perhaps before this date). In May 1915 he suffered violent bombardments towards the Bois d'Ailly north of Commercy (Meuse), was often in the front line, experienced the bombardments, shootings, occupations of trenches, the entourage of corpses and the development of infections . In June 1915 he left for the Verdun region where he again found himself on the front line. He knows there the explosions of mines, the bombardments of shells, torpedoes and bottles. In August 1915, a review of his regiment was held by a British general and General Joffre. At the end of September 1915 he found himself near Suippes in the Marne where the enemy was on the run and he crossed the first enemy line in which there were a large number of dead. On September 26, 1915, around 6 p.m., he was seriously injured, his leg broken or even torn apart by shrapnel. Relieved on September 27, 1915, he was evacuated to Suippes, then sent to Paris on September 28, where he arrived on September 29 at 7 a.m. He was dressed in the ambulance at the Chapelle station and taken to the Lariboisière hospital for urgent surgery. He was operated on September 29 in the afternoon then re-operated for bone scraping a second time on December 8, 1915 (very painful operation). He was operated on again a third time on January 2, 1916 and triggered a major haemorrhage, then a fourth time on January 13, 1916 and a fifth time on January 22, 1916 to perform an arterial suture, followed again by a very major haemorrhage. On February 24, 1916 he was evacuated to Enghein. He was operated on again for a sixth time on April 2, 1916. During this operation, a fragment of bone was removed from him. He was in plaster on April 25, 1916. He was re-educated to walk on April 27, 1916. Deplastered on May 27, 1916, his blood no longer circulated in his leg. He declares an open abscess on June 8 and is reoperated for the seventh time on June 22, 1916 to again remove 4 bone splinters. He left Enghein on November 20, 1916 for Clignancourt and was discharged on December 19, 1916 after 15 months of multiple operations and intense suffering. All the rest of his life, his wounds oozed, he survived but 40 years later it was still necessary to treat his wound daily. Despite all his sufferings he remained cheerful for his family. But his wife said "I've shed too many tears to have more to shed today".

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photo of François Vendel - War diary of François Vendel - Health report of François Vendel - Photo of the 13th Ci of the 172nd RI - Score of the patriotic song "La Française"

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Pierre Dudoy (paternal grandfather of Laurence Pedretti, the contributor) was born in 1885 in Barcus in the Basque country. In the Basque country, the right of primogeniture was still exercised at the beginning of the 20th century: tradition dictated that the first child be the universal heir, that the second enter religious orders and that the third emigrate. Pierre Dudoy therefore moved to Algeria where he married in 1910. It was in Constantine that he did his military service and then he went to the officers' school in Cherchell, about a hundred kilometers from Algiers. In 1914, he was mobilized in Constantine; in 1915 he was second lieutenant and in 1916 he was sent to France with the Algerian skirmishers of the 7th regiment. In 1917, he was injured: a bullet near the heart which could not be extracted. He therefore remained in France where his wife joined him. Mr. and Mrs. Dudoy will settle in the South until 1923 before leaving for Ile-de-France. Pierre Dudoy died on June 20, 1940.

title: FRAD033-054 Histoire d’Edouard Dubart | Documents from Edouard Dubart
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-054 Histoire d’Edouard Dubart | Documents from Edouard Dubart

METADATA
Title: FRAD033-054 Histoire d’Edouard Dubart | Documents from Edouard Dubart
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, Slovenčina, slk, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Edouard Dubart was born on March 16, 1878 in Marcq-en-Baroeul (Nord). A dental surgeon, he was 36 years old in 1914. He joined the army as an infantryman and refused to be incorporated into the health services. Prisoner in 1914 in the Munster camp in Germany, he escaped in 1917 after being informed of the evacuation by the Red Cross of his wife and 2 children. Knowing that his family was outside Germany, he decided to escape via Holland and then England accompanied by two prisoners. To end his journey in Valence where he joined the Health Service. During his detention, he shared his parcels with Russian prisoners. In gratitude, they offered him a belt buckle with the effigy of the Tsar and an icon. During his internment, he carves two pencil boxes for his daughters. He died in 1948.

RELATED MATERIALS
Account of Edouard Dubart's escape with its transcription; a map of the escape route; correspondence with his wife (1916-1917); a bust portrait of Edouart Dubart during his military service in 1898; a photograph of Edouart Dubart, his wife and two daughters in Valence; two photographs of groups in the camp; an escaped medal certificate (August 18, 1927).

title: AMSM-002_2 : Correspondance à mon grand oncle Auguste PIERREQUIN | correspondence of my grand uncle Auguste PIERREQUIN
text:
TITLE
AMSM-002_2 : Correspondance à mon grand oncle Auguste PIERREQUIN | correspondence of my grand uncle Auguste PIERREQUIN

METADATA
Title: AMSM-002_2 : Correspondance à mon grand oncle Auguste PIERREQUIN | correspondence of my grand uncle Auguste PIERREQUIN
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
After buying the house of my uncle, Auguste PIERREQUIN, I found this correspondence addressed to his parents, Alphonse and Hortense, sent by a cousin, Edmond BIGOUDOT The latter went to war, on the western front and was wounded in combat on September 12, 1918. In a first letter of August 31, 1916, he was worried about the bombardments suffered by the family and mentioned his life, particularly behind the front (relations with officers, food, vaccination). He also gives his feelings about Romania's recent involvement in the fighting. The rest of the correspondence consists of postcards. The first was sent on September 5, 1919, in which he evoked his convalescence in Moret. The particularity is that it was he himself, during his free time, who drew this postcard in the name of great uncle Alphonse. The second, dated September 17, 1919, is accompanied by 3 other postcards which have remained blank, on which are inscribed the names of the other men of the family.

title: Aquarelle vom Dorjan-See in Mazedonien | Aquarelles from Macedonia
text:
TITLE

Record type: TEXT

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Franz Schilling's family comes from Schmiedeberg in Thuringia. Later he went to Magdeburg. Schilling was a weapons master and served on the western front during the 1914/15 war and took part in the French campaign (Furigny, February 12, 1915). From 1916 at the latest he was transferred to the Balkans. In the family it was often said that "grandfather was in the bivouac in Macedonia". He was also unable to eat rice because there was almost nothing else to eat on site. He brought back watercolor drawings from the Balkans, which were found in the family's apartment in Berlin after the Second World War. Franz Schilling's son Erich Schilling (February 2, 1900 - June 3, 1984) was taken out of school in 1917 and drafted into the army. He survived the war unscathed. Franz Schilling had two other sons, Willi and Kurt. Willi died before the First World War as a result of a hornet bite.

RELATED MATERIALS
The watercolors were painted by Joh. Siebert and show: (1) the Paljorka Monastery in South Macedonia (1916), (2) Lake Dojran (1916) and (3) a weapons workshop in the bivouac in South Macedonia (11.7.1916) .

STORY / CONTEXT
In the photo of the foal: My father, Frans Van Sante (Wetteren 1899) was the youngest of five children. He was 53 years old when I was born. Father's eldest brother, August, (born Wetteren 1894) was also called to war in August 1914. Meanwhile, my father, Frans, then just 14 years old, had to run the farm with mother and three sisters. Very often I heard him tell with pride that 'the German' had not found their horse after all, because he always managed to deceive them with some excuse. And when this horse had a foal, the photographer was brought in to capture the whole family with mare and foal on the sensitive plate! This photo was then smuggled to the front by two sisters. In the meantime, however, it had been learned that brother Gust was injured in the hospital in Dunkirk. The journey of the sisters has also been told to me, but unfortunately I have little or no memories of it, except that it was accompanied by a lot of fright. But they were very proud that they had found brother Gust after much searching and asking! Items: add photos In the photo: Mother Sidonia, sitting, around it the three sisters, on the left of the photo you see the neighbor with the mare and next to it a proud Frans with the foal.

RELATED MATERIALS
I am in possession of the photo, which shows the family and the mare with the foal (probably taken around 1916-17?)

title: FRAD067-119 Cartes reçues de Wilhelm Keller (1873-1920) (armée allemande) par son épouse Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955), à Bischheim (Bas-Rhin) | Postcards from Wilhelm Keller
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-119 Cartes reçues de Wilhelm Keller (1873-1920) (armée allemande) par son épouse Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955), à Bischheim (Bas-Rhin) | Postcards from Wilhelm Keller

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-119 Cartes reçues de Wilhelm Keller (1873-1920) (armée allemande) par son épouse Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955), à Bischheim (Bas-Rhin) | Postcards from Wilhelm Keller
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Plasterer, married in 1904 to Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955) and father of two children, Alfred Wilhelm (1907-1991) and Magdalena Salomea (1905-1985), Mme Keller-Gatty's grandfather, Wilhelm Keller (1873- 1920), died as a result of the war. In the absence of an individual veteran's file in the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin, or of personal military papers, the cards he sent to his wife from 1914 to 1917 are perhaps the only source making it possible to trace his eventful journey, across the whole of Europe, during his war years. W. Keller (photograph at the top of the series of images) was first engaged on the Western front, in Belgium: he wrote from Enghien (26/11, 14 and 19/12/1914; 12/01, 11 /02 and 06/04/1915) and occasionally from Jemappes (08/12/1914). He continued the war on the eastern front, between Poland and Hungary: he dated his 4 successive maps of May 1915 from Kreuzburg, in Upper Silesia (today Kluczbork, Poland), "Hungary", "Galicia" (today Poland/ Ukraine) and Budapest. He then reports to 1. Kompagnie 1. Landsturm Infanterie Ersatz Bataillon Straßburg, XV AK On 08/14/1915, he is in Russia, at Christmas 1915, in Silesia (now Poland), in [Bielsko]-Biala, visibly in military hospital. W. Keller returned to Alsace at the beginning of 1916: his maps of 15/02 and 14/04/1916 are dated from the military camp of Oberhoffen-sur-Moder, near Haguenau, or 05/06/1916 from Karlsruhe (with mention of parking in Oberhoffen). However, he writes on 04/20/1916 from Wlodawa (Poland) and on 06/11/1916 from Parchim (Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania). On this last date, he belongs to the 2. Kompagnie 4. Landsturm Infanterie Ersatz Battalion XV AK 8. Such mobility seems to be explained by the fact that he was assigned to the "Zug Armee" (mention appearing on several maps). An important ellipse no longer allows us to follow his route so precisely after the spring of 1916: the last of the 20 maps that have come down to us – including one undated [1915?], unlocated [Bielsko-Biala?], incomplete [2nd part of a correspondence in 2 cards], placed at the back – is dated from Saarbrücken, 08/09/1917. W. Keller is stationed there injured or ill, giving the address “Res. Lazareth 1. Saarbrücken, Heilstätte Sonnenberg, Zimmer 2”. The front of the cards offers views of Enghien (civil hospital), Brussels (Porte de Hal, Houses of Tailors and Victor Hugo, Royal Theater of La Monnaie), La Hulpe (Castle of Nieuwenhuys), Nieuport (Bridge of the Cup Gorge), Vereshegy (Hungary), the Tatra chain (Poland/Slovakia), Budapest, Oberhoffen-sur-Moder (views of the military camp), Wlodawa, Karlsruhe, Parchim in Mecklenburg. Three other cards (rejected after those of W. Keller), sent by military post to the Keller family in Bischheim, provide specific information on relatives or acquaintances, Georg Daab (21/02/1916, prisoner in Saint-Rambert- sur-Loire (Allier)), Alfred Elgass (17/08/1916, at Res. Laz. III in Hanau am Main (Hesse)) and Konrad Oehl (06/02/1917, 3. Kompagnie 1. Ersatz Regiment 136 at Strasbourg).

RELATED MATERIALS
24 postcards (all parts preserved)

title: FRBMLY-60 Histoire de Fortuné Avid, sauvé par son livret militaire | Documents from Jean-Claude Avid

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Fortuné AVID was a student in Normandy, incorporated into the 23rd Colonial Infantry Regiment. He was from the 1916 class but was drafted in 1915, at the age of 19. He was wounded twice: at Chemin des Dames, in Vauxaillon in the Aisne, at night, he was shot at point-blank range by the enemy on April 20, 1917. This bullet had the route derived by his military booklet which was in his inside coat pocket. He was evacuated to the Laval hospital in Mayenne where the bullet was extracted from him without being asleep. He was saved because the bullet did not hit his liver. Despite this injury, he returned to the front in 1918 at Fort de la Pompelle (the only fort in the hands of the French who were defending the city of Reims). They put chalk on their helmets to blend in with the landscape. He and his entire team were gassed. The gassed soldiers were evacuated to the cellars of Champagne. After the war, he was part of the French soldiers who occupied the Ruhr. He was in Mainz in June 1919. He received several military decorations.

RELATED MATERIALS
Military booklet, photo, cubit card

The contributors' grandfather and father both served in the 1914-1918 war. Grandfather Théophile Guillen, from the Villefort region, was mobilized in 1914. Upholsterer by profession and very sensitive to a job well done, he brought back from the front objects made in casings and shell heads for his two sons. , Alfred and René: candle holders, lighters, inkwells, letter openers... His eldest son, Alfred, father of the contributors, volunteered for four years in January 1918 (he belonged to class 1919) and was incorporated into the 5th Engineer Regiment (52nd battalion). He was recalled in 1940 to the gunpowder factory of Sorgues.

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean Noyer was mobilized in August 1914, at the age of 25, in the 22nd BCA (Alberville), sent to the Vosges. At the start of the campaign, he crossed the border with his regiment, passed through Alsace, went as far as the suburbs of Colmar. Jean was the fourth in a family of eight children, including four boys who all fought in the war. His older brother Clément joined him at 22° BCA in early September 1914 and was killed alongside him on November 19, 1914. His two other brothers, Antoine, an alpine hunter, and Léon, a stretcher bearer, came out of the storm alive. From the handwritten notebook drawn up by Jean Noyer, I myself carried out his Alsatian journey between August 1914 and March 1915.

RELATED MATERIALS
A photograph. A military booklet. the handwritten booklet. A frame.

STORY / CONTEXT
Madame Nicole Robine bears witness to the memory of her father-in-law Alphonse Robine, a fighter in the Great War. He was in the signals and had on him, during the war, a sketchbook on which he drew daily. He took over this notebook after the war to turn it into a remarkable watercolor album. His album bears witness to the different faces of war. Daily life in the trenches, images of the council of war, Easter ceremony... He wrote to his wife every day. He was a lawyer in Cherbourg.

Main document. Selected extracts from a 164-page watercolor album covering the period 1915-1917.

title: FRBMLY-65 Mon grand-père ce héros | Documents from Chantal Danel
text:
TITLE
FRBMLY-65 Mon grand-père ce héros | Documents from Chantal Danel

METADATA
Title: FRBMLY-65 Mon grand-père ce héros | Documents from Chantal Danel
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Pas-de-Calais
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My grandfather Nestor Tholliez was born on September 22, 1890, in Rieux-Lillers in a small town in Pas-de-Calais. In 1910, he was incorporated for two years in order to perform his military service. On July 31, 1914, like so many other young people of his age, he was called upon to defend his country. He was assigned to the 56th battalion placed under the command of Colonel DRIAND. In 1917, I do not know as a result of what combination of circumstances, he was promoted to stretcher-bearer and remained so for the duration of the war. During this conflict, he was awarded five citations for bravery, he was wounded twice. Returning to his homes, he found his land and married Célinie, my grandmother. They remained childless for seven years to their great despair. Then came Paul in 1926, Gérard in 1928, Marie-Thérèse, my mother in 1930 then Maurice in 1932. He had made a wish to build a chapel if he returned from the war. He kept his promise by having it erected in 1932, it bears the name of Notre-Dame des Victoires. He got involved in the life of his village by participating in the revival of the fanfare called l'Indépendante where he was himself a musician and became an active member of the "rescuers of Rieux", a kind of volunteer firefighters before the letter. In 1954 he was awarded the Legion of Honor. This decoration was his pride. Towards the end of his life he often spoke to us of his war, of his friends whom he had seen struck down by German shells or bullets, in full youth. He died one fine day in June 1976, surrounded by his children and grandchildren...

STORY / CONTEXT
Henri Mermin and Marie-Louise are from the same village in Haute-Savoie. Henri did his military service in Bizerte (Tunisia) in the 3rd Zouaves Regiment, then in 1913 he returned to France and worked at the Hôtel Moderne in Paris. He corresponded with Marie-Louise until March when he returned to his village, then they married in May 1914. He was called up in August 1914, and continued to correspond with his family and his wife, who was pregnant at the time. of their 1st child who was born on March 5, 1915. Henri Mermin was killed in November 1914 in battle at Tracy le Val.

RELATED MATERIALS
Correspondence between Henri, his wife and his family, photograph of the wedding of Henri and Marie-Louise and official telegram announcing his death.

Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Wilhelm Sandvoß came from Segeste in the district of Alfeld. During the war he served in the 10th company of the Reserve Infantry Regiment 249, which was deployed on the Eastern Front and in the last year of the war also on the Western Front. The surviving photos testify to his participation in the Battle of Kovel, trench warfare between Krevo-Smorgon-Noroch-Tverech and the Battle of Riga. In his letter to his relatives in February 1916, he reports on the everyday hardships, for example with regard to food or life in the trenches. The last photo dates from May 1918 in Montdidier on the Somme. Nothing else is known about his life story after the war.

RELATED MATERIALS
War photos of Wilhelm Sandvoß from his deployment on the Eastern Front and a letter to his family dated February 25, 1916.

STORY / CONTEXT
Hermann Stegmayer (born 1891, died 1918) was the contestant's mother's favorite uncle. She collected his letters from him and compiled a photo album of pictures to commemorate him. He was first deployed in France in the Vosges, then came to Galicia (today: Poland / Ukraine), after the December 1917 armistice with Russia, he was back in France where he died. She commented on the album by hand, for example with the entries: "To commemorate my unforgettable uncle Hermann, who died a heroic death on Easter Monday, April 1, 1918 / Offenburg 1914 / for recovery at home in May (?) 1914 / memory to the 3rd I R. 84 / April 15, 1915 Vosges / September 1915 Tête du Violu / Vaccination against typhus May 1916 / Fort Witry, May 1917 / Galicia June 1917 / Kociubincziki (eastern Galicia) – September 21, 1917 / With Russians (at) Armistice in East Galicia December 1917 / Laon March 1918 / Hermann's grave April 3, 1918" / Also included in the album: Death notice: Lieutenant Hermann Stegmayer, leader of the 5th company of an infantry regiment, Iron Cross 1st and 2nd class , and the Zähringer lion with swords / detailed newspaper report about his wounding on March 25th and his death / he died near Crécy s. Serre as Lt. i.e. R. 5./ Replace IR 29.

STORY / CONTEXT
My paternal great-grandfather Louis Cavil, born April 9, 1879 in Penquesten Inzinzac-Lochrist (Morbihan) was a blacksmith. He was mobilized in the 6th Engineer Regiment Company 11/1. Gassed during the conflict, he died of his injuries in 1922. Following his injuries, he asked to work at the Kerglaw war factory at the Forges de Lochrist. He was deputy special mayor for Penquesten in the municipality of Inzinzac-Lochrist.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My maternal grandfather, Emile Woirgard, born in 1894, 24 years after the war of 1870, is from Lorraine and is therefore mobilized on the German side during the war of 1914. He is incorporated into the 217th infantry regiment in Germany. He writes to his family in German. He went to war with German nationality, he returned from the war in territory that had become French again and therefore with French nationality. In the meantime he had fought for Germany against Russia. On his return he always spoke to his family in French. In Lorraine, he lived near Remilly in Moselle. He would have been taken prisoner by the Russians during the war, but none of the documents presented bear any trace of it. During the war he suffered from severe pneumonia but managed to recover. He knew his wife, my grandmother, before the war and could not marry her until after the war in 1924.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photo of Emile Woirgard in front of an artillery piece. - Verso of a photo: correspondence from the family of Emile Woirgard indicating his regiment - Gravestone map sent to his parents - Map showing the characteristics of a camp near a railway line.

title: FRBNBU-106 Histoire de Joseph Auguste Chenavier, grand-père de Guy Chevenet | Joseph Auguste Chenavier's story
text:
TITLE

Subjects: World War I

Provider: Europeana 1914-1918

STORY / CONTEXT
Joseph Auguste Chenavier, the maternal grandfather of Guy Chevenet, was born on March 8, 1882 in Gillonnais (38, Isère). A farrier by profession, he worked as an agricultural contractor. He was mobilized in 1914 in Vienne (Isère), private 2nd class in the 99th Infantry Regiment (RI). He made the campaigns of the Vosges, the Somme, the Aisne and Verdun. He was killed on April 23, 1916 in front of the Thiaumont farm, near Verdun. He left a widow and two children aged 6 and 2.

RELATED MATERIALS
01 Joseph Auguste's military booklet. Copy of the page of his death. 02 Auguste Joseph Chenavier, 2 photos from 1914-1915. 03 Aeronautical photo album, war years 1914-18.

title: FRBMBO-070 Histoire du soldat Albéric Bru | Albéric Bru's story
text:
TITLE
FRBMBO-070 Histoire du soldat Albéric Bru | Albéric Bru's story

Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. Jean-Michel Bru bears witness to the memory of his paternal grandfather: Albéric Bru born in 1881 in Bergerac. He was called up at the age of 33 while teaching mathematics at Blaye College. Between the beginning of August 1914 and the end of September 1915, he wrote a war diary. He was assigned to the ambulance of the 92nd Territorial Infantry Division. Leaving Ile de France, he passed through the Oise, the Somme, the Pas de Calais, the Argonne. From September 20, 1915, he was assigned to Bar-le-Duc (Meuse) in the laboratory of the 3rd army as a chemist. He was in charge of the powder division (choking gases) He was demobilized in 1919.

RELATED MATERIALS
Document: Three small notebooks (97 pages in all) which constitute a war diary between September 1914 and September 1915 from which it was necessary to select extracts.

title: Tagebuch des jungen Bernhard Bültemann aus Halle | The diary of young Bernhard Bültemann
text:
TITLE
Tagebuch des jungen Bernhard Bültemann aus Halle | The diary of young Bernhard Bültemann

METADATA
Title: Tagebuch des jungen Bernhard Bültemann aus Halle | The diary of young Bernhard Bültemann
Record type: TEXT
Places: Jaroslaw, Polen
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
When war broke out in 1914, Bernhard Bültemann was 12 years old and was on vacation in Gotha. Because of the hustle and bustle that prevailed at the time, his father came from Halle to pick him up. In Halle he immediately noticed some changes: the "Electric" ones were stuck with red mobilization notices and the poster pillars with warnings about spies. When school started again, Bültemann's father was the only one who had to join the army straight away. He came to Infantry Regiment 36 "Graf Blumenthal". His next stations were Torgau and Altengrabow, where French, Zuavi, Belgian and British soldiers were already interned in a barracks camp on the military training ground there. The 12-year-old, who was visiting relatives nearby with his mother, got into conversation with some of them. Soon after, his father was transferred to the Eastern Front. In October 1914, the Infantry Regiment 232 went via Berlin and Frankfurt to Posen and from there via Deutsch Eylau (today Ilawa) to Neu-Jucha, a few kilometers north of Lyck (today Elk), in East Prussia, which had been destroyed by the Russians. Immediately after his arrival, his father took part in the winter battles in Lyck and Augustów. He suffered from rheumatism from constant marches and spending the night outdoors. On February 9, 1915, the father wrote to his son from Sierniewice near Lodz: "How is it now on the ice. I hope you have a good time. I don't." On the postcard he handwritten the words "God punish England". He wrote to his wife on April 18, 1915: "You get really rough here. Aren't you afraid of your husband when he comes back? And he'll come back!" The last card was from Jaroslau (today Jaroslaw) on June 9th: "We have to endure great hardships here in the mountains, and it's difficult for me. I'm also not very sane." On June 25, a comrade wrote that the father had died in an assault on June 13 at 7 a.m. Of four brothers, he was the only one who fell. His mother, Bernhard Bültemann's grandmother, is said to have treasured the cards, letters and pictures of her favorite son and cried a lot every time she looked at them.

RELATED MATERIALS
War diary by Bernhard Bültemann. In it he describes both his father's military service, who died in 1915 in the East, as well as the general development of the war, school days during the war, the efforts of the women at home and at home, the harsh war winter of 1916/17 and the prisoner of war camp in Halle.

title: FRAD066_090 Barthélémy Garreta | Documents of Barthélémy Garreta
text:
TITLE
FRAD066_090 Barthélémy Garreta | Documents of Barthélémy Garreta

METADATA
Title: FRAD066_090 Barthélémy Garreta | Documents of Barthélémy Garreta
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Stuttgart
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, deu, Deutsch, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Barthélémy Garreta is from Hérault. Child of separated parents, he had a difficult childhood, torn between his two parents. Called up in 1913 to do his military service, he took part in the conflict as soon as war was declared. He served in the 92nd Infantry Regiment as a corporal. The chronology of events is not known to the family but we know that he was a prisoner of war twice (he escaped once), notably in Stuttgart. He was also wounded by a shell which killed some of his comrades: he received shrapnel and became deaf in one ear. After the war, he worked as a stonemason in a cemetery: it was there that he met his future wife in 1919. She came every day to put flowers on the grave of her fiancé killed in the war. Marked by the separation of his parents and traumatized by the war, he refuses to have children: it is only 14 years after his marriage that his daughter is born, "by accident". Although unwanted, she brings joy to her father and the household. From his imprisonment in Germany, he will keep an aversion for Frankfurt sausages, and by analogy, for those of Strasbourg, which he forbade his family to consume...

RELATED MATERIALS
Photographs of Barthélémy Garreta with comrades (1st: standing left, 2nd: standing right, 3rd: seated); photograph of Barthélémy Garreta while he is a prisoner in Stuttgart and stamp on the back; commemorative card of the 92nd regiment.

title: FRAD043_060 Alexis Rispal, soldat de la Grande guerre | Documents about Alexis Rispal
text:
TITLE
FRAD043_060 Alexis Rispal, soldat de la Grande guerre | Documents about Alexis Rispal

METADATA
Title: FRAD043_060 Alexis Rispal, soldat de la Grande guerre | Documents about Alexis Rispal
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Yvette Clemencet tells the story of her paternal grandfather Antoine-Alexis Rispal. Alexis Rispal, born in 1879, was a photographer in the department of Cantal, based more precisely in the town of Ternes. He died in 1953. His granddaughter did not know her grandfather who died when she was only a few months old. The family hardly ever mentioned the war. Yvette's father, son of Alexis Rispal, passed on his father's collection of postcards to her. After reading all the cards, Yvette listed them in chronological order. During the conflict, Alexis Rispal wrote many cards to his family. Often they are numbered because the text continues on several cards. Reading Alexis Rispal's wartime correspondence, his granddaughter realizes that he traveled a lot during this period. In 1917, Alexis Rispal was expecting her fourth child and hoped to benefit from a reform in order to be able to stay at home and take care of her four children. His granddaughter knows nevertheless that in 1918 he is still mobilized. Consequently, in her correspondence, Yvette Clemencet discovers a feeling of great weariness, of depression from her grandfather who remained mobilized, far from his family and his children.

RELATED MATERIALS
1/ Two photographic postcards by Antoine-Alexis Rispal, including one from February 1917. 2/ A photograph of Antoine-Alexis Rispal with three regimental comrades (Antoine-Alexis Rispal is the third from the left). 3/ Two photographs by Antoine-Alexis Rispal, one portrait and one bust.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Georges Dubos is from Gironde. An orphan, he was raised by his great-aunt. He studied violin at the Bordeaux Conservatory. Belonging to the 1917 class, he benefited from a reprieve for health reasons until 1918. On the front (Seine-et-Marne), a comrade made him a violin from a cigar box. We see him playing in the photographs, he also mentions it in his war diary During the war, he met Marcel Vincent: they became friends, so much so that Georges Dubos went on leave with his comrade in the family of this last. There he met his sister Yvonne: they married in 1925, the time Georges made a "situation". On his return from the war, he gave up the violin, if not for his leisure, and entered the administration. Marcel Vincent also survived the war; he was gassed which caused him emphysema.

RELATED MATERIALS
Travel diary by Georges Dubos (excerpts); photographs by Georges Dubos; photography by Marcel Vincent; letters from Marcel Vincent to his sister Yvonne (including one written at the signing of the Treaty of Versailles).

STORY / CONTEXT
Eugène Lucien Lebert was a restaurant hunter in Paris. From the 1909 class, during the 14-18 war, he was 1st class horseman in the 15th Chasseurs Regiment. He fell in battle at Les Eparges (near Verdun) while providing liaison, leaving a widow and their only child, an 18-month-old daughter. This little girl became the mother of Josette Préel, contributor.

Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Major Louis Menetrier was squadron leader in the 61st artillery regiment. An officer of great value, he was killed in action on September 8, 1915. A letter sent four months after his death evokes a reconnaissance ride on horseback in the springtime and seemingly unreal atmosphere of this war, and the health of his men and the 500 horses for which he was responsible. He was cited twice in the orders of the Army and was made a member of the Legion of Honor.

RELATED MATERIALS
Documents of family origin: Letter written in May 1915 by Louis Ménifère to his father in Le Havre Letter written by a Mr. Martin about the death of Commander Ménifère Military document of summons to the order of the regiment by Lieutenant-Colonel Delagout , and citations to the order of the Army by Generals Sarrail and Langle de Cary

FRAD033-102 Histoire de Louis Gauthier | Documents from Louis Gauthier

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Louis Gauthier was born on November 11, 1895 in Corrèze. Incorporated into the 86th Infantry Regiment of the 13th Army Corps, it took part in the battles of Lorraine, Verdun, the Somme and the Marne. He writes his daily life in notebooks. Accordionist, Louis Gauthier also added songs to his writings during his hospital stays. He learns of the signing of the Armistice at the station when he returns to the front. He died in Corrèze in April 1975.

RELATED MATERIALS
Handwritten notebook "History of operations during the 1914-1918 war" accompanied by songs (1915). Small handwritten notebook of his life on the front and songs (1915). Notebook of songs written or copied during his convalescence in hospital and after the war (1918).

title: FRAM - Claude Alfred Camille VENOT | Claude VENOT
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Claude Alfred Camille VENOT | Claude VENOT

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Claude Alfred Camille VENOT | Claude VENOT
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Claude Alfred Camille VENOT (1866-1914) was an active officer. He had notably made the campaign of Madagascar (1894-1895). He was mobilized as a lieutenant-colonel in the 206th infantry regiment. He died "killed by the enemy" in Nancy (Meurthe-et-Moselle), on September 7, 1914, north of the Bouzule halt, on the edge of the forest. A monument has been erected on this spot. Recognized for his uprightness and his sense of duty, he later married Marie Marthe Francise TROTTIER (June 1, 1909). His son, born on June 7, 1914, was only three months old when his father died, at the age of 48. He is buried in the south cemetery of Nancy under the number 67385.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photograph of Claude VENOT - Service record of Lieutenant-Colonel Claude VENOT - Franchise card from Claude VENOT addressed to his wife (September 6, 1914) - Letter from du Mesnil to Claude VENOT's wife following his death (September 15, 1914) - Notes held by Claude VENOT in his hand when he was killed (September 7, 1914)

STORY / CONTEXT
Story told by Germaine Mattson, daughter-in-law of Knut William Mattson (medical doctor in Sweden whose diploma had not been recognized in France). Although not mobilized, being Swedish and married to a Franco-Swedish woman and living in Paris, he had deemed it his duty to make himself available to the country at war which had welcomed him. He therefore offered his services to the Red Cross as a masseur for the wounded from August 2, 1914 to November 11, 1918, which entitled him to the commemorative medal of the great war for the good care given to the wounded in CRF ambulances and in the ambulances of the Rothschild family.

RELATED MATERIALS
Military Casualty Relief Certificate Certificate of Loan from Knut Mattson's Son Letter of Thanks from Halphen Rothschild Photo of Soldiers with Knut William Mattson

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story of Alphonse Colignon, the great-grandfather of Jocelyne Janez. My paternal great-grandfather, Alphonse Colignon, was born on December 2, 1873 in Jouarre (77, Seine-et-Marne). He was a cooper in Jouarre. He was mobilized on August 4, 1914 at the age of 41, married and father of three daughters, the eldest of whom was 7 years old. He was based in the 36th Territorial on December 18, 1914. He left as reinforcement on January 30, 1915 to the 276th, then to the engineers in Montpellier, as a roadmender on October 1, 1915. Then, it was the Rodez depot on January 21, 1916, he spent to the 27th company, then to the 39th company. On March 19, 1916 he was assigned to the depot of the 123rd Territorial. He was injured in the big toe by a sharp object (bayonet?) in service on December 25, 1916. His injury required an operation on January 24, 1917. He was killed on the Chemins des Dames in Pontavert on April 20, 1917.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 01 Alphonse Colignon. Military booklet. - 02 Alphonse Colignon. 2 Soldier's diplomas. Death on April 20, 1917. - 03 Alphonse Colignon. Enamelled plaque. Died in Pontavert (Aisne) at 44 years old. - 04 Alphonse Colignon and his brother Eugène. 2 pictures. - 05 Alphonse Colignon. Card to his family. - 06 Alphonse Colignon. 2 objects, one of which is engraved (each of the objects is made from 2 balls with their bottoms welded together).
