STORY / CONTEXT
Originally from Marseille, Gustave Gravier is the eldest of a family of eleven children. Polytechnician, he was mobilized from the start of hostilities as a maritime engineering officer in the 20th army corps. He married in 1917, he had a daughter in September-October 1918. During this period, he became an instructor for American officers. He was never injured during the war. He lost two of his brothers: Édouard and Jean. The latter was injured in September 1914 by shrapnel and died in 1916. Gustave subsequently worked in his father's mixed shipping company in Marseilles.

RELATED MATERIALS
Correspondence: letters from Jean to Gustave and letters from Gustave to his parents.

title: Mémoires d'un prisonnier : Abel Lugez : 1914-1919 | Memories of a prisoner of war
text:
TITLE
Mémoires d'un prisonnier : Abel Lugez : 1914-1919 | Memories of a prisoner of war

METADATA
Title: Mémoires d'un prisonnier : Abel Lugez : 1914-1919 | Memories of a prisoner of war
Record type: TEXT
Places: Chemnitz
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Abel Lugez, my paternal grandfather, was born on July 12, 1885 in Lillers in the Pas de Calais. In 1914, he was a miner by profession, married to Blanche Martin and father of a three-year-old daughter, Suzanne. June of this year, he completed a 17-day military period at the Sissonne camp. He was mobilized shortly after in August 1914 to the 345th RI. Around September 7, he was captured near Fort Leveau in the siege of Maubeuge, with tens of thousands of comrades. Arriving in Zossen, near Berlin, he was transferred to a camp in Obersdorf-Chemnitz, in Lower Saxony, where he spent more than 50 months in captivity, before returning to France in January 1919. First held in barracks from Chemnitz, he was then assigned to a cobblestone quarry for a year and a half. Following several attacks of appendicitis, he was appointed to carry out agricultural work on several farms. Wrongly accused of larceny, he was imprisoned for 2 months in inhuman conditions (6 people in 4 m2...). On leaving this jail, he is sent again to a farm (where he will be treated well), then to a quarry, finally to a coal mine, where he still works hard. He is operated on for appendicitis but survives. of the Spanish flu. When the Armistice intervenes, he returns to the camp and is only released on January 1, 1919. Back to Lilles, what a joy to find relatives and friends! But he must wait some time before finding his wife and daughter evacuated for more than a year in the Nièvre, in Cosnes d'Allier at a place called "L'Etang des Granges". My grandmother Blanche worked as a cook in a camp of American soldiers. return to his position as an underground miner. he will become a "meneu d'chevaux" (horse driver), then maneuver on the floor of the mine, before finishing as gardener of the director of the mine. In 1920, then in 1925, the Lugez family grows from Abel my father, and Robert. Living with his daughter since his widowhood, he died in August 1969 in Banyuls-sur-Mer (Pyrénées-Orientales), at the age of 84. In 1958, Abel Lugez wrote his Memoirs in verse, in a school notebook. About thirty pages relate this period of captivity.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Mobilization booklet - Handwritten, French / German lexicon in a school notebook - Correspondence of Abel with his family: photographs-postcards, letters - Quite a number of photos of comrades in captivity - Postcards of places: Chemnitz camp

title: FRAD087_062-"Notes de voyage" de Martial Daigueperse | Diary and documents from Martial Daigueperse
text:
TITLE
FRAD087_062-"Notes de voyage" de Martial Daigueperse | Diary and documents from Martial Daigueperse

METADATA
Title: FRAD087_062-"Notes de voyage" de Martial Daigueperse | Diary and documents from Martial Daigueperse
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story of Martial Daigueperse told by his granddaughter. Martial Daigueperse was the only son of a coachman living in Saint-Germain-les-Belles (Haute-Vienne) and a servant born in Bujaleuf (Haute-Vienne). He learned the profession of typographer and worked in Limoges at the Bontemps printing works, specializing in scientific works. He worked there while on leave. During the war, he was a gunner serving in the 34th Artillery Regiment. The war saw him pass through Champagne, and he fought in particular at Verdun, without being injured. Martial Daigueperse kept a diary, written in pencil on two notebooks, which he called "Notes de voyage". This diary covers the period 1914-1919, from his departure from Limoges until his return from the front. He observes daily life at the front, adopting a very neutral tone. He describes the construction of the shelters, the cooking he often does for the group to which he belongs, the German attacks, the news he receives, the permissions, the climate and his fatigue, the attacks to retake Fleury and Thiaumont. Martial Daigueperse died around 1967. His notebooks were fully transcribed by his granddaughter.

RELATED MATERIALS
Two notebooks entitled "Notes de voyage" (August 7, 1914-March 26, 1919); military booklet; photograph by Martial Daigueperse taken before the war; his parents' family record book.

title: FRAD087_038-Histoire de Georges Philippon | Documents about Georges Philippon
text:
TITLE
FRAD087_038-Histoire de Georges Philippon | Documents about Georges Philippon

METADATA
Title: FRAD087_038-Histoire de Georges Philippon | Documents about Georges Philippon
Record type: TEXT
Places: Noulette (Pas-de-Calais)
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, Dansk, dan, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story of Georges Philippon told by his great-niece. Georges Philippon's family was originally from Creuse. His parents, masons, settled in Paris, where Georges Philippon was born. His parents then moved to Creuse, where they settled as grocers. Georges Philippon, of the 1915 class, was incorporated into the 3rd Infantry Corps as a 2nd class. He maintained a regular correspondence with his mother during the conflict. For lack of news of her son, his mother became worried. On September 10, 1915, she received a letter from Sergeant Mathieu who had been her son's instructor in Bellac (Haute-Vienne). This one tells him in this letter the circumstances of the death of his son: Georges Philippon had fallen on June 27, 1915 at 8 am, from a shrapnel to the head in Noulette (Pas-de-Calais) . Highly esteemed by his superiors, he was about to be made a corporal. The named Mathieu knew the area where Georges Philippon had fallen, but not the precise location. Constant, brother of Georges, also took part in the conflict (class of 1916). He returned, but died of his injuries (he was hit by gas) in 1941 at the Limeil-Brévannes hospital.

RELATED MATERIALS
Letter addressed to the mother of Georges Philippon dated September 10, 1915; letter from Constant to his brother Fernand from the hospital, undated.

title: FRAD077-139, Ernest Hylaire, correspondance avec sa plus jeune fille pendant la guerre. | Ernest Hylaire, letters to his younger daughter during the war.
text:
TITLE
FRAD077-139, Ernest Hylaire, correspondance avec sa plus jeune fille pendant la guerre. | Ernest Hylaire, letters to his younger daughter during the war.

METADATA
Title: FRAD077-139, Ernest Hylaire, correspondance avec sa plus jeune fille pendant la guerre. | Ernest Hylaire, letters to his younger daughter during the war.
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915, 1916
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The contributor's grandfather, Ernest Hylaire, was married with three daughters when he was called up. He lived in Fontainebleau (France, Seine-et-Marne) where he worked as a carpenter. During the conflict, he exchanged correspondence with the youngest of his daughters, Marguerite, the contributor's mother. He reused the cards that his daughter sent him to reply, writing between the lines written by her. Madame Cauwet, the contributor, cannot talk much about what her grandfather went through during the conflict, but she does know that he was gassed in the trenches. The postcards she keeps indicate that her grandfather was in the trenches of the Meuse, around Verdun between 1915 and 1916.

RELATED MATERIALS
1- A photograph of Ernest Hylaire in soldier's uniform with his family, 2- A postcard with a humorous drawing, 3- A postcard from Marguerite to her father dated April 26, 1915 with an answer from Ernest Hylaire dated October 12, 1915 , 3- a postcard from Marguerite to her father dated September 14, 1915 with an answer from Ernest Hylaire, 4- a postcard from Marguerite and Suzanne to their father with an answer from Ernest Hylaire dated October 25, 1915, 5- a Postcard from Marguerite to her father dated December 29, 1916 with an answer from Ernest Hylaire.

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean Pastour, perhaps one of the ancestors of the contributor Hélène Arbousset but who does not remember exactly, was enlisted from 1914 to 1915 as a corporal quartermaster (the quartermaster is the non-commissioned officer in charge of stewardship) and he returned with the rank of sergeant major. The year of his return is not known.

A photo album with photographs of Jean Pastour during the war, a photo album with the environment of the war in 1914 and 1915 including a photo of a mass at the front and a military review.

title: Brief van Omer De Backer aan zijn vader, geschreven aan het front in Pervijze (12/11/1914) | Letter from Omer De Backer to his father, written from the front in Pervijze (12/11/1914)
text:
TITLE
Brief van Omer De Backer aan zijn vader, geschreven aan het front in Pervijze (12/11/1914) | Letter from Omer De Backer to his father, written from the front in Pervijze (12/11/1914)

METADATA
Title: Brief van Omer De Backer aan zijn vader, geschreven aan het front in Pervijze (12/11/1914) | Letter from Omer De Backer to his father, written from the front in Pervijze (12/11/1914)
Record type: TEXT
Places: Pervijze
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Pervyse, 12-11-14 My dear Father, I send you all my best wishes for health and happiness on your birthday. I have never, since my tenderest youth, ever written to you in circumstances so critical for all of us. I hope, despite the Prussian reinforcements which still arrive every day, you will not have to leave the house where you have spent the best part of your life; you may be among the privileged few who will never be Prussians. We are still victoriously holding our own all the way. The Kaiser however said "We must take Calais at all costs" and despite this formal order what happens? The Prussians are massacred en masse. We will not know how many Prussians died along the Nieuport-Arras line until after the war, but what is certain is that figure will be fantastic. I am always well. I haven't heard from you since I left Beveren. I don't even know if you get this letter. Don't worry if you don't receive my correspondence regularly because some get lost. Take care my dear parents brother and sister, and if everything goes as I hope, there will still be good days. My best kisses to you all. Omer I haven't heard from Michel or Remi. They are all in good health.

RELATED MATERIALS
Copy of a letter from Omer de Backer to his father Leopold (constable in Beveren aan de Yser) from the front in Pervijze on 12 November 1914 on the occasion of his name day (15 November)

title: FRAD043_064 La guerre de Jean Jouvenel, maréchal-ferrant | Documents from Jean Jouvenel
text:
TITLE
FRAD043_064 La guerre de Jean Jouvenel, maréchal-ferrant | Documents from Jean Jouvenel

METADATA
Title: FRAD043_064 La guerre de Jean Jouvenel, maréchal-ferrant | Documents from Jean Jouvenel
Record type: TEXT
Places: Mortagne
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. Daniel Grenier and his wife Odile talk about their childhood neighbor, Jean Jouvenel, farrier and blacksmith in Vergongheon (Haute-Loire, France). He bequeathed his memories of the Great War to Mr. Grenier whom Mr. and Mrs. Jouvenel, childless, had taken in affection. Jean Jouvenel, class 1907, was mobilized as soon as war was declared. He arrived at the front on September 3, 1914 with the 36th field artillery regiment, a regiment in which he served throughout the war. He will take part in the combats of Verdun. Decorated with the Croix de guerre, the Victory medal and the commemorative medal, he was also mentioned twice in the order of the division and in the order of the regiment for bravery in combat. Mr. Grenier kept the memory of Jean Jouvenel talking about the war and his flank wound. On November 13, 1916, while trying to recover ammunition during a bombardment, Jean Jouvenel was seriously hit in the side as evidenced by the summons to the order of the division. Returning to civilian life, Jean Jouvenel will resume his activity as a farrier. He was married to Germaine Chabillon of Charbonnier-les-mines.

RELATED MATERIALS
Portrait of Jean Jouvenel. Military quotes. History of the 36th and 236th field artillery regiments. Commemorative frame "1914-1918" accompanied by decorations and a portrait of Jean Jouvenel.

title: AMSM-009_1 : Le journal de route de mon grand-père | The Journal of Onesime Maupiller
text:
TITLE
AMSM-009_1 : Le journal de route de mon grand-père | The Journal of Onesime Maupiller

METADATA
Title: AMSM-009_1 : Le journal de route de mon grand-père | The Journal of Onesime Maupiller
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1915
Places: Saint Sauveur
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
I still have this little diary from my grandfather. He had given it to my father and it is from him that I now have it. My grandfather was called Onesime MAUPILLER and was a farmer in Saint Sauveur before the war. Moreover, he will become so again on his return from the war, in the same commune. He was incorporated on August 3, 1914 as a driver in the 49th artillery regiment, 1st colonial corps of Poitiers, 33rd section of colonial MI. I know very little about my grandfather and what his life was like in the war because he lived far from us. The distance, the character of my grandfather and my young age were not in favor of a discussion between him and me on this episode of his life. All that remains for me to relate to his story is this notebook, of which I cannot explain why it stops after the period 1914-1915, when I know that he remained at the front until 1918. .I think he wrote this notebook over the course of the war. It reads at the beginning "Recollection of the 1914 campaign, as if he thought the war would only last a year. Then the year 1915 was added. I think then he stopped keeping his diary. Among the things that I noted, there is this short passage in which he writes of having found a German in the forest, hanged with his belt.

title: FRAM - Henri MESSEILLER | Henri MESSEILLER
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Henri MESSEILLER | Henri MESSEILLER

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Henri MESSEILLER | Henri MESSEILLER
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Henri MESSEILLER (1876-1955) was called up as a doctor and made Verdun as such. Married, he had three daughters. He was allowed to leave the front to bury his wife who had died of tuberculosis. At the age of 20, her second daughter succumbed to the same disease. He remarried after the war to a widow. His two surviving children were raised by their stepmother. He then took care of his granddaughter to whom he gave a taste for drawing. She also became a stylist. Doctor before the war, he then became a herbalist.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photograph of the MESSEILLER family in front of their pharmacy, located in Asnières (Hauts-de-Seine). - Photograph of Henri MESSEILLER with his daughters. - Postcard from the staff of the hospital to which Henri MESSEILLER belonged.

title: FRAM - Albert et Charles Rauch | Documents from Albert and Charles Rauch
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Albert et Charles Rauch | Documents from Albert and Charles Rauch

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Albert et Charles Rauch | Documents from Albert and Charles Rauch
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1880, 1919
Places: SAVERNE puis LE HAVRE puis DUISBOURG ET MÜNSTER
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. RAUCH entrusted us with documents relating to his grandfather, Albert Rauch and his great uncle, Charles Rauch, Albert's brother. Their father, Auguste, who lived in SAVERNE (ZABERN) left Alsace in 1880 at the age of 20 after this region became German in 1873 (not to do his military service in Germany). he worked as a locksmith in NANCY, EPINAL, GRANDRUPT, VARVILLE until 1890, then moved to Le Havre (did he want to emigrate to the Americas?). he married Marie RABECQ on July 30, 1892 under German nationality (sic). Charles was born in 1893 and is German like his parents. The family will be naturalized French on March 19, 1894. The 2nd son Albert is born French in 1895. Auguste RAUCH opens a locksmith at 236 route de Normandie in Le Havre (now avenue Aristide Briand since 1932) which still continues its activity today and remains managed by the great grandsons of Auguste. During the First World War, Albert and Charles were both mobilized from August 14. Charles was taken prisoner in Germany on September 14, 1914 and did not return until the end of the conflict. Documents such as postcards, song books and above all a booklet of 38 copied letters are available as well as a censored letter (see the documents). Albert left on September 9, 1915, as a miner in the 21st Engineer Regiment. He was cited in the orders of the 3rd Division of the 1st Colonial Army Corps on July 25, 1918 for acts of bravery, in particular during the battle of the Chemin des Dames (this distinction is the subject of an article in the newspaper " le petit Havre" of Monday, August 12, 1918. After the Armistice, he was assigned to the French occupation troops in Germany and was demobilized in Fécamp in September 1919. Albert received the war cross and then multiple distinctions and diplomas in the part of his cultural life in Le Havre after the Great War Marius CHIBRAN, a soldier and friend of Albert and Charles Rauch, left 2 military instruction booklets on firearms and in particular an extremely detailed rifle through numerous sketches colored.

RELATED MATERIALS
Postcard, censored letter with content of the censored part, song book, Licensee's Guide, quotes from Albert Rauch, photographs, excerpt from Marius CHIBRAN's military instruction book

Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Vincent Burkarth, paternal grandfather of contributor Fabienne Burkarth, was born in Pantin on September 26, 1885. From the class of 1905 he was mobilized from 1914 to 1919 in the 245th infantry regiment, registration number 1267. Wounded on 15/10/1914 he also suffered gas in the trenches, he remained in fragile health. He returned home in 1919 but died prematurely in 1933. His granddaughter Fabienne Burkarth did not know him.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Military booklet - Military quote from October 1915 - Two photographs/postcards of groups of soldiers

title: AMSM-017_1 : Mon grand-père, ce hussard. | Barthélémy Rey's Picture And Letters From The Front
text:
TITLE
AMSM-017_1 : Mon grand-père, ce hussard. | Barthélémy Rey's Picture And Letters From The Front

METADATA
Title: AMSM-017_1 : Mon grand-père, ce hussard. | Barthélémy Rey's Picture And Letters From The Front
Record type: TEXT
Places: Saint Félix en Haute-Garonne
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, eng, Patois du Languedoc, English, Français, Other
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My grandfather, Barthélémy REY, was born on March 13, 1881 in Saint Félix in Haute-Garonne. He was a winegrower before being mobilized in 1914. When his house was sold, we found a set of documents belonging to him relating to his participation in the conflict. It was in a way a discovery for me, because at home we finally talked about him very little and even less about the war, I just know that he would have been exposed to gas but I don't know under what conditions or in what proportions. He was very attached to his vineyard, and this can be seen in particular in his correspondence with his parents. He speaks just as much about the vine as about the war and his family when she writes to him, also speaks about the vine. Among his functions, I know that my grandfather was a trumpet. And what is surprising is that one of his cousins was too, as we can read in one of his letters. All these documents, I found them at the sale of my grandfather's house. I felt quite a strong emotion when I discovered their content, this image of a daily life which has not ceased despite the war.

title: FRAD071-003, Jacques SAUVAGEOT, parcours d'un soldat. | Documents from Jacques Sauvageot
text:
TITLE
FRAD071-003, Jacques SAUVAGEOT, parcours d'un soldat. | Documents from Jacques Sauvageot

METADATA
Title: FRAD071-003, Jacques SAUVAGEOT, parcours d'un soldat. | Documents from Jacques Sauvageot
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1917
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story by Claude Sauvageot, about Jacques Sauvageot, his father. The latter fought near Notre-Dame-de-Lorette and was wounded in the right hand, at Souchez, in September 1915, by shrapnel. He was sent to various hospitals including that of Sainte-Anne-d'Auray. He was reformed after his injury and took over his parents' farm in Saint-Martin-Belle-Roche (Saône-et-Loire). Died on January 5, 1968, he had two children (two boys). His father often spoke of the war, of its harshness, of his comrades who died next to him. Claude Sauvageot also contributed to the story FRAD071-074, Hyppolite TARDY.

RELATED MATERIALS
Set of photos and postcards, military booklet and a framed quote.

title: FRAM - Odon de Viguerie, sous-officier au 18e régiment d'artillerie, blessé en 1915 et 1916 | Odon de Viguerie
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Odon de Viguerie, sous-officier au 18e régiment d'artillerie, blessé en 1915 et 1916 | Odon de Viguerie

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Odon de Viguerie, sous-officier au 18e régiment d'artillerie, blessé en 1915 et 1916 | Odon de Viguerie
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My father was 20 years old when he was mobilized. He was seriously wounded for the first time in combat on September 25, 1915: being in the capacity of artillery liaison officer with the attack battalion commander, he did not did not hesitate to follow him to the end when he could have stayed at the rear of the attack. He was treated in Brest and after two months of convalescence, he was sent back to the Reims front. He was again seriously wounded on September 4, 1916, while he was providing liaison with the infantry, in a particularly bombarded sector. He received shrapnel which tore off part of his face and left eye. He kept his cool despite great pain and went to the aid station alone under the shelling. He recounts in his diary the date of September 6, 1916 "operated in the morning - chloroform - removed what remains of the left eye - bears well" and on September 7 "good day, I have my bandages redone - good care from the college Saint-Dizier". My father is getting married after the war. He was awarded the Croix de Guerre and the Legion of Honor.

title: AMSM-021_1 : Le retour d'Orient de mon grand-père | Eugène Fresia's return from the Orient
text:
TITLE
AMSM-021_1 : Le retour d'Orient de mon grand-père | Eugène Fresia's return from the Orient

METADATA
Title: AMSM-021_1 : Le retour d'Orient de mon grand-père | Eugène Fresia's return from the Orient
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1919
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My maternal grandfather, Eugène FRESIA was born on December 22, 1892. After his classes in 1912, he was mobilized on December 17, 1914 against Germany, before being sent to the Eastern Front from July 25, 1917 to 22 March 1919. I know very little about his participation in the war, because my grandfather was deeply pacifist and anti-militarist. For him there was no kind of honor in having fought in the war, so he hated it when people said of a soldier that he was brave, in his eyes the war was just a vast butchery. Whenever he spoke of his experience of the war, he invariably said negative things about it. It was in his military booklet that I found the document in question here. It is a simple loose sheet on which he wrote down the journey he took to return from Bulgaria in 1919. It was when I found this piece of paper that I remembered the reasons for his being sent to the Eastern Front: Returning from leave, my grandfather arrived at the depot several times too late to return to the front. So much so that he ends up being consigned there to prevent him from "losing his way" again. In the end, being there, he was embarked on a ship leaving for the East. The latter will finally be sunk near the Dardanelles Strait. He will then spend 24 hours at sea, clinging to a piece of debris before being rescued, then treated in Greece following a fairly serious impetigo caused, according to him, by the "scar" he had had. It was only in 1919 that he was able to return with his regiment from Bulgaria, passing through many cities. In itself, this document is rather dry, the information it contains is quite poor and does not capture what his life was like during these few days of travel. Also, more than reading it, it is by writing the cities mentioned on a map that I was finally able to touch the finger of his route - a large part of which was made on foot - to return home. He ends by writing "la Piole" which expresses his relief at having returned from what he refused to call the Great War. It must be said that, having left for the army in 1912 for military service, he home almost 7 years later.

title: Des Poilus du Valdonnez | Documents belonging to Antoine Jean Francois Mejean
text:
TITLE
Des Poilus du Valdonnez | Documents belonging to Antoine Jean Francois Mejean

Title: Des Poilus du Valdonnez | Documents belonging to Antoine Jean Francois Mejean

Places: Meuse
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My paternal Great-Grandfather was Antoine Jean François MEJEAN. He was killed in action, in the trenches of the Meuse on June 17, 1916, at the age of 38. He orphaned a Son: Pierre François Louis (my Grandfather). 3 other of his brothers died in 14; 2 others were injured, and one was taken out of the fight to prevent the family from disappearing forever. Our family has paid a heavy tribute; all the more so since on my Mother's side, there were also victims.

RELATED MATERIALS
The documents are letters, postcards, portraits, statements, notebook...

title: FRAD033-126 Histoire de Marcel Geneste | Documents from Marcel Geneste
text:

FRAD033-126 Histoire de Marcel Geneste | Documents from Marcel Geneste

Record type: TEXT

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Born on January 30, 1876, Marcel Geneste married Eléonore Bouteil on September 30, 1899. He was part of the 1896 class and benefited from a suspension of mobilization until July 10, 1917. He worked as a carpenter and made a light bulb table ("Geneste" model) in order to facilitate the work of the doctors of the radiology department of the main physiotherapy center of the 12th region located in Limoges.

RELATED MATERIALS
Correspondence with the main physiotherapy center of the 12th region (Limoges), with the chemical engineer P. Roubertie, with doctor Lomon from hospital 35 in Paris-plage (Pas-de-Calais). Suspension of mobilization. Technical description of the table. Photographs of the table.

title: FRAD033-079 Histoire de Pierre, Louis Mercé | Documents from Pierre Louis Mercé
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-079 Histoire de Pierre, Louis Mercé | Documents from Pierre Louis Mercé

Title: FRAD033-079 Histoire de Pierre, Louis Mercé | Documents from Pierre Louis Mercé
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Pierre, Louis Mercé was born in Bègles on June 1, 1892. Owner of the cod dryer, he married Anna Mercé, restaurant owner. He was incorporated on October 10, 1913 as quartermaster sergeant (in charge of distributing food and providing accommodation for soldiers). Assigned to the 339th Infantry Regiment, he was wounded and gassed several times during the fighting. Loving to sing, he participated in the artistic evening of June 1, 1919 given by his infantry regiment before being demobilized on August 26, 1919.

RELATED MATERIALS
Military booklet. Pass for Anna Mercé. Postcard (photograph) addressed to his parents (May 26, 1916). Program of the artistic evening (June 1, 1919). Song "The good little woman".

title: FRAD033-074 Histoire de Georges Coignet | Documents from Georges Coignet
text:
TITLE
FRAD033-074 Histoire de Georges Coignet | Documents from Georges Coignet

METADATA
Title: FRAD033-074 Histoire de Georges Coignet | Documents from Georges Coignet
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Georges Coignet was born on June 11, 1894 in Menon (Nièvre). He went to the front with his five brothers (Georges, Gaston, Henri, Jean and Georges) on October 18, 1914; they will all come back alive in 1918. Assigned to the 121st infantry corps, Georges Coignet is cited in the orders of regiment 335 on November 8, 1916 for having chased the enemy from a trench with grenades. He received the Croix de Guerre with bronze star. He was demobilized on September 10, 1919. Driver at "Esso Rep" in Bègles, he died in Villenave d'Ornon in November 1971.

RELATED MATERIALS
Military booklet accompanied by its identification plate. 9 black and white photographs and postcards (1914-1918).

title: FRAD033-070 Histoire de René, Jean Dutruch | Documents from René Jean Dutruch
text:

Title: FRAD033-070 Histoire de René, Jean Dutruch | Documents from René Jean Dutruch
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Photographs: print representing a group in front of the Truck Transport Company after a banquet (soon after the war) [René Dutruch seated in the front row]. Postcards-correspondence: 4 postcards-correspondence: departure of troops for Morocco; Souges camp (camp avenue and barracks); German prisoners between Furmes and Nieuport (1914); 20 postcards-correspondence from André Dutruch to his cousin René, Jean (1914-1915); from René, Jean Dutruch to his parents (1915); friendly correspondence received by René, Jean Dutruch (1915-1917). Revue "La Grande Guerre": 69 copies (1914-1918).

RELATED MATERIALS
René, Jean Dutruch was born in 1896 in Toignan, commune of Saint-Loubès (Gironde). A soldier in the 25th company of the 57th infantry regiment, he joined the group of student corporals at Camp de Souge. During the conflict, he was injured and taken to the temporary hospital 46 Le Touquet - Paris-Plage (Pas-de-Calais).

title: AMSM-013_1 : Une enfance marquante | Letters and Picture Belonging to Joseph Pierre Antoine Eynard
text:
TITLE
AMSM-013_1 : Une enfance marquante | Letters and Picture Belonging to Joseph Pierre Antoine Eynard

METADATA
Title: AMSM-013_1 : Une enfance marquante | Letters and Picture Belonging to Joseph Pierre Antoine Eynard
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1916
Places: Souilly
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
This is the story of Joseph Pierre Antoine Eynard, my maternal grandfather, of his military campaigns on the other side of the world, and of what his story evokes for me Michèle, who was born in 1946. My mother, an orphan of his father, had never been able to mourn him. We had to share his sadness by avoiding speaking too loudly and showing too much joy. My narration concerns the advance engagement of my grandfather, the military course in the Far East, in Africa, the letters of contribution which concern the mobilization on the Western front. Joseph Pierre Antoine Eynard falsified his date of birth (20/09/1880) to anticipate a military engagement. In 1900, he was in Beijing, from 1908 to 1910, a soldier in Ivry in France, he went to Lisbon, went to Africa, to Senegal, to Guinea, to the Ivory Coast,... to the Canary Islands. In 1912, after fifteen years of campaigning, he wanted to retire from the military. But during the mobilization on August 2, 1914, he announced his departure with the 42nd colonial infantry regiment, sent various letters to his family, then to his wife Marcellou, whom he had just left. He was killed on September 6, 1914 in Souilly in the Meuse and put in a common grave. An official letter will start the war widow's pension on 02/18/1916. On April 27, 1915, a correspondence with the priest of Souilly explains to my grandmother the impossibility of exhumation. With the wait for confirmation of death, she wonders about the repatriation of the body. She declines it so as not to separate her husband from his companions and "to avoid an error by receiving the remains of bones from an unknown person who could be of color". This is why my grandfather's body remained at the National Necropolis of Rembercourt-aux-Pots.

RELATED MATERIALS
A photograph of my soldier grandfather (1910) 4 letters 4 cards

title: Mes Souvenirs pendant mon Emigrage | Memorial diary of the relocation from Troissy (Bouquigny) Marne to Maizières la Grande Paroisse dans l'Aube
text:

Mes Souvenirs pendant mon Emigrage | Memorial diary of the relocation from Troissy (Bouquigny) Marne to Maizières la Grande Paroisse dans l'Aube

METADATA
Title: Mes Souvenirs pendant mon Emigrage | Memorial diary of the relocation from Troissy (Bouquigny) Marne to Maizières la Grande Paroisse dans l'Aube
Record type: TEXT
Places: De Troissy (Bouquigny) Marne à Maizières-la-Grande-Paroisse (Aube)
Subjects: World War I, Transport
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Here are my memories during my emigration with my family from May 1918 to November 1918 from Troissy (Bouquigny) in the Marne valley to Maizières la Grande Paroisse in Aube. My name is Gilbert BAUDIN. I was born on September 5, 1903 in Chèzy-sur-Marne (Aisne). I wrote the original document "My Memories during my Emigration" from the beginning of 1919 to October 1923 (15 and a half to 20 years old). These memories recount the adventures experienced by many inhabitants of the Marne region, which became the scene of battles, to take refuge in the Aube. Being almost illegible, I copy them clean, respecting the spelling errors and those of "French", in order to preserve their "flavor" and their "childish" style. I am now 71 years old. (February 1975)

RELATED MATERIALS
1 PowerPoint document: My memories during my Emigration 1918.pps 1 PowerPoint document: My memories during my Emigration_recopie.pps

title: Livret militaire Auguste Ruckebusch | military pass book of Auguste Ruckebusch
text:
TITLE
Livret militaire Auguste Ruckebusch | military pass book of Auguste Ruckebusch

METADATA
Title: Livret militaire Auguste Ruckebusch | military pass book of Auguste Ruckebusch
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1921
Places: Haubourdin
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
I entrust to you the military booklet of my paternal grandfather Auguste Ruckebusch living in 1914 Haubourdin rue de l'abattoir. He was mobilized on August 13, 1915. In his absence, his wife Marie Knockaert had to leave her two sons at home, to keep the house and the business of her employers, who left during the evacuation. She had taken her five-year-old daughter, Marguerite. Haubourdin was very close to the front. My grandparents' house was requisitioned by the German army. Three German soldiers were staying there with the two boys, Jules, thirteen years old, and Maurice (my father), eleven years old. My grandfather came back physically unscathed, but very shocked and morally transformed. He had become taciturn and sometimes even violent whereas by nature he had previously been gentle and cheerful.

title: FRAD067-206 Quelques bribes du parcours de Victor Heymes (La Wantzenau, Bas-Rhin, 1889-1979) (armée allemande) | Postcards from Victor Heymes
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-206 Quelques bribes du parcours de Victor Heymes (La Wantzenau, Bas-Rhin, 1889-1979) (armée allemande) | Postcards from Victor Heymes

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-206 Quelques bribes du parcours de Victor Heymes (La Wantzenau, Bas-Rhin, 1889-1979) (armée allemande) | Postcards from Victor Heymes
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1916, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Marcel Heymes, his eldest son (1912-2013), was perhaps not unaware of why Victor Heymes (1889-1979) lacked a finger joint. His grandson never knew, reduced to imagining that he could have been mutilated during the First World War. But neither the 4 postcards that he kept, nor the consultation, in the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin (classification mark 844 D 164), of Victor Heymes' individual Veteran's file, provide an answer on this subject. .. Private, mobilized from August 2, 1914 to November 11, 1919 (sic), Victor Heymes was admitted to the benefit of the combatant's retirement in April 1940, after having asserted his participation in the fighting between the Narjowka and Zlota-Lipa rivers (Ukraine), from November 6 to 14, 1916 (then belonging to 5. Komp. Res. Infanterie Regt 5), in front of Jakobstadt and Kreuzburg (Latvia (?)), from July 2 to November 9, 1917, and in front of Reims, from 15 November to December 1, 1917 (then belonging to 3. Komp. Infanterie Regt 400). The two dated postcards that have come down to us, addressed by him on 07/24/1916 to his wife Sophie, and received by him from an acquaintance writing from Kilstett (Bas-Rhin) on 04/26/1918, tell us nothing more, except that he was stationed on this last date, with the 5. Komp. 1 Ers. Batl. Infantry Reg. 161, in Düren (North Rhine-Westphalia). Snippets of a journey that would require, to be better understood, additional investigations...

RELATED MATERIALS
4 postcards or photo cards.

title: FRAD067-208 Le "petit gas" René Rodier demande pardon à son père (1915) | The little René apologizes to his father
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-208 Le "petit gas" René Rodier demande pardon à son père (1915) | The little René apologizes to his father

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-208 Le "petit gas" René Rodier demande pardon à son père (1915) | The little René apologizes to his father
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The surname and first name are too common for the little René Rodier, probably born around 1910, to have been able to identify, especially since this is an isolated postcard within a vast thematic collection. . We bet that the posting of the military registers of the classes concerned by the First World War will soon make it possible to remedy this, by providing, to begin with, details on his father Auguste Rodier, on the date of December 8, 1915 corporal in the "70th territorial, 4th Company, 4th Labor Battalion". As it stands, the forgiveness requested, in a still hesitant writing, by the child from his father, shows in a particularly sensitive way the moments of discouragement of these mothers who were sometimes left alone to educate their children with authority.

title: FRAD067-114 Papiers militaires d'Anton Steyer, né à Lemberg (Moselle) en 1875 (armée allemande) | Miltary documents, postcards, photographs and prayer book from Anton Steyer
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-114 Papiers militaires d'Anton Steyer, né à Lemberg (Moselle) en 1875 (armée allemande) | Miltary documents, postcards, photographs and prayer book from Anton Steyer

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-114 Papiers militaires d'Anton Steyer, né à Lemberg (Moselle) en 1875 (armée allemande) | Miltary documents, postcards, photographs and prayer book from Anton Steyer
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1897, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, English, eng, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
From his maternal grandfather, Anton Steyer, Mr. Eugène Brisbois has kept the complete military papers, as well as a Feldgesangbuch, a Catholic prayer booklet adapted to all military situations (entirely digitized). Mobilized on 03/08/1914 to the 1. Landsturm Bataillon Bitsch 1. Kompagnie, Anton Steyer (who had already served from 15/10/1897 to 30/09/1899) was attached to the 4. Kompagnie of the same Battalion on 17/ 01/1915. From 20/02 (according to his Militärdienstzeitbescheinigung of 1931) or from 14/04 (according to his Militärpass) to 16/07/1915, he took part in the battles fought on the Bzura river, near Kawka (Poland), and from 17 on 07/22/1915, in the fighting in front of Warsaw. He was probably injured there, since he was referred on 07/22/1915 to a military hospital. Anton Steyer moved on 19/08/1915 to 2. Kompagnie 10. Landsturm Infanterie Ersatz Battalion Magdeburg, then on 14/09/1915 to Landsturm Infanterie Battalion 1. Hagenau, with 4. Kompagnie from which he was again assigned, from 26 /09/1915 to 08/15/1918, on the eastern front. Because of his age, he entered the military administration on this date (Militärverwaltung Litauen, Bezirk Süd, Militär Kreisamt Bialystok Stadt: Lithuania/Poland). He was still exercising these functions when he was sent back to his homes on 18/11/1918.

RELATED MATERIALS
Photograph and driving certificate dating back to Anton Steyer's first period of service (1897-1899); military booklets (Militärpass and Soldbuch); photo card received from his brother (27/05/1916, Bischwiller (Bas-Rhin), 1. Rekr. Depot 2. Ersatz Battalion Infanterie R. 138); photo card sent to his wife (married in 1907) and his 3 children (27/02/1917, Russia); family photography (nd); fighter card; Feldgesangbuch (64 pages)

title: Mon grand-père paternel poilu de 14-18 | Charles Georges Richard Pauquet's Pictures from the War
text:
TITLE
Mon grand-père paternel poilu de 14-18 | Charles Georges Richard Pauquet's Pictures from the War

METADATA
Title: Mon grand-père paternel poilu de 14-18 | Charles Georges Richard Pauquet's Pictures from the War
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Montdidier
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My paternal grandfather Charles Georges Richard PAUQUET, known as Georges Pauquet, was born in 1880 in the Somme, near Montdidier. Coming from a very modest background (although natural son of the village squire), he did an apprenticeship as a salesman in a Parisian department store, then became a sales representative. Married at the age of 25 in 1905 to Marie Louise Baubillier (whose brother, a thermal engineer, was one of his friends) he successively settled in several towns. In 1914, he lived in Clermont-Ferrand where my grandmother had taken over a haberdashery business overlooking Place de Jaude; the couple had two daughters, then aged 5 and 7. My grandfather was mobilized and took part in many battles, including the terrible battle of the Chemin des Dames in 1917. When the battle began in April 1917, my grandmother was 7 months pregnant, a pregnancy conceived while on leave but unfortunately unwanted. On June 1, she gave birth to a boy (my father died in 2004). The birth took place in Paris rue d'Alésia in an apartment rented for the occasion because my grandfather did not want to give birth to an "Auvergnat". I don't know if my grandfather was present that day, or if he had to wait for the next leave to meet his third child. A child who 21 years later was in turn mobilized, then taken prisoner by the Germans in 1940. Source of new anxieties for the generation of those who had believed that 14-18 would be "the last of the last". My grandfather died in 1960.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photo top left: my grandfather (cross) and other soldiers are resting in front of the entrance to a trench, smoking a pipe or a cigarette. Photo taken in 1914 or early 1915 because they wear kepis and not helmets. you will notice the puttees and the big shoes. - Photo (cropped) in the center: another moment of relaxation, freshening up in front of the drinking trough, my grandfather in the background, same dates. - Photo bottom left: the same on the beach in Cherbourg - Photo top right: he is obviously posing for the photographer while scanning the sky with binoculars (enemy plane in sight?) - Photo bottom right: confers with his comrades in the shelter of a hedge of fir trees

title: FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story
text:
TITLE
FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story

METADATA
Title: FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
House painter, Marcel Jean Eugène Migot is the son of Victor Edouard Migot and Clémence Anne Fougerol. Married to Germaine Berthe Filon, he has a daughter, Nicole Migot. Called on April 12, 1915 in Vannes (Meucon camp), he volunteered to join the front with the 116th infantry regiment, within the 29th Company. He was hospitalized twice: the first time, in Toulouse, from September 16 to December 20, 1916, following a concussion, and the second time, in Chateaudun, for a wound made in the thorax, when he fought at the Chemin des Ladies. Although he was seriously injured, he nevertheless returned to the front in 1918, once his convalescence was complete. His courage is rewarded with numerous decorations: Legion of Honor, military medal, war cross with palms (2 citations), combatant's cross, Verdun medal, Saint-Mihiel medal, medal of the Survivors of Aisne, medal of the wounded, silver and bronze medal of the voluntary combatants. After his return from the front, he wrote a lot about the war. Some of his texts are kept in the municipal archives of Saint-Denis. Others have been published, in particular Le Ravin de Veaudoine (in: Journal of the voluntary combatants). He also participated in the collective work Verdun: history of the fights which were fought from 1914 to 1918, on both banks of the Meuse, directed by Jacques Péricard and published in 1933.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photographs - "Logbook" = story between 1915 and 1918 (one of which was published in a newspaper) - Certificate of good conduct - Volunteer combatant card - Identification and services report - Copy of a clinical "report"

Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story by Michel PETIT about his great-uncle, Justin GONNET and his maternal grandfather, Claudien GONNET. Son of Jean Marie and Marie GONNET, née CHOPELIN, Claudien and Justin GONNET both participated in the First World War. Justin, born on August 6, 1885, worked as a servant. Matricule 57 of the 1905 class of the Mâcon office, he was successively assigned to the 17th, 109th and 152nd infantry regiments. Wounded by shrapnel in 1915 in Reillon (Meurthe-et-Moselle), Justin, proposed for medical reform, died of Addison's disease on March 22, 1918. His brother Claudien, born May 22, 1889, practiced the farming profession. Matricule 132 of the 1909 class of the Mâcon office, Claudien, was cited in the order of the 21st battalion of foot hunters (n° 529 of April 29, 1918) and was decorated with the Croix de guerre with bronze star.

STORY / CONTEXT
Testimony collected from Cédric Level, as part of the collection operation of November 16, 2013, concerning his great-grandfather. Born on October 15, 1885 in Rivière, he has 2 brothers (Julien and Justin), carpenter, he marries Julienne. He did his military service in Amiens and was mobilized in the 272nd infantry in Pas de Calais. On September 10, 1914, he died during the Battle of the Marne at the Petite Perte farm southwest of Vitry-le-François. He leaves fatherless a 2 year old son, his wife recovered the body and a small box containing his effects. He was buried in the Rivière cemetery in the 1920s.

title: FRAM - Xavier Cambon, un observateur photographe | Documents from Xavier Cambon
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Xavier Cambon, un observateur photographe | Documents from Xavier Cambon

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Xavier Cambon, un observateur photographe | Documents from Xavier Cambon
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1818
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Xavier Cambon, the contributor's grandfather, was born in Bône in Algeria on August 8, 1892. He works as a wheelwright. Mobilized, he was incorporated into the 10th foot artillery regiment then moved to the 6th African artillery group (ALGP). He chose to serve in aviation and joined the aerial shooting school of Cazaux (Gironde). In July 1916, he obtained his combat gunner's certificate and continued his training at the Pau aviation school. When he left, he was assigned to the observation and photography squadron MF 45, until November 1917. He carried out 38 missions there for a total of 43 flying hours. He was appointed to the rank of brigadier in March 1917. A pilot student in Istre, he obtained his military aviation license in March 1918, then went to combat school. He was demobilized on March 15, 1919. Mentioned in the order of the division in 1917, he was honored with the military medal and named Chevalier of the Legion of Honor.

RELATED MATERIALS
1. Photograph of Xavier Cambon in aviator's uniform (nd). 2. Photograph of Xavier Cambon aboard an airplane (nd). 3. Logbook (1916-1918). 4. Mention in Order of the Division (1917).

STORY / CONTEXT
Field postcards, photos, war propaganda postcards and other documents of unknown origin. Most of the correspondence comes from the brothers Ernst and Hermann Büscher from the years 1914 and 1915. Both of them either inform each other about their condition or they write to their mother in Gelsenkirchen. Ernst Büscher writes to his brother, Landwehrmann Hermann Büscher, from a military hospital in Wachenheim in the Palatinate that he was "injured by a grazing shot on the right shoulder blade 10 km from Longwy" on August 24, 1914. Ernst was then stationed as a hunter in Hirschberg in Silesia, including with the 2nd company of the 2nd Reserve Battalion Jäger No. 5, and was released on June 1, 1918 as Oberjäger. Hermann Büscher was also on the French western front with the 7th reserve company of the 56th Infantry Regiment.

RELATED MATERIALS
Numerous photos, documents and field postcards, including a certificate of award for wearing the Iron Cross 2nd Class of Corporal Borchert dated May 15, 1918.

title: FRAM - Quatre frères au combat | Four brothers in Great War
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Quatre frères au combat | Four brothers in Great War

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Quatre frères au combat | Four brothers in Great War
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: English, Français, fra, eng
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My maternal grandfather Pierre Le Grand (1878-1964) and his three brothers, Julien, Louis and Yves, fought in the 1914-1918 war. Pierre Le Grand was born in 1881, he did three years of military service to which was added the four years of war between 1914 and 1918. He served in a cavalry regiment and was sent to the eastern front at Santi Quaranta (Albania, opposite Corfu) and Salonika (Greece). Yves Le Grand, was mobilized in 1914. He joined the 162nd infantry regiment. At the beginning of April 1917, this regiment, part of the 69th infantry division, was positioned in the Berry-au-Bac sector, east of the Chemin des Dames, between the Aisne and its tributary on the right bank, the Crumb. On April 16, 1917, the regiment was on the front line during the outbreak of General Nivelle's offensive. Seriously injured, Yves Le Grand was evacuated to the field hospital in Prouilly (Marne), west of Reims. He died there as a result of his injuries at the age of 30, on April 25, 1917 and was buried in Prouilly. From April 16 to 25, 1917, the Nivelle offensive caused the death of 30,000 French soldiers, without result. In 1920, the family will transfer the remains of Yves Le Grand to bury him in the family vault in Lochrist. His name is engraved on the monument to the dead of Inzinzac-Lochrist as well as the memorial of Sainte-Anne-d'Auray in Morbihan. Julien le Grand was part of the 3rd Artillery Regiment on foot.

RELATED MATERIALS
Photographs, military booklets, Yves Le Grand's correspondence, army correspondence, mobilization booklet, information sheet, Julien Le Grand's notebook and photographs, identification medallion.

STORY / CONTEXT
My father Jean-Baptiste Potier, born February 18, 1896 and died December 1, 1966, was a farmer in Saint-Jean la Poterie (Morbihan). He did Belgium, Verdun, the Chemin des Dames, the Somme. He was gassed and wounded on October 2, 1916, by shrapnel to the cheek in the attack on Combles at Bois Louage.

title: L' affaire du Bonnet d'évêque, le 11 décembre 1914 | The Bishop's Bonnet Affair, December 11, 1914 the Life of Edmond Schaeffer
text:
TITLE
L' affaire du Bonnet d'évêque, le 11 décembre 1914 | The Bishop's Bonnet Affair, December 11, 1914 the Life of Edmond Schaeffer

METADATA
Title: L' affaire du Bonnet d'évêque, le 11 décembre 1914 | The Bishop's Bonnet Affair, December 11, 1914 the Life of Edmond Schaeffer
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1900
Places: SAINT JULIEN ( BELGIQUE )
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The action takes place during the 1st Battle of Ypres ( YEPER ) or Battle of Flanders. Edmond SCHAEFFER left Alsace for PARIS in 1900. He worked at the Alsace Pavilion at the Universal Exhibition, then accompanied a boss around the world. On November 12, 1910, he married Julienne PARIS. From this union will be born on March 10, 1912, Georgette who will be my mother in 1941, and I will have 3 brothers and sister. 1914, Edmond enlists voluntarily in PARIS: private 2nd class in the 153rd RI - 2nd Battalion - 12th Company - registration number 9085 in the Corps. In August 1914 his regiment left TOUL. After numerous engagements and "the race to the sea", the 153rd Infantry took part in the Battle of Flanders from November 3...and on December 11, Edmond was reported missing in a fight between YPRES and POELKAPPELLE: the 153rd Infantry lost 9 officers and more than 700 men... On the scene of the fighting, at the WINCHESTER FARM cited by the military as "the farm in Bishop's Bonnet" (name probably due to the shape of its land observed on the maps), there are 83 dead soldiers including 80 from the 153rd RI, Edmond SCHAEFFER is not mentioned, but appears in the list of the disappeared on December 11, 1914 in the JMO of the 153rd RI It is in the archives of my parents that I discover the handwritten document attached: a 9-page story, written the day after this fight, on December 12, 1914, in Saint-Jean, suburb of YPRES, probably by an officer of the 20°/2 Engineers who accompanied several squads in reinforcement of 2 battalions of the 153rd RI My grandmother will not be officially informed until mid -January 1921 of the death of her husband, Mort pour la France, killed by the enemy in SAINT-JULIEN in BELGIUM. My mother was about to be 10 years old. She received her father's decorations: Croix de Guerre and Military Medal. The photo of their display is attached to this text: in the medallion watch the portraits of her father and hers...Julienne and her daughter had been uncertain of Edmond's return for 8 years. My mother told us that every night of her life, her mother had waited for Edmond to return. The body has never been identified. Edmond had left his native Alsace, then annexed by Germany, and had told his wife when he enlisted in the Army that if he was about to be taken prisoner, he would get rid of all documents and plates allowing his identification and that he would simulate amnesia if necessary...because he feared that the Germans would shoot him for desertion... A war later, in Alsace then reoccupied by Germany, a sister of Edmond , saw his son-in-law - the husband of his only daughter, my godmother - cross the Rhine to the Russian front, where he will be taken prisoner. One of the many "despite us" who had the chance to return home at the Liberation "with only luggage... a handkerchief". Sportsman, robust, nurse in a psychiatric hospital, he died at the age of 63... My maternal grandmother, whom I could have known as a baby, died in an asylum in Haute-Saône on November 12, 1942, certainly of "malnutrition ", like tens of thousands of mentally ill people. She was only 60 years old..THE STORY HAS NO END...

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photos of a wooden frame forming a display, containing the military decorations of our grandfather Edmond SCHAEFFER and a medallion representing him on one side, and on the other, the portrait of his daughter Georgette BISCHOFF, née SCHAEFFER - The photos of the 11 pages of the manuscript relating the fight of SAINT-JULIEN. - A photo of a map of the northeast of the YPRES region mentioning the WINCHESTER FARM. - Research sheet, made by a friend: MC LAMAISON. - Wedding photo of Edmond and Julienne SCHAEFFER - September 12, 1910. - Death certificate of Edmond SCHAEFFER.

Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean-Marie Georges Marvoyer was born on June 22, 1894 in Paris. He was a hat maker in his father's business. He fought on the Western Front in the 119th and 131st Infantry Regiments. He was wounded in the left shoulder blade on June 9, 1916 in Fleury by shrapnel which tore his military booklet. Demobilized in September 1919, he worked at the Palais de Justice in Paris. He died in Sanary in 1968. He spoke little about the war, but told his granddaughter that the work "Les Croix de bois" by Roland Dorgelès corresponded to what he had experienced.

RELATED MATERIALS
Photograph Military booklet torn by shrapnel Military booklet (duplicate)

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
François Henri Morisset, paternal great uncle of the contributor, was born in Paris on April 6, 1870. Portraitist, painter of landscapes and still lifes, pupil of Gustave Moreau, he has been a member of French Artists since 1893. He exhibited in many salons : Salon of French Artists, Salon de la Nationale, Salon d'Automne and Salon des Tuileries. Mobilized, he fought on the front. He produced several paintings and watercolors on the subject of the Great War. In 1919, his painting entitled "Le Pépère" representing a soldier in his trench was exhibited at the Grand Palais. He died on July 7, 1958 in Paris.

RELATED MATERIALS
1. Watercolor representing a soldier in the middle of ruins (1916). 2. Watercolor depicting wood fetching in winter in the Apremont forest (1917). 3. Sketch of the painting “Le Pèpère” (nd).

title: FRAD066_098b Léon Bultel | Documents from Léon Bultel

TITLE
FRAD066_098b Léon Bultel | Documents from Léon Bultel

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Léon Bultel is from Seine-Maritime. He is a teacher, like his wife Antoinette. Their only son, Jean, was born in October 1914, when Léon was already in the army: he would meet his son during a leave of absence in the summer of 1915. Léon Bultel was a second lieutenant. He served on the eastern front of France, he participated in particular in the Battle of the Marne. He is stationed in the trenches of this sector. He writes, while he is at the front, poems which are carefully compiled in a notebook. His favorite themes are his wife, his son, but also the war and his daily life. The last of the poems in the collection is dated September 16, 1915: it is entitled "Avenir". Léon Bultel was probably killed a few days after writing it. It was his comrades who gave his wife his notebook of poems.

RELATED MATERIALS
Collection of poems by Léon Bultel (selection); photograph of Léon Bultel with his wife and son.

title: FRBMBX-058 - Fernand Caron, le courage d'un soldat ordinaire | Fernand Caron, a brave simple soldier
text:
TITLE
FRBMBX-058 - Fernand Caron, le courage d'un soldat ordinaire | Fernand Caron, a brave simple soldier

METADATA
Title: FRBMBX-058 - Fernand Caron, le courage d'un soldat ordinaire | Fernand Caron, a brave simple soldier
Record type: TEXT
Places: Heilbronn, Allemagne
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Fernand Caron enthusiastically opens his campaign diary on August 3, 1914. He writes regularly, his story becomes like a daily ritual. A curious isolated double page in the notebook announces his will, written on August 15, 1914. Few pages are filled in this notebook: Fernand Caron sees his brother killed on September 6, the same day he is wounded in the arm and stomach. He died as a result of his injuries on November 29, 1914. His family only had more information when a certain R. Watel, a friend from the Heilbronn hospital, sent them the meager possessions of the deceased. In this sober and moving letter, Watel recounts the last days of Fernand Caron. We read there both the solidarity of his comrades, and the courage of Fernand Caron whom Watel describes as "admirable".

RELATED MATERIALS
- Campaign notebook: first entry - Campaign notebook: testament - Letter from R. Watel to the family of Fernand Caron (17/04/1919). Account of hospitalization and death.

title: FRAD008_001 Une famille d'Hannogne-Saint-Martin dans la guerre | Documents from Genesseau family
text:
TITLE
FRAD008_001 Une famille d'Hannogne-Saint-Martin dans la guerre | Documents from Genesseau family

METADATA
Title: FRAD008_001 Une famille d'Hannogne-Saint-Martin dans la guerre | Documents from Genesseau family
Record type: TEXT
Places: Hannogne-Saint-Martin
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Henri Genesseau was mayor of Hannogne-Saint-Martin in 1914. He had three sons: the two eldest born in 1882, Charles and René, were twins, the youngest André was born in 1884. A former baker, Henri left the business to his eldest son Charles, married to Marthe and father of a little Madeleine, nicknamed Mad, born in 1910. During the mobilization, the three sons each joined an infantry regiment, while the parents remained in the country. A baker stays with the father to run the bakery, Marthe and Mad evacuate on their side to Fère-Champenoise, in the Marne with Marthe's family. The fate of this family is told day by day through the diary of the father of the family Henri, the notebook kept by Charles in combat and Charles's letters to his wife and daughter. In the Ardennes, Henri notices little by little the hardening of the living conditions and the pressure of the occupier. He thus explains that the population comes further and further away in order to stock up on bread. He has no news of his sons, the mail does not pass through the occupied Ardennes. The sound of the cannon is omnipresent in the distance, the rumors coming from Saint-Menges, Sedan or Mouzon are by turns alarmist or encouraging. From August 23, 1914 to January 31, 1915, he recounts the beginnings of the war, ending with: "Today we hear nothing and we don't even talk about the war anymore", as if the inhabitants were inexorably getting used to this life. In these letters, Charles questions his wife many times about the fate that may have been reserved for her parents, but it is impossible to get an answer. Almost every day, he writes reassuring words to his wife, about her good health, good food at the back and shows a lot of tenderness for his little Mad, nicknamed the "butt end". Granted, he also explains that he feels like a badger in the trenches, with a growing beard and hair, a dread of the rain, and cravings for packages with warm clothes and candy. For a time, he became a cook, cooking for the front lines. Quartered in Argonne near Sainte-Ménehould, he is not so far from his wife and hopes for permission to go see her. This permission will never come. In September 1914, he learned of the death in combat of his twin brother, René, who left a pregnant wife. Then from January 1915, he fell ill, Marthe's letters remained unanswered until a priest sent her a letter announcing the death of her husband on January 18, 1915, following typhoid fever at the Valmy hospital in Sainte-Ménehould. He also sends her a notebook found in her haversack, recounting her daily life. André, the youngest, continues to write to his sister-in-law. He became the father of a little René who is doing wonderfully and whom he has only seen in pictures. In June 1915, in a letter addressed to Marthe, he was happy to have finally received news from his parents back home and wondered “how long this cursed war will last”…

RELATED MATERIALS
Diary kept by Henri Genesseau. Notebook found in the bag of Charles Genesseau at his death. Correspondence exchanged between Charles and his wife Marthe.

title: Gefreiter Fritz Düngel, Bursche von Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth | Fritz Düngel under the command of Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth
text:
TITLE

METADATA
Title: Gefreiter Fritz Düngel, Bursche von Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth | Fritz Düngel under the command of Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1919
Places: Billy, Frankreich
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The recruit Fritz Düngel from Oberhausen served 1912-1914 with the 5th Lorraine Infantry Regiment No. 144 in Metz. In the course of mobilization, his regiment became part of the newly created 33rd Division. The private became the personal assistant to staff officer Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth and followed him to various staffs during the war: Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth was chief of the management department (Ia) on the staff of the 33rd Reserve Division (including in Billy), on the general staff of the Army High Command II (AOK II) in St. Quentin and later in Le Cateau and in 1918 at the General Staff of the IX. Army Corps in Marchè. The album with photos from Metz and the various locations also contains field postcards to "his young lady" Gertrud Dötsch in Oberhausen, to his father Friedrich Düngel in Oberhausen and messages from his "friend Heinrich".

RELATED MATERIALS
Album with field postcards and photos by Fritz Düngel. Depicted in the photos are, inter alia, Major Wilhelm v. Ditfurth, Grand Duke of Baden at the troop parade in the Billy Gorge on July 7th, 1915, Major vd Busche, several unnamed generals and the locations: Metz, Billy, Combres, Havrincourt Castle, Longeville, St. Quentin, Le Cateau.

title: FRAD083_41_Joseph Emile Bournadet | Joseph Emile Bournadet, french soldier
text:
TITLE
FRAD083_41_Joseph Emile Bournadet | Joseph Emile Bournadet, french soldier

Title: FRAD083_41_Joseph Emile Bournadet | Joseph Emile Bournadet, french soldier
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Joseph Emile Bournadet was born on August 5, 1882 in Cusset in Alier. He was a notary in Donzy in the Nièvre. During the war of 14-18, he was second lieutenant, chief bomber, commanding a squad of volunteers in the 95th infantry regiment. He had a strong patriotic feeling. He wrote letters regularly to his wife Marguerite Bournadet, née Berthelier. He died at Bois brûlé in the Apremont forest on April 8, 1915. He had just learned that he was a father for the second time. Jacques Péricard is the author of the book "Debout les Morts" and in volume 2 "Red Easter" (Paris, Editions Payot, 1919), he recounts the circumstances of the death of Mr. Joseph Emile Bournadet which he attended. He received the Legion of Honor and the Cross of Military Valour.

RELATED MATERIALS
Letters from Joseph Emile Bournadet written in the trenches to his wife Marguerite, the last of which remains unfinished. A photograph of Joseph Bournadet.

title: FRAD078-01_1 Léon Ottenheim, la passion de la photographie | Léon Ottenheim, fond of photography
text:
TITLE
FRAD078-01_1 Léon Ottenheim, la passion de la photographie | Léon Ottenheim, fond of photography

METADATA
Title: FRAD078-01_1 Léon Ottenheim, la passion de la photographie | Léon Ottenheim, fond of photography
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
"My great-grandfather, Léon Ottenheim, was passionate about photography, and these glass plate photos are of him. These photos of the city of Reims were taken in December 1918. Indeed, his son (my grandfather ), was a reserve officer, mobilized in 1914 as an artilleryman; having perhaps participated in the destruction of this city, he wanted to keep traces. We can see him in the photos, accompanied by his mother. The family photographs have also taken by my great-grandfather, during leave from my grandfather. They were taken in Normandy, at Corneville sur Risle. We see my grandfather with his wife Lucie, and their two daughters, Madeleine (my mother), and Monique. I inherited these glass plates from my family. I remember well my grandfather, who every 1st of the year, made us take a break for a photography session. I don't never discussed the Great War with him."

RELATED MATERIALS
Glass plates and their boxes, presenting the ruins of the city of Reims after the war, as well as family photos.

STORY / CONTEXT
Testimony collected from Marie-Paule Bontems, as part of the Great Collection operation on November 16, 2013, about her maternal grandfather Ernest Joseph Gaeremynck. "Knew by his calm and composure to ensure the supply of ammunition to his team despite the most violent bombardments with asphyxiating and large-caliber shells. perilous." AD Nord, Lille office, class 1910, registration number 3218. He told his granddaughter an episode that had particularly saddened him: the death of his horse, to which he was particularly attached, following the shooting of a shell at Verdun.

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. Charles Jaffard was born in Clermont-Ferrand on June 17, 1884. Career soldier. Enlisted in 1902. He fought against Germany from August 2, 1914 until October 23, 1919. He then occupied Rhine territories until July 1, 1920. He was wounded for the first time on September 2, 1914 by a burst of shells and in January 1915. Promoted to adjutant on October 10, 1914 then lieutenant on May 3, 1919. He received several decorations: military medal (September 22, 1914), war cross, knight of the Legion of Honor and three citations. He died in 1963.

RELATED MATERIALS
Citations, medals, postcard from his regiment

STORY / CONTEXT
Documents from Mme Coiffe, niece of soldier Jean Martin Mousnier, born October 28, 1893 in Châteauneuf-la-Forêt (Haute-Vienne). These three postcards addressed to his sister Jeanne by Jean Martin Mousnier, of the 169th infantry regiment, bear witness to the daily life of combatants in the trenches, the mud in which one sinks up to one's knees, mortar fire or the arrival of reinforcements. All date from February 1915. Jean Martin Mousnier died a few months later, on May 30, 1915, in Bois-le-Prêtre (Meurthe-et-Moselle).

RELATED MATERIALS
Three postcards addressed by Jean Martin Mousnier to his sister Jeanne (1915)

STORY / CONTEXT
Charles PINAUD (1883-1964) was mobilized in 1914 and served four years of war. During the Battle of the Marne, he contracted typhoid. He also did Verdun. He's a terrific war photographer. An insurer by profession, he resumed this profession after the conflict. Married, he had three children (two girls and a boy)

Testimony collected from Alain Claude Debombourg, as part of the Great Collection operation of November 16, 2013, concerning his maternal grandfather Charles Louis: Charles Louis was born in 1896 (he had origins in Lyon and Piedmont). Artillery non-commissioned officer under the orders of Pétain in Verdun. He belonged to the 37th Army Corps, 8th Battery, 3rd Gun. He made 38 firing positions according to what we can read in the document. Charles Louis didn't really tell the stories of the Great War.

STORY / CONTEXT
Robert Édouard DEPLANCK (1884-1915) was mobilized as a captain in the 2nd colonial infantry regiment. He died killed by the enemy on September 25, 1915 in Douais (Marne). Active officer, he had made Saint-Cyr, then had gone to Indochina. He was long missing.

Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Pierre CODERC is the maternal grandfather of Marguerite AUBERT. He was a farmer in Thuir. A soldier in the 126th territorial regiment, he fought at Epinal in the Vosges. He was gassed during the war and died of multiple sclerosis at his home in Thuir in 1929.

RELATED MATERIALS
A photograph of Marguerite AUBERT's maternal grandparents taken one day on leave from Pierre CODERC; a postcard dated September 11, 1916 addressed to his sister-in-law in which he gave his news and thanked her for his package.

title: AMSM-019_2 : Marius et Léon JAYNE | Marius and Léon Jayne at the Front
text:
TITLE
AMSM-019_2 : Marius et Léon JAYNE | Marius and Léon Jayne at the Front

METADATA
Title: AMSM-019_2 : Marius et Léon JAYNE | Marius and Léon Jayne at the Front
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914
Places: Béthincourt
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Marius and Léon JAYNE are the two brothers of my grandmother Caroline JAYNE. Both died for France at the front. I have no memories because my aunts Louise and Joséphine were too upset to talk about it. On the site "Memory of Men", I present to you Marius and Léon JAYNE. Marius Julien Séraphin JAYNE born on January 27, 1891 in Ollioules, was a sergeant in the 1st company, 173rd Infantry Regiment, died for France on April 24, 1915 in Bois Buchot (Meuse) killed by the enemy. His brother Léon Jean JAYNE born on August 21, 1892 in Ollioules, was 2nd class in the 3rd Infantry Regiment, died for France on September 20, 1914 in Béthincourt (Meuse) missing in action. I had a photo of my two great-uncles during their class in Bonifacio (Corsica) then also a correspondence between the parents and their son, unfortunately when reading these letters, Marius died 3 days after his letter and Léon died 8 days later his last letter. After the return of a letter to one of their sons, my desperate great-grandparents speak in German to the enemy General Staff to have news of their sons at the front. The disappearance of my great-uncles was still a shock by these sad facts, which is why we didn't talk about it in my maternal family. See the story of Pierre IMBERT, Louise JAYNE

RELATED MATERIALS
- 1 photo of the two JAYNE brothers in Bonifacio during their military class - 4 letters
