STORY / CONTEXT
Amédée Desforges, the contributor's grandfather, was born on December 8, 1891 in Beauzée-sur-Aire in the Meuse. Mobilized, he was incorporated into the non-ranking company of the 165th infantry regiment, with the rank of corporal at the post of telephone operator. He was wounded at Verdun in December 1914, then at the Laffaut mill in the Aisne in September 1918. Mentioned in the order of the regiment, he was decorated with the Croix de Guerre and French and Belgian military medals.

RELATED MATERIALS
1. Photograph of Amédée Desforges (seated) with two regimental comrades (nd). 2. Mention in the order of the regiment (1918) 3. Diploma commemorating the battles of Verdun (1917) 4. Veteran's kepi plate (?).

title: Ledermappe mit Fotos und Dokumenten von Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck | Leathercase with photos and documents of Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck
text:
TITLE
Ledermappe mit Fotos und Dokumenten von Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck | Leathercase with photos and documents of Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck

METADATA
Title: Ledermappe mit Fotos und Dokumenten von Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck | Leathercase with photos and documents of Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915, 1918
Places: Abguldener See/Lettland
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Wilhelm (Willy) Große Munkenbeck from Bochum-Stiepel (August 23, 1890 - April 23, 1968) probably studied economics in London before the war. It is not known whether he volunteered for military service or was drafted. He was stationed as vice sergeant of the Bavarian Foot Artillery Battery No. 281 in the Lille/France area and later as a pilot in the 1st Royal Bavarian Flieger-Ersatzabteilung 1 in Schweissheim/Bavaria, where he probably stayed until 1918, as did his pilot's license , issued on February 16, 1918. His tasks included above all reconnaissance flights, on which he made numerous aerial photographs of militarily important areas on the western front, especially in France. Wilhelm regularly wrote postcards to his family in Bochum, and at one point during the war, as the family history relates, he found an opportunity to visit his parents in the Ruhr area. During a plane transfer, he threw the following message over the family home in Stiepel: "I'll be having coffee soon" and landed his plane in a nearby stubble field. After the coffee break, the plane was brought back into position with the help of a team of horses and Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck flew back to Munich. During one of his missions, he was wounded and sustained a bullet wound in his leg, but otherwise survived the war physically unharmed. The Grosse Munkenbeck family worked in agriculture. From 1951 until it was closed down in 1965, Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck operated the small colliery "Mit Gott dared" in Bochum. The bundle includes a collection of weapons, numerous photos, medals and uniform parts as well as other documents.

RELATED MATERIALS
(1) small leather folder for storing photos, postcards and other documents. (2) Photos, including with a company, under the Christmas tree, from the "Abguldener See" and various other motifs as well as a field postcard (3) New Year's card and invitation card to an "autumn celebration at 286" on October 26, 1918 (4) Points card from an English card game named "Trump Whist" (5) obituary by unknown

title: FRAD062_107 - Carnel Ulysse, 1911-1916. | FRAD062_107 - Ulysse Carnel, 1911-1919.
text:
TITLE
FRAD062_107 - Carnel Ulysse, 1911-1916. | FRAD062_107 - Ulysse Carnel, 1911-1919.

METADATA
Title: FRAD062_107 - Carnel Ulysse, 1911-1916. | FRAD062_107 - Ulysse Carnel, 1911-1919.
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Testimony collected from Denise Wiccart, as part of the Great Collection of November 16, 2013, about Ulysse Carnel, her maternal great-uncle. Son of a shoemaker from Fruges, Ulysse Carnel enlisted as a volunteer in the army on September 16, 1911 because he did not want to take over the family business. Mobilized on August 2, 1914, he was incorporated with the rank of maréchal des logis in the 32nd battery of the 12th artillery regiment. Seriously wounded on June 30, 1915, he was decorated with the Croix de Guerre. He was killed in action on October 18, 1916 at Combles (Somme). The military medal was awarded to him posthumously in 1921.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 1 citation to the order of the army corps, July 18, 1916. - 1 citation to the order of the brigade, December 1, 1916. - Death notice dated March 15, 1917. - Military booklet. - Artillery notebook. - Letter addressed to his father Jules Carnel concerning the allowances sent following death in action, dated April 15, 1917. - Certificate addressed to his mother Sidonie Carnel concerning the allowances sent following death in action, dated January 9, 1919, following the death of her husband. - Pocket watch returned to the family with Ulysse's personal effects following his death. - 7 photographs.

Five postcards, including four sent from the Vosges from 1914 to 1916 to his family and one dated June 23, 1915 representing a group of German soldiers in which he describes on the back the conditions in which the fighting took place and who probably had to escape to censorship. Three portraits of Charles Mirc in soldier's uniform (1916-1917).

RELATED MATERIALS
Charles MIRC, maternal grandfather of Marie SOLAZ, is from Pia. A winegrower before the war, he became a policeman after the war for fifteen years, then resumed his activity as a winegrower. He fought in the Vosges in the 253rd infantry regiment.

title: FRAD071-007, Charles QUELIN | Documents from Charles Quelin
text:
TITLE
FRAD071-007, Charles QUELIN | Documents from Charles Quelin

METADATA
Title: FRAD071-007, Charles QUELIN | Documents from Charles Quelin
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1915
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Story by Odile Quelin, concerning Charles Quelin, the contributor's uncle. Born on October 16, 1896 in Saint-Point (Saône-et-Loire) and died for France on April 15, 1915 in Commercy (Meuse) as a result of his injuries. Killed with a bayonet, the father recognized the body thanks to a spike in his son's hair. Buried in Saint-Point, the memory of this young man, who died at 19, remained very strong in the family. Coming from a family of farmers, enlisted, private 2nd class in the 56th infantry regiment, he had two brothers and two sisters.

RELATED MATERIALS
A photo, a letter and an extract from the registration number.

title: FRAD043_065 Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel, soldats de la Grande guerre. | Documents about Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel
text:
TITLE
FRAD043_065 Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel, soldats de la Grande guerre. | Documents about Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel

METADATA
Title: FRAD043_065 Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel, soldats de la Grande guerre. | Documents about Louis Jonget, Paul et Simon Borel
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1920, 1914
Places: France
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Dominique and Paule Jonget tell the story of Louis Jonget and Paul and Simon Borel, all three soldiers of the Great War. Dominique keeps the archives of his ancestors and has organized them chronologically. - Louis Jonget is Dominique's grandfather and Paule Jonget's stepfather. Born in 1896, Louis was too young to go to war in 1914. From 1914 to 1916 he served in the Chasseurs-Alpins in Grenoble, as evidenced by his mission order. During the war, it was his widowed mother, Mélanie Jonget, who took charge of the family farrier business on boulevard Saint-Louis in Le Puy. Among Louis' belongings dating from the Great War, the family keeps an embroidered horseshoe which was sewn, like a personalized stripe, on his uniform. By reading his grandfather's war correspondence, we can follow Louis' journey. In 1916, his regiment moved from Bourges to Dijon, then he left for the front in the Somme instead of Alsace which was originally planned according to his writings. Louis is serving as a 75 gun. In the Somme his unit changes location frequently, following the evolution of the front line, he passes through Crèvecœur, Saint-Omer-en-Chaussée, Remiencourt, Hortoy, Villers-Bretonneux, Bray -sur-Somme, Cléry sur Somme, etc. In his correspondence, Louis explains that as soon as he arrived at the front, his Major instructed him on the use of gas masks against asphyxiating gases. In a letter dated August 25, 1916, Louis tells his mother that his unit suffered a gas attack. With humour, Louis certainly embellishes reality so as not to worry him: "the Germans sent us asphyxiating shells, we kept our masks on for more than an hour, it's not worth the pure air of the Mézenc but we are content little here". Louis writes very regularly, almost every day. In a letter of August 20, 1916 to his mother, he describes in detail the situation in the trenches. Two days later in another letter, he evokes the enemy and the artillery in these terms: "We send them something […], also where the infantry advances there are heaps of Boche corpses […] one would become deaf around these pieces [of artillery]". After a month of presence in the Somme, Louis Jonget is injured in Cléry. It was in a letter dated September 6, 1916 written to his mother by Henri Quinqueton, a friend of Louis, that the latter learned that her son was injured: “[Louis] was seriously injured by shrapnel in the left arm. It's okay, he left the position at the back on his own and told his friends to eat what was in his bag. We know from the text of the summons to the order of the regiment, dated February 24, 1919, that Louis was wounded on September 5, 1916 "during a barrage, while depositing ammunition near the gun, by a violent rocket fire directed at the battery”. Louis was first treated at the nearest hospital in Amiens, then in Paris. His injury is serious, his shoulder and forearm are atrophied. After that, Louis Jonget no longer returned to the front. From the hospital, Louis returns to the operations he underwent in a letter of September 22, 1916 to his mother. Operated on several times, doctors extracted five shrapnel from his shoulder and arm. During his convalescence in hospital in Paris, Louis Jonget was cared for by Princess Lucinge-Faucigny with whom he maintained a correspondence after the war (see document n°6). Louis Jonget receives the medal and the war cross 1914-1918. After the war, unable to resume the family farriery because of his arm injury, he was behind the creation of the Cooperative des Artisans du Velay in 1920, a company that still exists today. This cooperative first brought together the blacksmiths of the region, then with the development of the profession, the locksmiths. Louis Jonget is well known in Le Puy because he is very involved in local life. From 1936, he was part of the municipal team as deputy mayor. There is an avenue that bears his name in Taulhac in Le Puy-en-Velay, as president of the Haute-Loire Chamber of Trades. - Paul and Simon Borel are two brothers, father and uncle of Paule Jonget and grandfather and great-uncle of Dominique. The Borels are a family of grain and pulse merchants in the Velay region. Their widowed mother took charge of the management of the store on avenue Foch in Le Puy during the war. The two brothers were mobilized from August 2, 1914 until the end of the war. From family memories, it appears that Paul fights among others at Baccarat (battle of August 1914 very costly in men for the 86th infantry regiment, regiment called "du Velay") and Salonika and that Simon goes through the horror of Verdun without being hurt. Neither Louis Jonget nor Paul and Simon Borel mention their situation at the front after the war. If they talk about it at family gatherings, it's only among themselves.

RELATED MATERIALS
1/ Photograph of Louis Jonget in the 1st row seated, second from the left (1914-1916). 2/ Postcard from Louis Jonget to his mother. Louis announces his departure for the front, “the time to go to the front has arrived” (August 3, 1916). 3/ Two postcards from Louis Jonget to his mother. Louis explains to his mother that he is very happy to leave Bourges quickly because normally his unit should have received "an instruction concerning the crapouillots de tranches" (mortars) and go to the front line in stride. Luckily, three batteries asked for reinforcements to serve the 75 guns (Bourges August 4, 1914). 4/ Postcard from Louis Jonget to his mother. Louis describes life in the trenches: "when the guns fire, we have our work, when there is rest we make shelters, we put earth, corrugated iron, all night we receive shells (Somme, August 20, 1916). 5/ Photograph of Louis Jonget in a Parisian hospital, his arm injured, in the company of the Princess Nurse Lucinge-Faucigny. Louis is the 4th from the left. Publication of the magazine Sur le vive (January 20, 1917). 6/ Photograph of Paul and Simon Borel taken by M.-M. Vessiere (Baccarat, 1914).

title: FRAM - Histoire de Georges Chasserot (1891-1978) | Documents from Georges Chasserot
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Histoire de Georges Chasserot (1891-1978) | Documents from Georges Chasserot

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Histoire de Georges Chasserot (1891-1978) | Documents from Georges Chasserot
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1911, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Georges Chasserot, the contributor's father, was born on June 14, 1891 in Granges-les-Plombières in the Vosges. Before the war, he was a farmer. He helps his father in the stone quarry of Granges-les-Plombières. As a young conscript from the 1911 class, he joined the army in October 1912. In April 1913, he joined the 170th infantry regiment. He was quickly promoted to the rank of corporal and then sergeant. Mobilized, he changed regiments several times: the 174th infantry regiment in February 1915, then the 21st infantry regiment in May 1916. Wounded for the first time, June 17, 1915 in Aix-Noulette (Pas-de-Calais) by shrapnel, he stayed in convalescence at Château-Gontier (Mayenne). He returned to combat in January 1917 with the 155th Infantry Regiment. He was injured again after inhaling asphyxiating gas on August 30, 1917 at Haudroment, near Verdun. He was then treated at the Vitry-le-François hospital (Marne). He was wounded one last time and removed from the front from June to September 1918. He ended the war with the rank of lieutenant. Demobilized in September 1919, he enlisted in the army in January 1920. He had a military career which led him notably to Morocco. He was promoted to captain in 1935 before leaving the army in November 1940.

RELATED MATERIALS
1. Photograph of Georges Chasserot at Solesmes in the Sarthe, seated, first from the left (1915). 2. Photograph of Georges Chasserot in military uniform (nd). 3. Mention in Order of the Division (1915). 4. Citation to the Order of the Army Corps (1917). 5. Hospital ticket (1917). 6. X-ray sketch (nd).

title: FRAM - Ernest LORRAIN | Ernest LORRAIN
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Ernest LORRAIN | Ernest LORRAIN

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Ernest LORRAIN | Ernest LORRAIN
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Ernest LORRAIN (1889-1972) was mobilized on August 1, 1914 and engaged in the 5th Cavalry Regiment. This regiment was "dismantled" in 1916 and became a cuirassier on foot. He was also transferred for a few months to the 83rd Regiment (heavy artillery) near Verdun. He was in all the fights (Somme, Champagne, Eparges, Argonne, Verdun, and always at the Front, as close as possible to enemy lines where he also did scouting. His unit radiated at Fort de La Pompelle near Reims, Moreuil, Aisne, Saint-Mihiel, Mézières. His feat of arms at Vaux-les-Palameix (Meuse) earned him a summons to the order of the Division. He had already been cited to the order of the Regiment. He received the Croix de Guerre with Right to carry the Fourragère in the colors of the Croix de Guerre which was awarded to him under the 2nd Foot Cavalry Division commanded by General Hennocque. He also received the military medal on March 16, 1921 and at that time was part of the 19th squadron of the Train des Equipages He married in Cunelières (Territoire de Belfort) on August 19, 1919 with Marie Louise Flavie Julie LENGLET His wife was a teacher at the nursery school of rue Baillet-Reviron in Versailles (Yvelines). They had 5 children. At the beginning of the se World War II, they lived at 132 bis avenue de Paris in Versailles (formerly 102). Ernest Lorrain had seven brothers and sisters, born between 1882 and 1896 in Cunelières to Michel LORRAIN and Marie LORRAIN, born TISSERAND, farmers. One of his brothers, Paul Lorrain, distinguished himself in the 47th Regiment in Verdun and his brother Benjamin Michel, killed in the Apremont forest, is buried in the Marbotte military cemetery.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photograph of Ernest LORRAIN as a cuirassier - Photograph of Ernest LORRAIN at the front - Mention in the order of the division of Ernest LORRAIN (February 10, 1921) - Diploma giving the right to wear Fourragère by Ernest LORRAIN (January 1919 )

title: FRAD034-037_Joseph VIDAL et Aimé HYACINTHE | Documents from Joseph Vidal and Aimé Hyacinthe
text:
TITLE
FRAD034-037_Joseph VIDAL et Aimé HYACINTHE | Documents from Joseph Vidal and Aimé Hyacinthe

METADATA
Title: FRAD034-037_Joseph VIDAL et Aimé HYACINTHE | Documents from Joseph Vidal and Aimé Hyacinthe
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Joseph VIDAL is the maternal grandfather of the contributor, Mireille Hyacinthe. He was born on December 20, 1890 to Sylvestre Vidal and Marie Claustres in Illier-Laramade (Ariège), where he became a farmer. He married on June 3, 1911 with Marie Vidal. They have two children, a boy, Georges, born in 1912, and a girl, the mother of the contributor, born in 1919. Joseph VIDAL did his military service at the 143rd RI in Carcassonne from 1911. He was mobilized in 1914 in Illier-Lamarade (Ariège), still in the 143rd RI, then notably moved to the 267th RI, to the 1st and 21st Engineer Regiment. He was demobilized on July 17, 1919. Despite his status as breadwinner, he went to the front in August 1914. He was wounded in the right thigh by shrapnel on November 6, 1914 in Wischaete, Belgium. He was treated there, and returned to the front where he remained until the end of the war. He obtained two medals: the French commemorative medal of the Great War and the military medal. He is a policeman when he returns from the war. Aimé HYACINTHE is a great-uncle of the contributor, Mireille Hyacinthe, on the paternal side. He was mobilized at the start of the war.

RELATED MATERIALS
Joseph VIDAL left the following documents and objects: - a wooden and metal sewing kit; - a case embroidered by a nurse, from the military hospital where he was treated. It bears an embroidered and painted inscription ("Hospital 101 / Christmas 1914 / Prop patria!"), as well as a French flag and a plant motif. Joseph VIDAL maintained a correspondence with the nurse after the war; - two photographs in uniform: one during his military service with the 143rd in Carcassonne according to the caption on the back; the other, when he is older standing next to a chair; - his military booklet; - an official letter giving him the right to wear the Victory Medal, dated November 17, 1920. Aimé HYACINTHE left two postcards: one dated September 5, 1914; the other, April 28, 1917, part of Creil, addressed to his brothers and sisters.

title: AMSM-016_1 Lettres à Amélie | Letters From Louis Anselmet to his Sister
text:
TITLE
AMSM-016_1 Lettres à Amélie | Letters From Louis Anselmet to his Sister

METADATA
Title: AMSM-016_1 Lettres à Amélie | Letters From Louis Anselmet to his Sister
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1917
Places: Vosges
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My maternal uncle Louis Anselmet wrote many cards to Amélie Anselmet, one of his sisters who worked in Lyon. He writes the alternation between the trenches and the rear police guardhouse. From the front he sent him various species of mountain moss, writing to him once that they were all sick with the flu, Jean his brother and his father too, except Paul his other brother. He asked his sister for objects, an oilcloth, money, Nestlé milk powder... He went back to the trenches belonging to an infantry regiment in the Vosges. Louis Anselmet died on September 17, 1917 in Bourg en Bresse hospital.

RELATED MATERIALS
3 Louis Anselmet photographs 9 correspondence on postcards

title: AMSM 016-2 Recueil de lettres | Letters of André Larfeuil, POW in Germany
text:
TITLE
AMSM 016-2 Recueil de lettres | Letters of André Larfeuil, POW in Germany

METADATA
Title: AMSM 016-2 Recueil de lettres | Letters of André Larfeuil, POW in Germany
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1913, 1918
Places: Argonne
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My father André Larfeuil was born on February 26, 1893 and died in 1970 at the age of 77. He made his classes in 1913 in the 156th infantry regiment, 3rd section machine gunners; he is taken prisoner in Germany, in the Black Forest; when he returns from the war it is with a hearing impairment. His war decorations remained with another family member. Restorer and framer of paintings at Braun avenue de l'Opéra before 1913, he will be in public transport in the Paris region after 1918 and will marry in 1921. As a child, I asked him a lot of questions, he agreed to answer but he was more talkative with my husband, his son-in-law. The captivity, the war had affected him morally. This was true in everyday life, as he was very nervous and tough. A series of a hundred letters to his parents from 1913 to 1915 animates all that he has of love, endurance, sometimes with a tone of humor. For the current contribution, about twenty letters are presented. His cousins write to him during classes. The very close correspondence with his parents for whom he was the only son, marks an invaluable filial bond because no permission will be granted it seems. He talks about the daily training before the declaration of war in Commercy and the war ''full of horrors'' in the Argonne, He surrounds his parents ''do not get emotional'' (2-8- 1914), ''be calm, get an idea that many parents are in your situation'' (10-12-1914). He asks his parents' forgiveness for a few ''unfair'' words. ''How sweet it is to have parents who think of you in misery'' he wrote to them (26-10-1914). To his cousins he confides: ''in the infernal situation where we are, we are disgusted with his person, I am in my 4th great battle'' (1-10-1914)... ''He pours, it makes a tremendous wind and we are transformed into sieves'' (17-11-1914); to face the snow, the frost, the shells, he diverts the readers his parents, on the menus of the snack bar (26/11-1914). On 9-12-1914, by a diagram he gives his position in Lacroix-sur-Meuse, south of Verdun, in good health despite the lice, he asks for sulfur ointment for the body. From his trench with 25cm of mud where water filters everywhere, he left to supply the first lines with ammunition (19-1-1915), also volunteered as a gunner. Alerts are very provided (20-1-1915) because of the Germans who try, day and night to seize the trenches of the 1st lines which are 25 meters from theirs. ''The Germans had succeeded in mining one of our trenches and blowing it up then had occupied the excavation containing two of our disabled machine guns...so, in the evening, having recovered from the surprise, we were assembled with bayonets with the guns ready to march, our machine guns opened a violent fire... today my company is relieved from the trenches, our squad is on fire picket in the village where there is no civilian, I am writing to you by the light of 'a candle and after three days of rest, we will return to the trenches.' ''In a 400 kilometer line, it looks like party gun shacks next to each other...despite the air of laughing , I ask the good god daily that he leave me my life and all my limbs'' (12-2-1915). ''The explosive bullets cause terrible wounds, they explode in the wound. '' (23-4-1915) It evokes bombs weighing 89 kilos in the wood of La Grurie, hard-hitting and exploding shells, a terrible tangle in the woods made of trees, wires, branches...( 30-4-1915) oil thrown by firefighters to retake a trench from the enemy (29-06-1915). ''A battalion has only 80 men left, another 300...(4-7-1915) we carry 26 kilos to run. We were almost buried by a landslide'' (30-7-1915)... André Larfeuil my father comments on the news in the newspapers, the Germans having torpedoed an English transatlantic liner with many American passengers. It is probable that this misfortune will unleash a great fury among the Americans...''(9-5-1915) ''It is the married men who go on leave...''(2-8-1915). ''The hospitals are full of wounded Those who threw us into this hell will suffer the consequences'' (8-8-1915) signs the hairy man from Argonne but ''to learn that in Paris, the people are distracted by spectacle as we learn from the workers who come to dig the trenches of folds!... Perhaps it is possible that the people cannot do anything to put an end to this war but they should let the government understand that it has lasted long enough''! (14-8-1915) ''We are treated like real cattle, as the government says walk or die but until the end, that's what these treaties call humanity. Finally, it is a despair to add in others; May God save my life'' (30-8-1915). ''I saw myself burn the observatory balloon which you are told about in the newspapers, I assure you that it was a tragic spectacle, especially at night! My dear parents don't worry, I don't do any bravery, but when you have to move forward, you have to walk of course. In my division many men refused to advance, about thirty surrendered to the enemy.'' He receives a summons. (9-10-1915) ''I read the newspapers and note that the ministry has been changed, it proves that those who govern us are fed up with the situation''(1-11-1915). He gets lost in the trenches beyond the French troops near the enemy lines. Then there is silence. A photograph of him in captivity (pen tip above) among a group of prisoners dates from 1917.

RELATED MATERIALS
_ choice of about twenty letters _ a photo of André Larfeuil prisoner

title: FRAD085-014 Journal de guerre d'Alfred Bonnaud | Alfred Bonnaud's diary
text:
TITLE
FRAD085-014 Journal de guerre d'Alfred Bonnaud | Alfred Bonnaud's diary

METADATA
Title: FRAD085-014 Journal de guerre d'Alfred Bonnaud | Alfred Bonnaud's diary
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
When General Mobilization was decreed on August 2, 1914, Chief Warrant Officer Alfred Bonnaud had been serving for 17 years in the 35th Field Artillery Regiment based in Vannes (Morbihan). The first engagement of his battery took place in Maissin, Belgium, on August 22. For almost four years, Alfred took part in all phases of the war. On November 3, 1914, he changed assignments within the 1st battery. He takes command of the echelon, ie he leaves the firing battery to join the group in charge of battery support and logistics. On August 4, 1915, the 35th RAC was relieved, and Alfred went on leave for the first time. Direction Vannes, then Vix (Vendée). From November 1, 1917, Alfred was recalled to the gun battery to perform the duties of gunnery officer, then battery commander. Directed to the interior on April 11, 1918, it did not take part in the last battles. Admitted to retirement in 1920, he retired to Vix (Vendée). See the biographical note of Charles Julien Alfred Bonnaud in the Dictionary of Vendeans

RELATED MATERIALS
Alfred Bonnaud wrote a war diary "in 1921 [based on] very brief notes, taken day by day, sometimes during the fight, and above all [from] memories". He precisely describes the movements, the military operations, the daily activities, the destruction, leaving little room for his personal impressions.

title: FRBMAN-012 Antoine Laurenty | Antoine Laurenty's story
text:
TITLE
FRBMAN-012 Antoine Laurenty | Antoine Laurenty's story

METADATA
Title: FRBMAN-012 Antoine Laurenty | Antoine Laurenty's story
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1917
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Antoine Laurenty, a Belgian citizen residing in Brussels, was in his fifties when the Great War broke out. He refused to leave his town and remained there throughout the conflict, despite the dangers and hardships. He began on August 1, 1914 a diary in which he related until his forces abandoned him in 1917 the daily life in the rear, made up of rumors and fleeting hopes. He regularly mentions his nephew René Sibenaler who is fighting in the trenches of the Yser. An edition of this journal was produced by Jean Sibenaler in 2003 at Cheminements editions in Angers under the title "The notebooks of a Belgian citizen, 1914-1918". This book is kept at the Municipal Library of Angers in the collections of the Printer's Legal Deposit under the symbol DLE B54292.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 2 linen-backed notebooks constituting Antoine Laurenty's diary - 1 digitization of the cover, prefaces, foreword and post-face of the 2003 edition of Antoine Laurenty's notebooks - 1 photograph of Antoine Laurenty, his wife Mathilde and her sister-in-law - 1 photograph of René Sibenaler in the trenches of the Yser - 10 small photographs concerning René Sibenaler at the front

title: FRBMAN-013-Les deux frères François et Jean-Baptiste Moreau | Two brothers, François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau
text:
TITLE
FRBMAN-013-Les deux frères François et Jean-Baptiste Moreau | Two brothers, François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau

METADATA
Title: FRBMAN-013-Les deux frères François et Jean-Baptiste Moreau | Two brothers, François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau were two brothers from Saint-Martin-du-Bois (Maine-et-Loire) who were both settled as nurserymen in Guernsey where they were married and each had three children. They were called up together, leaving Angers on September 3, 1914, with the 135th infantry regiment. They were both killed on September 27, 1914, in Prosnes (Marne). Their family inherited a two-handed war diary (François is on the left page, Jean-Baptiste on the right page) copied into a small notebook. This is probably a copy of two notebooks that belonged to each of the two brothers, because it is written by the same hand, and at certain times, the brothers having been separated, they could not write it together; but it is a fine tribute to their parallel destinies. The end of the notebook coincides with their deaths; but they had had the foreknowledge of their approaching death, for the last page contains Francis's farewell to his loved ones. They would be buried in Sillery, near Reims.

RELATED MATERIALS
-1 notebook containing the war diary of the Moreau brothers -1 postcard sent on September 6, 1914 from Semoy (45) by François Moreau to his parents

title: FRBMAN-014 Justin Usse, élève pilote au centre d'aviation militaire de Chartres | Justin Usse, fighter pilot
text:
TITLE
FRBMAN-014 Justin Usse, élève pilote au centre d'aviation militaire de Chartres | Justin Usse, fighter pilot

METADATA
Title: FRBMAN-014 Justin Usse, élève pilote au centre d'aviation militaire de Chartres | Justin Usse, fighter pilot
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Justin Usse (born in 1890), blouse by profession, enlisted on October 8, 1914 in Saint-Brieuc where he lived. First a soldier in the 129th infantry regiment, he moved to the 114th infantry regiment on March 25, 1915. He remained there until May 10, 1915, when he was injured by a mine explosion at Loos (Pas- of Calais). After his convalescence, he cannot return to the infantry and is versed in the aviation. He followed an aeronautical technical instruction, then a pilot instruction, at the military aviation center of Chartres, and received the military pilot's license on January 17, 1916 after 25 hours of flight. It is therefore operational and begins its missions. Most of them consist in making photographic surveys before and after the bombardment. During his career as a pilot, he was cited three times in the orders of the army corps; he received the war cross with two gold stars in November 1916 and a third gold star in May 1917. Returned to civilian life on August 15, 1919, he would not forget the lessons of his war years since he would later become president of the Morbihan flying club.

RELATED MATERIALS
-Military booklet by Justin Usse -Pilot booklet by Justin Usse -Album of photographs compiled after the war by Justin Usse (photographs of planes, aerial topographical surveys, portraits) -Letter from the Ministry of War (03/04/1920) for award of the Distinguished Conduct Medal (English Decoration)

title: Souvenirs et notes du cuirassier Jacques PAVARD | Memories and notes from cuirassier Jacques PAVARD
text:
TITLE
Souvenirs et notes du cuirassier Jacques PAVARD | Memories and notes from cuirassier Jacques PAVARD

METADATA
Title: Souvenirs et notes du cuirassier Jacques PAVARD | Memories and notes from cuirassier Jacques PAVARD
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1919
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
War memories of my great-grandfather Jacques Pavard (29-12-1890 / 02-02-1959, native of Dourdan in Essonne Jacques PAVARD did his military service in 1910. Not having his school certificate, he was put in the section of the illiterate until the day when an officer, entering the barracks, saw him writing the letters of his friends. He was then transferred to the regiment of cuirassiers residing in Cambrai. Returning to Dourdan, the service finished , he resumed his life and his work in the family butcher's shop until August 3, when the tocsin sounded the famous war and when he returned to the 12th regiment of the cuirassiers of Rambouillet.This regiment was attached to the 6th brigade of cuirassiers of Saint Germain of the 7th cavalry division of Melun. In 1917, this regiment passed from the 7th to the 2nd cavalry division then in January 1918, it will become the 12th regiment of cuirassiers on foot. During the war, he never wanted quitting his job as kitchen manager and ravi pruning. Faced with the desire of grandchildren and great-grandchildren to read the notebooks, I decided to transcribe them and publish a book for the whole family in 2009. This testimony may also be of interest to enthusiasts of this period. We meet in particular Lieutenant Destouches who is none other than Louis-Ferdinand Céline. The notebooks relate with modesty his days from September 1914 to April 1919 outside the periods of leave.

RELATED MATERIALS
Transcription of the war diaries with insertion of some photos, postcards and documents found with the diaries.

title: FRAD067-198 Un jeune engagé volontaire lyonnais dans la guerre, Anthelme Perrin (1895-1979) | Documents from Anthelme Perrin
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-198 Un jeune engagé volontaire lyonnais dans la guerre, Anthelme Perrin (1895-1979) | Documents from Anthelme Perrin

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-198 Un jeune engagé volontaire lyonnais dans la guerre, Anthelme Perrin (1895-1979) | Documents from Anthelme Perrin
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Son of Joseph Jean Perrin, supervisor at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts in Lyon, and Anne Louis, Anthelme Joseph Eugène Perrin was born in Lyon (6th arrondissement) on January 8, 1895. He volunteered for 4 years October 18, 1913 and, despite a first injury on May 12, 1914, began the war on August 2, 1914. Assigned to the 26th Dragoons, he was promoted to brigadier in September 1914. His career illustrates the evolution of the means assigned in war, and the predominance of artillery over cavalry. On April 24, 1915, he was assigned to the machine gun section of the 26th Dragoons, then, on April 20, 1916, to the 2nd marching skirmishers (brigade machine gun company). Appointed corporal, he had to leave the battlefield for a good part of the summer of 1916, due to illness. Having become a liaison officer for the commander of Maniort, he was shot on April 16, 1917 at the Godat farm, northwest of Reims. He suffered a complete fracture of his left leg and was transferred to Auxiliary Hospital No. 20 of Notre-Dame-de-Lorette (Bordeaux) on April 25, 1917, then to Desgenettes Hospital (Lyon) on January 29. 1918. He left there on February 13, 1918 for a two-month convalescence. He ended the war as a tractor artillery driver in Sathonay-Camp (Rhône).

RELATED MATERIALS
The documentation kept by Mr. Daniel Perocheau, who knew Anthelme Perrin well before his death in Marseilles on August 10, 1979, consists of a photograph of the soldier with his family (nd); a safe-conduct from 1916 and two travel authorizations from 1916 and 1917; an original letter; an information sheet from 1917; two notebooks of letters to his parents and his sister Julie Jeanne Perrin (Lyon, 12/12/1898-06/24/1945), copied by his mother and respectively covering the periods from 07/29/1914 to 09/19/ 1916 and from 03/01/1916 to 05/19/1916; a meal card from 1919; a military booklet. Only these last two pieces have not been reproduced for their anecdotal nature or in anticipation of the online distribution of the military registration registers of the classes that took part in the Great War. The notebooks were digitized by extracts: 26 first letters of the 1st notebook, documenting the first 4 months of war, and fragment of diary of the summer of 1915 copied at the top of the 2nd notebook.

title: Juste à temps! | A mother journeys to see her injured son, and attends his funeral
text:
TITLE
Juste à temps! | A mother journeys to see her injured son, and attends his funeral

METADATA
Title: Juste à temps! | A mother journeys to see her injured son, and attends his funeral
Record type: TEXT
Places: Angleterre (Folkestone et Londres) et France (Denain)
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My Canadian great-aunt, Susie Crombie, saw her three sons go to the front, Charlie, Vincent and Joe. Only Joe returned. Vincent died a few days after being wounded at the Battle of Denain on October 26, 1918. Charlie, also wounded in 1917, died on July 9, 1917, after several weeks in hospital. In the summer of 1917, their mother Susie traveled from Toronto (58 Hampton Court Appartments, Avenue Road), where she lived in Canada, to see Charlie in her London hospital (IODE Hospital, i.e. Imperial Order of the Daughters of the Empire Hospital, located in Hyde Park, a military hospital for Canadian officers from 1916 to 1919, see http://ezitis.myzen.co.uk/daughters.html). She arrived on July 13, four days after her death, announced to her by her other son Vincent: he adds that she is there just in time to be able to attend Charlie's funeral. Vincent, a lieutenant in the 19th battalion, died the following year, on October 26, 1918, from wounds received on October 11, 1918, during the battle of Denain. The military cross will be awarded to him in February 1919. These two soldiers have a tombstone in the Etaples cemetery in France.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Letter from Susie Crombie to her husband Bob: this letter is typewritten, on airmail paper, and written in Folkestone on July 14, 1917; Susie recounts her arrival, her journey to London, how she learns of the death of her son at the IOD Hospital, then his military funeral the next day, in a cemetery, no doubt in London (located 3/4 of an hour from Folkestone), buried with three other soldiers, two Canadians and an Australian, in the presence of several people including Colonel Clarke, of the 45th Battalion to which Lieutenant Charlie CS Crombie belonged. -Official letter announcing to Susie Crombie the death of her son Vincent. - Photo of Charlie's gravestone. - Photo of Vincent's tombstone.

title: FRBNBU-051 Pascal Thuillier très gravement blessé en septembre 1914 | Pascal Thuillier seriously injured in 1914
text:
TITLE
FRBNBU-051 Pascal Thuillier très gravement blessé en septembre 1914 | Pascal Thuillier seriously injured in 1914

METADATA
Title: FRBNBU-051 Pascal Thuillier très gravement blessé en septembre 1914 | Pascal Thuillier seriously injured in 1914
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1919
Places: Meuse
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Grandfather of the contributor Catherine Vanhaelewyn, Pascal Daniel Thuillier was born on April 06, 1893. From the class of 1913, he was part of the 131st infantry regiment which left the Coligny barracks (Orléans) on August 5, 1914 for Lerouville then Saint-Mihiel in the Meuse. He begins his war in Lorraine (Meuse and Meurthe-et-Moselle). At the beginning of September 1914, near Monfaucon and Epinonville (Meuse), he met his older brother Albert, a soldier in another regiment; they are at the same time in the forest of Argonne. Pascal was wounded by shrapnel in the back and a bullet to the head on September 7, 1914 in Louppy-le-Château. He is transported by wheelbarrow to the nearest ambulance located in a school. Then he is transported by train to Vichy where he arrives after three days of travel. He is treated in Vichy and his brother, injured in the lung, too. Pascal is hospitalized until November 16 in Vichy. He was then sent to convalesce at the Orleans depot and then 45 days with his family. He was in convalescence until April 15, 1915. On the decision of the reform commission, he was sent to auxiliary service in a prison camp depot until his release. He was one of the "broken faces". Three of the four siblings took part in the Great War, maybe even the fourth, but that's not sure because he turned 20 in 1918.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Account notebook of his war with photos during his care in Vichy and poem for the nurses with poem of response from the nurses - Photo of the four brothers who left for the war.

AMSM-027_1 : Documents de mémoire | Nazaire François Prat's Medical Records from WWI

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My maternal grandfather Nazaire François Prat born on 8-07-1874, from the recruitment class of 1894, had been wounded in the foot on 9/06/1918, and gassed in the war. He went to different hospitals, Angers on 06/11/1918, Toulouse on 06/21/1918, at the Roanne reform center on 12/4/1918. He lived in Six-Fours where he died in 1934 at the age of 60. Intoxicated by gas, with emphysematous type breathing and sclerosis, the doctor gave the right to a pension following the infirmity. My mother didn't tell me about it. I looked for these documents, my mother couldn't throw them away! Through this contribution, the soldiers are not forgotten and the documents bear witness to a painful aftermath of the 1914-1918 war.

RELATED MATERIALS
8 pieces concerning injury, evacuation, reform centers, convalescent leave.

STORY / CONTEXT
Louis Rouillon was born on September 20, 1892 in La Crèche (Deux-Sèvres, France). By profession a farmer, he was mobilized on August 2, 1914, wounded on September 8, 1914 at La Fère-Champenoise (Marne) by shrapnel: "multiple wounds in the hands and right thigh, left shoulder". Classified armed service and proposed for a change of arms by the reform commission. Passed to the 20th artillery regiment on December 11, 1916. He died in La Rochelle in 1976.

title: FRAD043 - Louis Gérentes, cycliste du colonel | Louis Gérentes, colonel's cyclist
text:
TITLE
FRAD043 - Louis Gérentes, cycliste du colonel | Louis Gérentes, colonel's cyclist

METADATA
Title: FRAD043 - Louis Gérentes, cycliste du colonel | Louis Gérentes, colonel's cyclist
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Louis Gérentes was born on December 4, 1876 in Laussonne (Haute-Loire, France). After studying law in Paris, he moved back to Le Puy-en-Velay before the First World War and opened a law firm on Boulevard Gambetta. In the meantime, he married Joséphine Gire (1905) and became the father of two daughters, Marie-Louise and Jeanne, born respectively in 1905 and 1911. During mobilization, he was assigned to the 101st territorial regiment based in Puy-en -Velay as colonel's cyclist until December 1917. In January 1918, he was appointed assistant clerk to the War Council of the 125th Division. At the end of the war, he was elected mayor of Laussonne until 1921. That same year, he went to Quimper to practice the profession of judge, and participated in particular in the Sezenec trial in 1924. In 1929, he left Brittany to practice in Bourges until 1938, then in Auxerre from 1938 to 1941. That same year, he took advantage of his retirement to return to his department of origin. He will later become mayor of Laussonne.

RELATED MATERIALS
Correspondence of Louis Gérentes with his wife Joséphine and his daughters

STORY / CONTEXT
The contributor is the grandson of Captain Edmond Rousseau. His story was told to him by his mother. The author of the letters is the captain himself, who died during the war. The photo was taken around 1902. " Edmond Rousseau was Captain in the 34th Colonial Infantry Regiment (based in Toulon), born April 17, 1872 in Boulogne-sur-Mer, he married January 23, 1902, to Madeleine Huret. In 1914 he had two daughters, Gabrielle 12 and Suzanne 8. He was mobilized from the first day, and left on Sunday August 2, 1914. In his hasty departure, he forgot his kepi (which he file), but urgently asks that the photos of Madeleine, Gaby and Suzon be sent to him in Toulon, whom he is very irritated at not having taken with him. In Paris, Aunt Marie Piriou, a cousin of Madeleine, l accompanies him to the Gare de Lyon, and stays with him until the train leaves at 2 p.m.

RELATED MATERIALS
Barrack plan, administrative documents, photographs.

Original item language: deu, Deutsch
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
The documents are a service card, letters, cards and photos of my grandfather from the time of the First World War. The originals are distributed among individual family members and have been compiled and made readable on DVD for the purpose of a general overview. He sent the letters and cards to his parents, his brother and his future wife.

RELATED MATERIALS
Kurt Willy Illing was born in Neuschoenburg in 1893 and fought on both the western and eastern fronts during World War I. After the war he successfully applied for the "German Field Honor Sign".

STORY / CONTEXT
Testimony collected from Martine Sinthomez, during the Great Collection operation of November 16, 2013, about Henri Sinthomez, her paternal grandfather. Henri Albert Corneille Sinthomez, class 1901, Dunkirk recruiting office, number 1350, was assigned to the 47th battalion of alpine hunters, 7th company. He was wounded at Maurepas in the Somme. He was treated in the Etinehem ambulance (5-1, in the Somme), an ambulance about which Georges Duhamel wrote. He died on August 28, 1916, two or three days after being wounded.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Photograph by Henry Sinthomez. - Photograph of the family of Henry Sinthomez. - Two memorial diplomas of "Mort pour la France" in the name of Henri Sinthomez.

title: FRAD067-102 Albert Daltroff, Mosellan engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec sa fiancée | Documents from Jean Daltroff
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-102 Albert Daltroff, Mosellan engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec sa fiancée | Documents from Jean Daltroff

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-102 Albert Daltroff, Mosellan engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec sa fiancée | Documents from Jean Daltroff
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915, 1918
Places: Stettin
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Albert Daltroff, Mosellan, born in Montigny-les-Metz on July 22, 1885, was engaged in the German army in 1914, as a private. He corresponded between 1915 and 1918 with Léa Jacob, a resident of Vantoux (Wanten bei Metz) - with whom he became engaged just before the war and who would become his wife after the war. All the sendings from Albert Daltroff to his fiancée represent some 240 postcards. The views and dates of the few maps presented make it possible to globally reconstruct his journey: in July 1915, he was at Wesel, the assembly point for the German troops for the Western Front; in April 1916, he probably wrote from around Lille; sent to the eastern front, he found himself in Stettin (now Poland) in July 1916 and June 1917; in April 1918 he lived in Turek (now Poland). A translation project currently being developed for this correspondence, which his historian grandson, Jean Daltroff, plans to study, will provide details of this journey.

RELATED MATERIALS
6 postcards sent between 1915 and 1918 to Léa Jacob in Vantoux (Wanton bei Metz).

STORY / CONTEXT
Originally from Sallèles d'Aude (Aude), Félix ROCACHER was a lieutenant of the 11th company 80 RI SP 140. Today his back little file has very little information about him. Married to Louise Alexandrine MARTIN, both had a child, Jeanne. Félix died on August 31, 1918 in Pont St Mard. He was buried in St Vassens (Aisne) then in the national necropolis of Crécy-au-Mont near Verdun.

RELATED MATERIALS
Letters. A letter explains the details of the circumstances of Felix's death in the trenches in a detailed, military style. Another written by a comrade describes the same facts but in another style (ROUX) A posthumous letter from the sergeant (L.MASSON) addressed to the family of Félix. 2 photographs.

title: FRAD067-167 Franz Xavier Host, Alsacien engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec les siens | Documents from Gérard Host
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-167 Franz Xavier Host, Alsacien engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec les siens | Documents from Gérard Host

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-167 Franz Xavier Host, Alsacien engagé dans l'armée allemande, correspond avec les siens | Documents from Gérard Host
Record type: TEXT
Places: Verdun
Subjects: World War I, Propaganda
Original item language: fra, deu, Deutsch, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Franz Xavier Host, Alsatian born December 2, 1885 is engaged in the field artillery of the German army from August 9, 1914 to November 25, 1918 as a private 2nd class. He gave news of the front to his relatives by regularly sending postcards and photographs addressed to his wife Amalia, his children and his father Joseph and recorded some personal memories of the period 1915-1917. His career can be reconstructed with the help of his "Militärdienstzeit Bescheinigung", issued on October 18, 1935 by the Reischsarchivzweigstelle of Stuttgart and appearing in his file as a veteran of the ONAC (transferred to the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin, 844 D 177): mobilized from August 3, 1914, he had several successive assignments: first assigned to the 15th army corps, in the infantry, in an ammunition column, he was sent, on August 31, 1915, to the Lazarett because of "Kniegelenksentzündung". He was sent on November 10, 1915 to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°80; from March 31, 1916, he was assigned to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°51, then from April 3, 1916, in the L. Munitions Kolonne II. Abtg. Felda. Rgt n°51. On April 28, 1916, he was sent to the Lazarett "mit Gelenkrheumatismus"; on June 21, 1916, he was sent back to the 3. Ers. beat Felda. Rgt n°51. Finally, from July 22, 1917 until November 23, 1918, the date of his demobilization, he was assigned to Flakbattr. No. 518 (anti-aircraft battery), in the artillery. His participation in several campaigns or his presence for more than two months in war zones are also reported: from October 30 to November 24, 1914, he took part in the battle near Ypres; from January 1 to August 31, 1915, to positional battles on the Yser; from April 3 to April 24, 1916, at the Battle of Verdun; from July 23 to December 31, 1917, to the battle and fighting in Flanders; from January 1 to November 11, 1918, the fighting in Flanders, the battle for Kemmel Hill, the fighting near Ypres, the fighting and the battle in Flanders. The second class Iron Cross was awarded to him on November 14, 1917. According to his correspondence, in January 1915 he was in Kortrijk (Belgium), in February–June 1915 in Wevelgem (Belgium); his cards were posted from Courtrai in August-September 1915, from Ghent at the end of September 1915; he wrote from Brussels in October 1915, then found himself in Neuf-brisach (Neubreisach) in Alsace from December 1915 to February 1916. In May-June 1916, he stayed in Bad Nauheim for medical reasons; he found himself in Ostend in November 1916, from July to December 1917 in Staden and from October 1917 to April 1918 in Beverne. He passed through Berlin in April 1918 and through Brussels, Louvain and Lichtervelde in September 1918. As a grandfather, he shared his memories of the war with his grandson, Gérard Host, and gradually gave him his documents and his belongings at each visit. of war.

RELATED MATERIALS
correspondence (postcards, photo cards) photographs personal notes soldier's cross and diploma leaflet press clipping

title: AMSM-025_1 : Sept ans sous les drapeaux | Célestin Joseph Baptistin's Story from the Great War
text:
TITLE
AMSM-025_1 : Sept ans sous les drapeaux | Célestin Joseph Baptistin's Story from the Great War

METADATA
Title: AMSM-025_1 : Sept ans sous les drapeaux | Célestin Joseph Baptistin's Story from the Great War
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1920, 1913
Places: Douaumont
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Célestin, Joseph, Baptistin BOYER, my maternal grandfather was born on May 13, 1893 in Puget-sur-Argens (Var). Class 1913, it was incorporated on 11/26/1913. 2nd class soldier. Colonial infantry. He participated with the RICM in the War of 14 and the Moroccan War where he was stationed in Sidi bou Knadel as evidenced by his songbook (see documents) Wounded, he received a citation N) 233 on 09/22/ 1918. (cf.documents) Sick following his wounds, he was demobilized in Sept.1919 then discharged definitively in 1922. He spent a total of 7 years in the flag, ending with the occupation of the Rhineland. He was happy to have learned a few words of German there, which was useful to him during the Second World War, allowing him to negotiate with German soldiers preparing to shoot innocent young people, taken for guerrillas. - He and my grandmother raised me. He didn't like to talk about the War. Every time he mentioned it, he cried. However, when I was in terminal, this period being in the history program, it was then that he told me his memories, spoke to me about the Chemin des Dames, Douaumont (June 1916 -January 1917) and Morocco. His evocations moved me, gave me great interest and pushed me to study history at university. Some memories: -When he was in the Colonial Infantry Regiment of Morocco, he said that his orderly Mamadou Koulibali, a Senegalese, had saved him in the desert, by feeding him snakes, because they had run out of food. -During the war of 1914-1918, my grandfather had the function of estafette for the transport of the mail between the trenches. Gassed, he was picked up by Henri Perrimond, his best friend, a native of Montauroux. They both got away with it! They remained very close friends all their lives. Another time, wounded by shrapnel in the stomach, he was treated on the spot. We used a fork to extract the pieces! So he was saved twice. - the suffering was such in the trenches, without rest, without permission for too long, that some soldiers took their feet out of the trenches, hoping to be injured in order to be sent to the rear. Like his friend Henry. In February 1919, he married in Vidauban (var), Marie Péronne, his war godmother, my grandmother. He had sent him a book of Songs written in Sidi bou Knadel (copy below). Demobilized, he worked as a hairdresser in Paris for two or three years, at 5 rue des Couronnes in the XXth arrondissement, where his boss had kept his place for him. This is where my mother was born in December 1920. Then he had to return to the South to treat his sick lungs. Anecdotes concerning the treatments of the time: -Sept.1919, in convalescence in Cogolin, where his mother lived, he had to drink hot blood for a daily prescribed cure, as well as swallow slugs. His infirmity was aggravated by pulmonary sclerosis. He suffered from it all his life. It was not until 1964 that his pension became full. He died in 1966 of yet another pulmonary complication. He was mentioned several times at the order of the regiment.. '' very courageous and spirited soldier, distinguished himself by his good conduct .. under the heavy fire of enemy machine guns during the last operations '' in Sept.1918 (see attached document). He received decorations (military medal, war cross) and treasured the fourragère of his regiment, the RICM, the most decorated regiment in France.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 4 photo-cards of Célestin Boyer, soldier. - copies and extracts from the Registrar Matriculations: class 1913 /Nice/N° 831, pages 194-95-96 (in Var Departmental Archives) - copy of a summons to the order of the regiment. - copy of the song book, addressed to Marie Perrone..

title: Besitzzeugnis über das Verdienstkreuz für Kriegshilfe von Franz Schönmehl | Certificate for decoration of Franz Schönmehl
text:
TITLE
Besitzzeugnis über das Verdienstkreuz für Kriegshilfe von Franz Schönmehl | Certificate for decoration of Franz Schönmehl

METADATA
Title: Besitzzeugnis über das Verdienstkreuz für Kriegshilfe von Franz Schönmehl | Certificate for decoration of Franz Schönmehl
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Places: Mainz, Deutschland
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My esteemed father Franz Schönmehl, born in Mainz in 1883, was a lively, liberal Mainz wine merchant. He did his obligatory military service, but at the annual exercises that followed he must have behaved so unwarlikely that he was sent to the office where he made himself useful. This also included writing the draft orders for an eventual war. And peace-loving as my father was, he wrote his own conscription to the station headquarters in Mainz. And when the war started in August 1914, he was already 31 years old and had a wonderful job that he was able to hold until he handed over the station to the French troops in November 1918. His only encounter with the war was a French air raid with small aerial bombs. My father was so frightened that while trying to escape he tripped over his own saber and fell headlong. But it was enough for a war merit medal. My mother's father, a well-known restaurateur from Chemnitz, spent the war with the Saxon troops at the goulash cannon in Poland. So none of my family had to shoot at people. This non-warlike past of my family has continued, partly thanks to the "late birth": I was just 1 year too young for the Nazi war 1939-1945, my father already too old and at the third event I was for the Bundeswehr too old.

RELATED MATERIALS
(1) Certificate of possession of the Cross of Merit for War Aid from Vizefeldwebel d. L. Franz Schönmehl. The certificate was issued in Berlin on December 21, 1917 and, according to the information on the back, handed over on August 3, 1918 and provided with a stamp from the "Chief of the field railway system". (2) Photo by Franz Schönmehl

title: FRAM - Laurent Tromeur, mémoires d'un poilu | Documents from Jean Tromeur
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Laurent Tromeur, mémoires d'un poilu | Documents from Jean Tromeur

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Laurent Tromeur, mémoires d'un poilu | Documents from Jean Tromeur
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915
Places: Verdun
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My father, Laurent Tromeur was born in 1885 in Lennon (Finistère). Professor of mathematics in Valenciennes, he was mobilized in 1915 as an infantryman in the 55th infantry regiment. He wrote this manuscript during his retirement and finished it a year before his death, on July 2, 1969. This account speaks of his daily life as a private 2nd class, from May 1916 to November 11, 1918. Wounded on May 26, 1916 during the 2nd Battle of Verdun at Le Mort-Homme, Cumières, he was hospitalized at the Château de Faux-Miroir. In 1917, he was transferred to the 3rd Machine Gun Company and assigned to the 3rd Battalion of the 42nd Infantry Regiment. He was on the front line, as a telemeter. His job was to sketch the distances from each machine gun to the dangerous points in the sector. In this story, it appears that my father did not like the hierarchy very much. He refused the decorations that were awarded to him and did not participate in any celebrations of November 11. “We will never forget those evenings when our father captivated our attention for hours with stories of war”. As a teacher, he never refused to tell his story to his students. He wrote his memoirs in a 73-page school notebook.

RELATED MATERIALS
Photograph of Laurent Tromeur and his family Memoirs of Laurent Tromeur

STORY / CONTEXT
Louis Auguste Vallerand, grandfather of the contributor, was born on October 2, 1886 in Sainte-Menehoulde (Marne). Residing at 62, rue Chauzy in Sainte-Menehoulde and mobilized there. Incorporated into the 106th RI, he fought in the Battle of the Marne. Died for France on October 4, 1914 in Lens (Pas-de-Calais). Buried in the ossuary of the Lens cemetery. Rank cyclist sergeant in the 5th Cavalry division's cycling group. He was also a firefighter, sportsman and plasterer.

title: FRAM - Journal de guerre de Jean Carré | Document from Jean Corentin Carré

TITLE
FRAM - Journal de guerre de Jean Carré | Document from Jean Corentin Carré

Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean Corentin Carré, born in Faouët (Morbihan), on January 9, 1900. On April 27, 1915, he enlisted at the age of 15 in the 41st infantry regiment. He arrived at the front at 15 years and 6 months and was assigned to the 410 infantry regiment. Section leader at 17, he earned the ranks of corporal, sergeant and adjutant, citations to the division, the army corps and the army. He died in aerial combat at the age of 18 and 3 months, on March 18, 1918. He was the youngest death for France in Faouët. In order to be able to engage, he invents an identity. His name was then Auguste Duthoy, born in Rumigny (Ardennes), on April 10, 1897. On December 29, 1916, from the depths of the trenches, he wrote a letter to Colonel Treillard, commander of the 410th infantry regiment. He asks to resume his identity, without leaving the front. This diary is kept at the Jean Carré college in Faouët, inaugurated in 1978.

RELATED MATERIALS
Journal of Jean Carré Press cutting with the letter of Jean Carré, addressed to Colonel Treillard, published in the newspaper Le Petit Parisein on July 8, 1917

My great-uncle, on the other hand, was very talkative, joyful, with a lot of humour”.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: nld, Nederlands
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Petrus Franciscus Marivoet, my grandfather and bricklayer and known as Frans Marivoet, was a veteran of the First World War. At the beginning of the war he helped defend Antwerp from the forts of Koningshooikt. After the fall of Antwerp, he defended Belgium and fought in Diksmuide (the trenches in Kaaskerke). On February 5, 1915, he was injured and was taken to France (Valognes) for treatment. After recovery, he returned in July 1915 to the front at Armentieres and Fortem (now a street in Alveringem). On December 21, 1915, he joined the artillery workers of Le Havre (ammunition depot) and remained there until the end of the war. In mid-February 1919 he was back at home in Londerzeel (Steenhuffel) with his wife (my godmother) and his child (my aunt Jeanne). Author: August Marivoet, Mechelsesteenweg 325, 2860 Sint-Katelijne-Waver.

RELATED MATERIALS
A letter from Petrus Franciscus Marivoet to the home front.

STORY / CONTEXT
Francisque Louis Majesté (paternal grandfather of the contributor) originally from a family of distillers from Ornans(25) was incorporated into the 94th infantry regiment (registration number 04518 and 5191) as quartermaster sergeant. Before the war, he worked as an accountant. From September 19, 1914, his family received no further news from him. He will be reported missing in Baconnes (Marne) until in 1919 one of his comrades confirms to his wife his injury and his disappearance. His body was never found.

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Paul Emile Larcher was born on May 23, 1890 in Saint-Dizier (Haute-Marne, France). Locksmith by profession, he was incorporated into the 1st section of military nurses on October 10, 1911. He participated in the campaign against Germany from August 2, 1914 to January 29, 1919 as a private 2nd class. He was assigned to the campaign Eastern Railways, left with ambulance 4/20 on August 5, 1914, he was taken prisoner on August 19 and was repatriated in September of the same year. Returned to the depot of the 23rd section on September 5, 1914, he left on December 24 and arrived at the RPS of the 8th army on December 25. He returned his military effects to the Engineers at [Chalons] on October 18, 1919. He died on November 30, 1954.

RELATED MATERIALS
1 military booklet, 1 photograph, 1 certificate.

Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Fernand GAILLON (1884-1917) was mobilized at the start of the war and incorporated as a quartermaster sergeant in the 22nd company of the 287th infantry regiment. He made the Belgian campaign and fought in the front line at Verdun for 72 days in 1916. His courage in combat earned him two citations, the content of which is as follows: "Beautiful conduct in the fire on April 16, 1917" and "Sous-officer of "great coolness and great bravery. Already cited in the order of the division, distinguished himself again during the attack of August 20, 1917 where he found a glorious death while going to communicate an order of the battalion commander". He is buried in the Marceau cemetery in Verdun. He had been manager of the Société Générale office in Nouvion-en-Thiérache (Aisne) for 4 years when the war broke out. Married, he had three children. He died at the age of 33.

RELATED MATERIALS
- Extracts from the album of photographs taken and captioned by Fernand GAILLON himself (1915-1916)

title: FRAM - Le retour du corps de Charles Hamelin | Documents from Charles Hamelin
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Le retour du corps de Charles Hamelin | Documents from Charles Hamelin

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Le retour du corps de Charles Hamelin | Documents from Charles Hamelin
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Originally from Breteuil (Eure), Charles Hamelin joined the 9th Company of the 28th Infantry Regiment based in Evreux. In August 1918, Mrs. Hamelin was worried that she had no more news from her husband. At the end of the month, she was informed by a letter from her combat comrade, Private Jégou, that Charles had been killed by shrapnel on August 10. Jégou buried him near the village of Ressons, in the Oise. Her body was transferred to the Ressons military cemetery on July 18, 1919. Mrs. Hamelin then asked the Ministry of Pensions for the restitution of her husband's remains, which were to be exhumed on February 1, 1921. His remains, as well as those of seventy-five other soldiers from the Department of Eure who fell in combat, was repatriated by train to Evreux station, before being buried again in the Breteuil cemetery. Charles Hamelin obtained the Croix de Guerre posthumously on August 13, 1922.

STORY / CONTEXT
01-Group photo (March 8, 1919) Amédé Fleury is the 2nd from the top left. 02-Photo portrait of Amédé Fleury (1915) sent to his father. 03-Photo portrait of Amédé Fleury (1916) sent to his sister, Valentine. 04-The Somme from 08/19 to 09/19/1916; Daily life in the trenches. 05-Photo "My office..." by Amédé Fleury (February 1916). 06-Photo of Alexandre Meurisse, his brother-in-law, a teacher like him, who died next to Amédé Fleury in October 1914 at the age of 20. 07-Photo of Amédé Fleury's foster family during his convalescence (September 1914). 08-1914-Beginning of the conflict. July 09, 1915 - Rosnay camp, La Meule trench. August 09, 1915 - Reims, Pontavert, Cauroy. 09-October, November 1915 - Hermonville, Sapignol bridge, Cauroy. 09-December 1915 - Cauroy, Trenches of Bois Charpentier and Luxembourg. January 10, 1916 - Le Godat, elongated wood. February 10, 1916 - La Source, Trench of Godat, Battle of Verdun. March 10, 1916 - Verdun, Sainte Catherine hospital. 10-May, June 1916 - Craonne district of the Source. July 10, 1916 - Craonnelle camps of Blanc Sablon and Beau Marais, march from Craonnelle to Epernay. August 10, 1916 - Francastel, Celestine camp; the Sum. September 10, 1916 - Battle of the Somme. October 10, 1916 - Champagne, Bois de la Chenille. 10-November 1916 - Villages of the Somme. December 10, 1916 - Cauroy, Trou Bricot. January 11, 1917 - Suippes, Montmirail, Beau Marais sector. February 11, March 1917 - Beaurieux. April 11, 1917 - Beaurieux, attack on Craonne, Plateau de Vauclerc. May 11, 1917 - La Ferté under Jouarre, Pargny Le Thus. June 11, 1917 - Mailly camp. July 11, 1917 - Courchamp, Steemwerk, Esquelberq. August 11, 1917 - The Yser, Looberghe. 11-September, October, November, December 1917 - Woesttine, Sangatte. 12-January, March, April, May, June 1918 - Clerey Chartreux, Perennes. July 12, 1918 - Croutay, Lascay, Fontenoy, Vic sur Aisne. August 12, 1918 - Auberge du chat embarrassé, Amblémy, Tartiers. September 12, 1918 - Soissons, Saconin, Breuil, Cutry, Vailly sur Aisne. 12-October, November 1918 - Colmar, Metzeral. 13-1919 - Request for news and Souvenir of Champagne. 14-Correspondence August-September 1914.

RELATED MATERIALS
Annette Bon, contributor, comes from Marie-Madeleine Fleury (born in 1923), only daughter of Amédé Fleury (December 1887 in Lomme (Nord)-July 1966). Amédé Fleury was a teacher at the Ecole Michelet. He spent his leave at the Carmel of Compiègne in the company of his aunt. He was a sergeant in the 43rd line. As a teacher, he became a vaguemestre. He also writes for other soldiers. He has a camera with which he photographed "his" war; he develops his photographs and mentions on the back of each the dates, places, characters and events to which they refer like a logbook from 1914 to 1919. He is in the occupied zone (Lille and Lomme). Some of his photographs serve him as a support for correspondence with his wife, Marie-Madeleine Fleury.

My grandfather, Ernest Lebarbey, of the class of 1896, was assigned to the 3rd battalion of the 74th Territorial Infantry Regiment. From September 23, 1917 to February 3, 1919, he kept a diary in which he recorded the movements of his battalion. "Very disciplined and very dedicated soldier, wounded in the course of refueling during the last battles", he was cited for the Croix de Guerre on April 15, 1918.

STORY / CONTEXT
Contribution on behalf of Eric Braire. Abraham Florent Villain (1897-April 4 in the 1990s), distant uncle (not first) of Eric Braire's mother, is part of class 19; he was mobilized on 04/17/1918 in the 66th Infantry Regiment. Abraham Florent Villain called himself André Villain from 1939. He was an insurer between the two wars, then a property dealer and a bookseller. Eric Braire remembers that his mother spoke of Abraham Florent Villain only by using the name of André Villain, because this one did not want to be called otherwise.

RELATED MATERIALS
01-Notebook "The Victory Notebook" (1919) gathers information on Abraham Florent Villain dit André Villain; the notebook bears the usual information (civil status, assignment, civil correspondents, citations, permissions, body changes); however, the notebook is peppered with eccentric indications added in black ink, surely later (!). 02-Leaflet folded in the format of the notebook (inserted in this one) "Chant des Volontaires" dedicated by the 1st Company to Our Commander.

_ I had to take many steps to obtain the archival elements concerning seven members of our family. Research of Jean Marcadal, farmer born on 8/6/1896 in Réghaïa Algeria, uncle of my wife. From the Regiment of Algerian Riflemen. Joined the front at the age of 19 in Verdun. 2 citations, a military medal. Died as a result of his wounds and buried in the necropolis of Amiens on 3/5/1918. When we repatriated from Algeria in 1962, we had no echo of him. Forgotten until 1998 when we learned the date and place of his burial.

Provider: Europeana 1914-1918

STORY / CONTEXT
When the conflict broke out, Thomas and Marguerite Fouineteau, residents of Aubervilliers, entrusted the custody of their son Louis, born in 1905, to his aunt, Joséphine Lenfant. Thomas's sister resides with her husband Henri at the Château de Collivault, in Candé-sur-Beuvron, near Chailles (Loir-et-Cher). Thomas Fouineteau wrote to his son Louis and his sister throughout the conflict. His last letter dates from April 23, 1917. Three weeks later, his wife received a letter written by a nun from the hospital in Châlons-sur-Marne: Thomas had been injured and his condition was not good and he was worried. Thomas died a few days later from his injuries, at the age of 39.

RELATED MATERIALS
Copy of the active correspondence of Thomas Fouineteau

STORY / CONTEXT
Antoine-Marc Giorgetti a great-uncle born May 6, 1868 in Olcani in Corsica. I was fascinated by the numerous navigations carried out by him since 1888 and by other members of my family. Chief engineer in the navy, mobilized on a transport ship, the steam freighter ''Diane'' of the company of the United Charterers. The transport ship was attacked by a German submarine on June 6, 1917 in the Mediterranean off the coast of Oran. After firing for two hours, the ship began to sink, the crew put two canoes in the sea. After the torpedoing, the cargo ship was robbed of its supplies and tools by the Germans. Courageous attitude of the crew during the attack.

RELATED MATERIALS
_ a reward telegram _ report on the attack 2 pages _ letter from the Ministry of the Navy for citation

Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
André Brillet was born on November 8, 1898 in Pérignac (Charente, France). Volunteered for 4 years on December 8, 1916 in Angoulême, he arrived in the corps of the 52nd artillery regiment as a private 2nd class the same day. He moved to the 38th artillery regiment bound for the Army of the Orient on March 14, 1919. He was directed to Salonika (Macedonia) via Toulon on April 4, 1919, then he joined the 242nd artillery regiment on June 29 from the same year, then the 21st regiment on December 23, 1920. Finally, he joined the active army reserve on December 28, 1920. He then worked as a supervisor, chief supervisor and director in a penitentiary center in Saint-Martin-de- Re (Charente-Maritime, France).

RELATED MATERIALS
1 military booklet, 1 certificate of registration in the Verdun book, 1 citation to the order of the 169th infantry regiment, 1 diploma of the Voluntary Combatant's Cross, 1 certificate of good conduct, 1 commemorative medal diploma for operations carried out in Syria and the Levant, 1 service report, 1 demobilization certificate, 2 repatriation certificates.
