STORY / CONTEXT
FANTINO Émile Louis Constant, my paternal uncle, born October 5, 1898, died in Cogolin (Var) in 1966 FANTINO Émile, my father's brother, I knew him well. On the other hand, I never heard him talk about the war, he never mentioned this period. I know he worked as a blacksmith in Cogolin. As a young soldier, my uncle was called up to serve in the Army of the Rhine as an aide-marshal and 1st driving gunner. He was in the 40th Cavalry Artillery Regiment and also of the 4th Cavalry Division 23rd Battalion. In his military record, listed from May 4, 1917 to October 23, 1919, he fought against Germany, from October 24, 1919 to June 15, 1920, his Cavalry Division crossed the Franco-German border to occupy the area of Landau (city from Germany). He was released from his duties and returned to his home on June 15, 1920. On photo postcards sent to his parents, we can see him with his company of marshals and gunners in Landau.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 1 military booklet belonging to FONTINO Emile - 1 Certificate of good behavior - 1 leave: a permission - 1 travel voucher - photos: postcards addressed to his parents

title: FRBMLI-047 Carnets de mon arrière-grand-père, Léon Fernand Lemeré | Story of Léon Fernand Lemeré
text:
TITLE
FRBMLI-047 Carnets de mon arrière-grand-père, Léon Fernand Lemeré | Story of Léon Fernand Lemeré

METADATA
Title: FRBMLI-047 Carnets de mon arrière-grand-père, Léon Fernand Lemeré | Story of Léon Fernand Lemeré
Record type: TEXT
Places: Verdun
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
My great-grandfather, Léon Fernand Leméré, was a soldier in the 245th infantry regiment, 18th company and was mobilized on August 5, 1914. At the time, he was living in rue Saint-Louis en l'Ile in Paris. Married in 1912, he had a granddaughter, Marcelle (my grandmother) born in September 1913: she was the couple's only surviving child, two twins born in September 1916 died shortly after birth. He fought throughout the war: the Ardennes, Alsace, Lorraine and Verdun. He evokes his first Christmas, December 25, 1914 in La Neuvilette near Reims, the songs of the Germans in the trench opposite during Christmas night and the meeting in the early morning of 3 Germans and 3 French which ends peacefully on an "intimate interview". He recounts the Verdun front several times (1916 and 1917). He was wounded on May 31, 1918 and treated in Vitry-le-François hospital. Miraculously, the metal box in which he kept his tobacco preserved him from a more serious injury: a piece of shrapnel passed through the famous box without killing him; he kept it as a souvenir and passed it on to us. He was demobilized at the beginning of the summer of 1919 only. Family link: it is my grandfather on the paternal side and I knew him between 1971 and 1979. I only know his story through the notebooks and the correspondence he left, because we lived in Pas -de-Calais and he in Paris, so we rarely saw each other. He was very conservative and kept all kinds of documents that seemed important to him (the Armistice Day diary for example). I have almost no recollection of any stories, but I do remember him imitating the drumbeat with his thumb against the door. When he died, we collected these documents from his apartment and kept them.

RELATED MATERIALS
4 war diaries kept from 1914 to 1917 (missing the year 1918 announced, but lost) by Léon Fernand Lemeré containing his diary, the list of correspondence sent and received, expenses, songs and notes. An aluminum ring with the embedded portrait of her daughter, Marcelle (my grandmother) born in September 1913. Photographs by Fernand Lemeré.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Marcel Amédée Pellet, paternal grandfather of Dominique Pellet married Degrémont, contributor, was born on June 16, 1896 in Rivesaltes (Pyrénées-Orientales) to a family of wine merchants. From the class of 1916, he served in the war in the 403rd infantry regiment (named 403rd marching regiment on the board with his photo) in the 1st company. Called in advance, he was seriously injured in the left leg on June 6, 1915 in Fricourt-et-Mametz in the Somme. A heart problem ensues. Once injured he was repatriated to Paris. He did not go back to the front and carried crutches for a long time. It was in the Paris region during this war that he met his future wife, in a cellar during the fear of a bombardment. They married in 1918. His son (father of contributor Dominique Pellet-Degrémont) was born in 1927. Marcel Amédée Pellet limped all his life. He died in 1969.

RELATED MATERIALS
01- Frame of Marcel Pellet in photo with his crutches and his war cross. This frame remained prominently in his apartment until the end of his life. 02- Map in watercolor and ink, drawn by L. Blain "1st flag taken from the enemy" August 24, 1914. 03- Map in watercolor and ink, drawn by L. Blain, "3rd flag taken from the enemy" 1914 04- Map in watercolor and ink, drawn by L. Blain, "shooting on a German plane" 1914 05- Three postcards of the village of Gland destroyed after the second battle of the Marne in 1918.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
This story is that of my paternal grandfather, ROUBERT Louis born in 1896, died on December 25, 1945. He lived in La Seyne-sur-mer (Var), he worked as a diver in the navy. From the height of my five years, my grandfather seemed very tall to me. He did not die at the front, but he died from his war wounds, heart damage. My grandfather did not speak of this period, he had suffered too much. He wasn't even looking at his Medal of Honor. On the other hand, I was playing with this medal with the red and green ribbon. My grandfather brought back shells from the front. In the trenches, these shells were considered trophies of war. The soldiers and my grandfather filled the shells with sand, hammered them with the points of their bayonets to carve them.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 4 sculpted shells from 1915

title: Omer Martin, soldat Maître-ouvrier du Génie, 1914-1919 | Omer Martin, Soldier Master Engineer, 1914-1919
text:
TITLE
Omer Martin, soldat Maître-ouvrier du Génie, 1914-1919 | Omer Martin, Soldier Master Engineer, 1914-1919

METADATA
Title: Omer Martin, soldat Maître-ouvrier du Génie, 1914-1919 | Omer Martin, Soldier Master Engineer, 1914-1919
Record type: TEXT
Places: Versailles
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Farmer in Bailleau-sous-Gallardon (Eure-et-loir), Omer Martin was assigned to the 1st Engineer Regiment in Versailles in July 1914. His correspondence established his presence in the 1st line for two weeks in August 1915, then his stay in Etavigny (Oise) in December 1915. He was also present on the Craonne plateau, probably for the preparations for the Nivelle offensive of February 1917. 15 April 1917 for having "particularly distinguished himself during the period of preparation for the offensive and [having] valiantly participated in the pursuit of the enemy". Gassed and then suffering from gallstones, he stayed at the Bayonne military hospital between October and November 1918. Back in Versailles in January 1919, he was transferred in July to a placement center for demobilized soldiers.

RELATED MATERIALS
Postcards (correspondence with his family), photographs, War Cross, diploma of the order of citation (1917), veteran's card (1935)

title: FRAD008_3 Documents trouvés par M. Bonalda | Documents found by M. BONALDA
text:

FRAD008_3 Documents trouvés par M. Bonalda | Documents found by M. BONALDA

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. Bonalda found these documents in the bins of a sorting center for the most part, and in an attic for the last, between 2003 and 2004. He has no connection with the persons concerned but he has decided to keep these testimonies of the Great War for their historical value and for their link with the Ardennes. He is indeed passionate about history and a collector. In addition, some documents relate to his place of residence. He thought it was a shame to find such documents in trash cans.

RELATED MATERIALS
Notes from Abbé BOUCHER, from Issancourt. Entry of the Germans into Mohon, by Pierre FAUVILLE. Story by Adolphe OUICE, reservist at the 247th line. Name of the Germans staying in Charleville, with their address. Response letter from the King of Spain to the request for the release of Marcel DROUART.

title: FRAD078- 09 Mon grand- père et parain, Claude Mercier, blessé de guerre. | My grandfather and godfather, Claude Mercier
text:
TITLE
FRAD078- 09 Mon grand- père et parain, Claude Mercier, blessé de guerre. | My grandfather and godfather, Claude Mercier

METADATA
Title: FRAD078- 09 Mon grand- père et parain, Claude Mercier, blessé de guerre. | My grandfather and godfather, Claude Mercier
Record type: TEXT
Places: Givry
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
"My grandfather, Claude Mercier, was wounded in Douaumont on March 21, 1916. He was given convalescent leave on June 9, 1917, for one month. Following his injuries, he changed assignments, and followed training to become a field artillery officer. His regimental commander authorizes him to wear the ribbon and the red enamelled star, constituting a special sign for the war-wounded, retired soldiers, discharged soldiers, discharged for illness contracted or aggravated in service during the current campaign against Germany and its allies. I received these documents from my family, and I lived with my grandfather, whom my parents took in at the end of his life. I remember large works on the Great War, which my grandfather commented on with me. Moreover, I bear the name of my grandfather and godfather, Claude Mercier; it is a family tradition at we. "

RELATED MATERIALS
Administrative documents and black and white photographs.

STORY / CONTEXT
Three brothers go to war Grandfather Numa Petit Great uncles: Guillaume Petit, Numa's brother and Gilbert Arnaud, their half-brother Numa was a nurse at 144 RI with the Tunisian spais in Charlerois. He did Verdun and the Marne. One day when he was supposed to go on leave, it was cancelled. In anger he threw away his tobacco and proclaimed "I'll never smoke again, I'll be sick of it". He will emerge unscathed, uninjured, from the war.

title: FRAM - Histoire de Jean Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON | Documents from Jean-Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Histoire de Jean Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON | Documents from Jean-Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Histoire de Jean Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON | Documents from Jean-Adolphe et Zoé GUIGUET-DORON
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean Adolphe Guiguet-Doron was born on May 2, 1879 in Hauteluce, he is the son of Josephte Aimé Guiguet-Doron. Her sister Zoé Favre-Coutillet, born Guiguet-Doron, is the contributor's grandmother. Jean Adolphe's photograph and medals hung in his sister's room. The only element known to the contributor is the death of this great-uncle, who died for France in 1915; his memory was never mentioned in front of her. His identification sheet allows us to reconstruct his career: in 1899, he lived in Grenoble where he was a chimney sweep. He volunteered several times from 1897 in the 140th infantry regiment and then in the foreign legion. He was recalled at the time of general mobilization and incorporated into the 110th infantry regiment. He died on April 5, 1915, killed by the enemy at Flirey (Meurthe-et-Moselle). He posthumously received the Croix de Guerre with Palm and the Military Medal. His sister Zoé will keep her medals with her portrait and a letter from Private Lacroix dated November 27, 1915 giving her the list of effects found on her brother's body. The contributor found these objects among her father's belongings.

RELATED MATERIALS
1 - Photograph and medals of Jean Adolphe Guiguet-Doron (war cross). 2 - Letter card from Private Lacroix to Zoé Guiguet-Doron, November 27, 1915.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Painting representing a couple of civilians from Brieulles-sur-Bar (Ardennes) welcoming two American soldiers. It is about the arrival of the Americans in this small village in the Ardennes, at the end of 1918. Photograph acquired at a flea market by Pierre Guéry, mayor of the village of Brieulles-sur-Bar. The story could have ended there, but a few years later, the mayor receives an American couple, Mr. and Mrs. Herbert Floyd, a copy of said photo in hand. These people want to find traces of the descendants of Mr. and Mrs. Baleux, from the village who warmly welcomed the soldiers. Among these two soldiers of the American army, is Allen Floyd of the 42nd infantry division, the cousin of the father of Herbert Floyd, who was himself an infantry officer. Herbert Floyd also learns that the famous shot has a great reputation in the United States and also in France. It occupies a place of honor on the wall at the entrance to the photographic section of the National Archives in Washington and is part of a painting showing the cooperation of the two allies during the Great War of 14-18 at the Caen memorial.

RELATED MATERIALS
Couple of civilians welcoming two American soldiers in Brieulles-sur-Bar

title: FRBMLI-024 André VAN EYCKE, Maréchal des Logis, tué en 1916 à Maurepas | Story of André VAN EYCKE
text:
TITLE
FRBMLI-024 André VAN EYCKE, Maréchal des Logis, tué en 1916 à Maurepas | Story of André VAN EYCKE

METADATA
Title: FRBMLI-024 André VAN EYCKE, Maréchal des Logis, tué en 1916 à Maurepas | Story of André VAN EYCKE
Record type: TEXT
Places: Maurepas (Somme)
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
André Van Eycke, our maternal uncle, was killed on September 21, 1916 in Maurepas in the Somme, at the age of 24. He belonged to the 27th Artillery Regiment and served there as Marshal of the Logis: he commanded a battery of 75. Before the war, he was a student and did technical studies. He wrote to his aunt, Sidonie Van Eycke, his father's sister, to whom he was very close, because she was in the free zone (she worked as a tutor in a rich family and lived, among other places, in Switzerland). Our mother, who stayed throughout the war in Mons-en-Baroeul, was about twelve years old at that time and remembers the announcement of André's death and told us how devastated André's father was. (he had only this son, child of a first marriage). She had accompanied her mother to look for the body in 1921 (the bodies were not returned immediately) and had been marked by it.

RELATED MATERIALS
Last letter from André Van Eycke dated 20 09 1916. Official letter dated 16-10-1916 announcing his death. Photograph of André Van Eycke in military uniform (1913). Citation of André Van Eycke to the Order of the Army Corps.

title: FRAD080 - Les mémoires de guerre d' Emile Degand | Emile Degand's diary
text:
TITLE
FRAD080 - Les mémoires de guerre d' Emile Degand | Emile Degand's diary

Title: FRAD080 - Les mémoires de guerre d' Emile Degand | Emile Degand's diary

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Emile Degand was born on February 22, 1889 in Cambrai. He died in 1967. He tells us about "his war" in 6 handwritten notebooks that he bequeathed to his children. He was incorporated into the 37th Infantry Regiment in the 1909 class of Nancy. Mobilized in the 291st in Mézières on August 3, 1914, he took part in all the combats: the Ardennes, the Marne, Verdun... Wounded and cited for his bravery on several occasions, he was released from his service in May 1919. He was also Treasurer of the Union of disabled and reformed war veterans of Cambrésis. These notebooks go from August 3, 1914 to June 5, 1919.

RELATED MATERIALS
Excerpt from the first notebook of Emile Degand's memoirs.

title: FRAM - Histoire de Paul et Eugène ANGELIER | Documents from Paul ANGELIER
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Histoire de Paul et Eugène ANGELIER | Documents from Paul ANGELIER

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Histoire de Paul et Eugène ANGELIER | Documents from Paul ANGELIER
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1915, 1917
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Paul Angelier lived in Allevard (Isère) where he was a teacher. During the war, he wrote to his parents and to his brother Eugène, a teacher before the conflict. The contributor is Eugène's granddaughter who has kept several letters from her brother. The subject of the war was not discussed in front of her but she remembers that her uncle Paul was wounded in the Dardanelles. Paul writes to his brother Eugène, adjutant at the fodder park in Grenoble and to his parents. On March 21, 1915, while he was in Marseilles, Paul Angelier learned of his assignment to the Dardanelles expeditionary force from his parents and his brother: "I am in charge of the bakery. It will be a nice service." He will be part of the 156th division which has 18,000 men and will take care of the pay, the ordinary and the police. The departure took place on May 5, 1915, aboard the Natal; the landing takes place on May 12. Paul Angelier wrote to his brother on May 16 that after landing at Sedd-Ul-Bahr – “the shells were raining down hard (…) I had the pleasure of hearing them whistle and burst very close to me. Luckily none of us were hit! - they re-embark for the island of Lemnos. At Moudros, he was responsible for setting up a bakery and then from July was assigned to the framework of the section. In his letters, he describes the arrival of the many wounded on the island, the field hospital which is beginning to be organized, the sanitary conditions on the island but also the satisfaction of seeing his bakery well organized and of find “on an island where it is hot but where (he is) in perfect safety”.... On October 19, 1915, he is in the hospital, victim of dysentery. He was evacuated on November 13 aboard the hospital ship Bretagne and landed in Ferryville, at the Sidi-Abdallah hospital on November 18. He left the hospital on December 16 and was offered convalescent leave by the chief medical officer. In his letters written from France in 1916-1917, Paul Angelier describes to his family his work at the sub-intendancy (he becomes familiar with the use of the typewriter in February 1917, for example), the moments of freedom that he obtains his assignment (his wife Léonie visits him and they attend together "the parade of the Russians in the streets of Marseilles" in April 1916, he goes to see the wreckage of a zeppelin in March 1917) or his hope of being reformed. He also writes of his concern at seeing his parents become exhausted working in the fields in the absence of their sons (letter of May 26, 1916). The last letter in the series is dated December 24, 1917. Returning to civilian life, Paul Angelier finished his career as a teacher in Isère.

RELATED MATERIALS
1 – Letters from Paul Angelier to his brother Eugène, Moudros May 17 - October 27, 1915. 2 - Letter from Paul Angelier to his brother Eugène, Marseilles, April 29, 1916. 3 - Letter from Paul Angelier to his brother Eugène, Marseilles, May 26, 1916. 4 – Letter from Paul Angelier to his brother Eugène, Baugy, February 10, 1917. 5 - Card from Paul Angelier to his parents, March 19, 1917.

title: FRBNBU-080 Joseph Antoine Callet, photographe de l'escadrille des cigognes | Joseph Antoine Callet, photograh of "escadrille des cigognes"
text:
TITLE
FRBNBU-080 Joseph Antoine Callet, photographe de l'escadrille des cigognes | Joseph Antoine Callet, photograh of "escadrille des cigognes"

METADATA
Title: FRBNBU-080 Joseph Antoine Callet, photographe de l'escadrille des cigognes | Joseph Antoine Callet, photograh of "escadrille des cigognes"
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1916, 1918
Places: Cachy
Subjects: World War I, Transport
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Joseph Antoine Callet, grandfather of the contributor, Beatrice Godebert-Callet, was born in 1877 and died in 1922. Photographer by profession at 163 rue de Rennes in Paris, he was part of the Cigognes squadron, a famous squadron from 1914 to 1918. Joseph Antoine Callet was the squadron's photographer and, as such, took pictures of the squadron and its members during the war. Combat Group No. 12, also known as the Storks Squadron, is a famous aeronautical unit of the French army whose aircraft were adorned with a stork. It was the birthplace of great figures of French fighter aviation including pilots Fonck, Guynemer, Dorme, Heurteaux, Echard, Guiguet, Roland Garros, Nungesser, Tenant de la Tour and many others. The 12th Combat Squadron Group was formed in May 1916 with eight squadrons, then split in November into two groups of four squadrons each. The first included numbers 3, 26, 73 (which was to be replaced a few months later by 67) and 103. It was successively called Groupe de Chasse Brocard, Groupe de Chasse de Cachy (from the name of the neighboring locality of Amiens where he quartered). But it was to the choice of the stork as an insignia that he owed the happy name to which he was to conquer universal glory. Under the orders of Commander Brocard, GC 12 took part in operations in the Somme and Lorraine, in the offensives in Aisne and Flanders.

RELATED MATERIALS
- 14 photographs of the Cigognes squadron and its members, including Joseph Antoine Callet, Georges Guynemer, Charles Nungesser, and the squadron's mascot dog.

STORY / CONTEXT
Performing his military service in the 13th RI in Nevers, Faidherbe GAGET was 50 days from liberation when the First World War broke out. He is then a telephone operator. His diary covers the period from August 1 to October 10, 1914. Like many, he thinks the war will be short. He is however lucid and foresees that this conflict will be deadly in view of the armament of the time. He was then assigned to the 9th Engineer Regiment.

Record type: TEXT

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918

STORY / CONTEXT
Account by Mr. and Mrs. COUTAUDIER on Emile COQUELU, maternal grandfather of the contributor, conscript of the class of 1905 (office of Autun in Saône-et-Loire, registration number 140). Acquirer in 1914 of a printing works located 44 rue Carnot in Montceau-les-Mines, whose family activity will continue until 1992, Emile COQUELU continued, during the war, in spite of his responsibilities of corporal and manager of a team of stretcher-bearers, to rigorously manage the affairs of his company. Taken prisoner on July 15, 1918 in front of Saint-Hilaire-le-Grand, he was transferred to the camp of Langensalza (Saxony) in Germany. After nine days of travel by train and barge via the Netherlands, Emile COQUELU returned to France on January 11, 1919.

title: FRAD067-124 Jacques et Emile Humann, d'Entzheim (Bas-Rhin) : deux frères sous les armes (armée allemande) | Postcards from Jacques and Emile Humann
text:
TITLE
FRAD067-124 Jacques et Emile Humann, d'Entzheim (Bas-Rhin) : deux frères sous les armes (armée allemande) | Postcards from Jacques and Emile Humann

METADATA
Title: FRAD067-124 Jacques et Emile Humann, d'Entzheim (Bas-Rhin) : deux frères sous les armes (armée allemande) | Postcards from Jacques and Emile Humann
Record type: TEXT
Years: 1914, 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Deutsch, deu
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jacques Humann known as "Burjöckel" (1894-1978) was one of the three men behind the initiative, in 1921, of the First World War memorial in Entzheim (Bas-Rhin), renovated and repositioned in 2003. His granddaughter, Mrs. Yvonne Brevers, presents the "complete correspondence" - which proves after study to also contain letters from his brother Emile (1897-1983), also engaged in the war. Son of Daniel Humann and Marie Eve Bauer, Jacques Humann was mobilized from August 5, 1914 to December 4, 1918. On 10/19/1914 he was transferred from the Ersatz-Bataillon Fußiculaire-Regiment 18 to the 3. Batterie Fußiculaire-Bataillon 159, unit in which he will continue the whole war. His services are as follows: Battle of Lodz (26/11-15/12/1914); Battle of Rawka-Bzura (18/12/1914-16/07/1915); positional combat on the Greek border, on the Vardar river (Axios) (04/03-15/09/1916); fighting around the position of Jarabitzna (not loc.) (05/10/1917); breakthrough on the Macedonian front (09/16-09/21/1918). “Überzähliger Gefreiter” (27/01/1916) then “etatmäßiger Gefreiter” (24/12/1917), he was decorated with the Iron Cross (Eisernes Kreuz zweiter Klasse) on 17/12/1917. The career of his brother Emile, for lack of military papers or a veteran's file kept in his name at the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin, is more difficult to trace. Preserved postcards indicate that he belonged to the 2 Garde Reserve Regiment, 11 Kompagnie 3 Bataillon, visibly stationed in Belgium or in the North of France (cards sent by him on 30/10/1916, 23/03/1917 and 21 /01/1918 representing respectively the Théâtre Royal de la Monnaie in Brussels, the portal of the cathedral of Tournai and the church of Carvin).

RELATED MATERIALS
Military papers of Jacques Humann (Soldbuch, Entlassungsschein, Militärdienstzeitbescheinigung, 3 veteran cards), 1 photograph, 50 postcards addressed by Jacques Humann to his father Daniel Humann (1914-1918), 8 postcards received by Jacques Humann (1914 -1918), 13 undated unwritten photo cards (mainly Macedonia), 5 cards sent by Emilie Humann to her father Daniel Humann, 8 cards received by Emile Humann (including 3 cards from his brother Jacques). Among the documents on loan, only a notebook was not digitized (Kriegserlebnisse, 05/08/1914-25/03/1916).

title: FRAM - Eugène BERTRAND (1876-1918) | Eugène BERTRAND
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Eugène BERTRAND (1876-1918) | Eugène BERTRAND

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Eugène BERTRAND (1876-1918) | Eugène BERTRAND
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I, Propaganda
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
As an officer, my great-grandfather Eugène Bertrand spent his entire career in the Army of Africa, first in the Zouaves regiment then in the Infantry and Intelligence. He notably worked in General Lyautey's staff. He was made a Knight of the Legion of Honor on July 10, 1913. Captain of the regiment of Moroccan Tirailleurs based in Casablanca, he remained in Morocco until May 25, 1915, before joining the French front. Reims in June-July 1916, Chemin des Dames in April 1917, Lorraine in September 1917. He was appointed Battalion Commander on May 11, 1917. Wounded on July 18, 1918 near Soissons (Aisne), he died four days later at Pierrefons. He is then a commander.

RELATED MATERIALS
War album of photographs taken between 1916 and 1917 on the battalion fields, before and after the attacks but also on leave (1916-1917). State of services (1920).

Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Jean (François, André) Boudou was born in Cluny in 1888. An orphan, he was raised by cousins in the Rodez region. He then belonged to the 81st Infantry Regiment of the 1908 class. He married at Drucourt, in the Eure, on July 25, 1914. He was called up on August 3, 1914. He entered the 4th Engineers of Grenoble as a 2nd class soldier. . The flu saves his life. While he was transferred to the depot for cripples in the castle of Mercastel (Oise), he learned that part of his squad had been killed during his hospitalization. He joined his army corps on September 28, 1918. According to his family, Jean Boudou never recovered from the loss of his companions, a loss he experienced as an injustice throughout his life. After demobilization, Jean Boudou joined his wife in Normandy in December 1918.

RELATED MATERIALS
An extract from a medical book (1918), an anti-typhoid vaccination card (1917), a photo taken with his wife before he left for the war (1914).

title: FRAM - Histoire de Firmin, Marie-Louise, Emile et Cécile ALLAMAND | Documents from ALLAMAND family
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Histoire de Firmin, Marie-Louise, Emile et Cécile ALLAMAND | Documents from ALLAMAND family

METADATA
Title: FRAM - Histoire de Firmin, Marie-Louise, Emile et Cécile ALLAMAND | Documents from ALLAMAND family
Record type: TEXT
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Isidore Firmin, known as Firmin, Allamand was born on June 27, 1882 in Séez. He is the son of Victor Allamand, farmer and Marie Julie Eugénie David, housewife. He was successively a teacher in Bourg-Saint-Maurice, Notre-Dame-du-Pré then Bellentre from 1907. On September 22, 1913, he married Marie-Louise Miège, teacher, born in Tournon on February 13, 1891. He has two children, Emile, born August 15, 1907 from his union with Aurélie Collombet (deceased August 15, 1907) and Cécile, born July 1, 1914 from his union with Marie-Louise Miège. The contributor is Cécile's son who, after his mother, kept all the documents and letters exchanged between his grandparents but also friends and other family members between 1914 and 1916. If Cécile Allamand spoke little about her father with her own children, she read her letters with her own daughter, and her son read the entire correspondence a few years ago. The correspondence between the two spouses begins with the general mobilization, on August 2, 1914. Firmin Allamand is mobilized as a non-commissioned officer in the 22nd battalion of alpine hunters, barracks in Albertville. He was first in charge of training recruits in Albertville, then Barbières and Bollène (Drôme). He completed an internship at Valbonne (Ain) and became a gunnery sergeant of the 22nd Chasseurs, 12th Company. From Modane, he reached the Vosges at the end of June 1915. The two spouses corresponded extensively. In her letters, Marie-Louise Allamand evokes her daily life, her work as a teacher, the life of the children and in particular Cécile's progress... As she wrote to her husband, "we live". Firmin Allamand describes to his wife his life as an instructor at first, then from the end of June 1915, the front, the rain, the dead but above all, he seeks to participate in family life through the short stories written by his wife. His son Emile sometimes takes up the pen and tells him about his life as a child. Firmin Allamand died near Maurepas, in the Somme, on August 24, 1916 "as a result of shrapnel wounds". The last letters are sent by the wife, unaware of the death of her husband. Bearing the mention "THE RECIPIENT COULD NOT BE REACHED", they were not opened by the family.

RELATED MATERIALS
1 – Letter and card from Firmin Allamand to Marie-Louise Allamand, Albertville, August 2, 1914. 2 – Letter from Emile Allamand to Firmin Allamand, Bellentre, July 12, 1915. 3 – Family portrait taken June 14, 1916 at the favor of permission: Firmin, Marie-Louise, Emile and Cécile Allamand. 4 – Double letter, from Marie-Louise Allamand to her husband, Bellentre on August 19, 1916 and in return from Firmin Allamand to his wife, on August 23, 1916. He slips a piece of newspaper to indicate his position. He is killed the next day. 5 – Last letters from Marie-Louise Allamand to Firmin Allamand.

Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Sergeant Marius Ancel, of the 170th infantry regiment, was born in 1896 in Golbey, in the Vosges. He worked as a game hunter. He was a very brave soldier, as evidenced by his many citations and decorations. The contributor, his grandson, remembers in particular that he saved his lieutenant. He also told her that he killed "his first German" with a bayonet. He began to cry shortly after because, having looked in his papers, he had noticed that he was the father of a family, and he himself was an orphan. When he retired, he came to live with his daughter in Revin (Ardennes).

Emmanuel Guégain, gunner-driver in the 25th field artillery regiment, was mortally wounded during an ammunition supply and died on September 18, 1918. He is buried in the district of Vesoul (Haute-Saône). The contributor, his grand-niece, did not know him but his grandmother talked a lot about him.

RELATED MATERIALS
Drawing number to the review board, letter to his parents, letter announcing his death.

STORY / CONTEXT
Enlisted on July 24, 1914 during his military service as an aide-de-camp assigned to the care of horses in Belgium, then a stretcher bearer in the 32nd Dragoons regiment until the end of the war, my maternal grandfather Fernand Greteau was quoted at the order of his regiment in September 1914 and August 1918 for his courage and bravery. He recorded his daily memories in a series of notebooks during "his four years of nightmare" which I will publish.

RELATED MATERIALS
1/ Postcard "In the Argonne" double-sided 2/ Mentions to the order of the regiment 3/ Postcard of the 32nd Dragoons double-sided

Places: Epinal
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Mr. Van Dijen, as part of his professional activity, discovered this "kit book" during the demolition of a middle-class house in Gouvray-sur-Marne (Seine-Saint-Denis) in the 1990s. It is the diary of a Parisian doctor, whose name is unknown, who was mobilized at the start of the war and confined to the Epinal field hospital (Vosges). From July to December 1914, he recounts day by day, the course of his day (his dreams, the weather, his meals, his guards ...), the most significant international events and his daily life as a war doctor seeing an influx of more and more injuries. He counts them and on September 13, 1914, already counts "46,259 wounded, sick, crippled seen and supplied at Épinal station until this day..."

title: FRAM - Emile Maurice PORTET (1879-1947) | Emile PORTET
text:
TITLE
FRAM - Emile Maurice PORTET (1879-1947) | Emile PORTET

METADATA

Record type: TEXT
Years: 1918
Subjects: World War I
Original item language: fra, Français
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Coming from a military family, Émile Maurice Portet, was mobilized with the class of 1896 on August 2, 1914, he was then assigned to the 87th Territorial, 5th Company in Brest. He joined Seine-et-Marne on August 24 and did not leave the front until August 20, 1917, when he was wounded. On November 12, 1915, he transferred to the 276th Reserve Regiment of the Territorial, 2nd Battalion, 6th Co. In June 1916, he responded to the request for non-commissioned officers in the 3rd Army. He joined the Oise to follow the courses until August 19, 1916, when he was appointed second lieutenant in the 16th regiment of Infantry Company of Machine gunners. He will take part in the battles of the Aisne, the Somme, the Oise and the Meuse. Seriously injured in August 1917 by shrapnel, he lost the use of one hand. He will then pass through the hospitals of Tours, Montpellier and the American Hospital of Neuilly for six months. He was made a Knight of the Legion of Honor in October 1917. He was also appointed lieutenant on August 18, 1918. Throughout the war, he kept a war diary in which he recounted day by day where he was, the battles but also the living conditions and daily life of a soldier.

RELATED MATERIALS
Extract from Emile Portet's military booklet. Extracts from the war diary kept from 1914 to 1918. Photo-postcard of the officers of the 5th Company of the 87th RI, in November 1914. Photographs on the battlefields [1916]. Portait d'Emile Portet, in his officer's uniform, post-war. Mention in the Order of the Legion of Honor (1916).

Original item language: Français, fra
Provider: Europeana 1914-1918
Rights: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/

STORY / CONTEXT
Émile Fristo, the contributor's great-grandfather was born in 1884 in Jarville in Meurthe-et-Moselle. Farrier, he lives in Bouxières-aux-Dames. He is married to Marie Gilain with whom he has a son named Marcel. He did his military service in the 12th Dragoon Regiment. Mobilized, he fought at the front. He will be injured, which earned him a stay in the Châteauroux hospital. On March 23, 1915, Antoine Gilain, Marie's brother, residing in Jarville, wrote to his brother-in-law. He recounts the attacks of German planes in the vicinity of Nancy, the firing of anti-aircraft batteries, as well as the passage of French airships. Émile finds his family at the end of the war.

RELATED MATERIALS
1. Postcard written to Émile Fristo by Antoine Gilain his brother-in-law (March 1915). 2. Postcard written by Émile Fristo (3rd from left) to his wife (nd). 3. Postcard representing Émile Fristo, then in care at the Châteauroux hospital (nd).

STORY / CONTEXT
The contributor's grandfather, Joseph Marie Horel, a farmer, was born on May 18, 1897 in Cléguer (Morbihan). Although adjourned in 1915 for "weakness", he was recognized as fit for auxiliary service by the Morbihan review board on May 16, 1916. He was incorporated as a 2nd class horseman in the 2nd Chasseurs Regiment horse, based in Pontivy.
